div.head: DataFrame as matrix and operations
div.p: Arthur Cayley worked on matrices and introduced operations (Cayley, 1858).

div.head: Matrix and vector multiplication
div.p: We createfoot_6 a vector with its index as the matrix column names and we can then perform a matrix multiplicationfoot_7 We might want to multiply element by element, this is also called Hadamard product, that we write here ⊙.
First we duplicate the column to form a square matrix and multiply the two matrices:
4 6 2 4 6 2 4 6   =   2 4 12 6 16 30 14 32 54   11.3.3 Singular and non singular matrices
A matrix is fundamental to many advanced concepts in mathematics and econometrics.
• Square Matrix: A matrix is called "square" if it has the same number of rows and columns. So, if a matrix has dimensions of 3×3, 4×4, and so on, it's a square matrix 16 .

• Identity Matrix: Imagine a matrix filled entirely with zeros, but its main diagonal (from the top-left corner to the bottom-right) is filled with ones. This special matrix is called the "identity matrix", denoted by I. It acts like the number 1 in regular arithmetic for matrices.
• Non-singular Matrix: We say a square matrix ∆ is "non-singular" if we can find another matrix, ∆ -1 , such that when we multiply them, the result is the identity matrix: ∆ * ∆ -1 = I.

Another way to determine if a matrix is non-singular is to look at its rows (or columns). If all rows (or columns) of an n × n matrix are linearly independent -meaning no row (or column) can be expressed as a combination of the others -then the matrix is non-singular. This is also referred to as the matrix having "full rank". A quick check for this is the determinant: non-singular matrices have a determinant that is not zero.
In the realm of econometrics, matrices play a vital role in various models, including AR(p) and VAR models.

div.head: Eigenvalues, eigenvectors, and shock propagation
div.p: When analyzing the stability of a linear propagation process, it is useful to study the eigenvalues and eigenvectors of the underlying matrix.
An eigenvector v of a matrix ∆ is a non-zero vector such that
div.formula: ∆v = λv,
div.p: where λ is the associated eigenvalue. Intuitively, multiplying ∆ by v only stretches or shrinks v, without changing its direction. This matters because the long-run behavior of repeated applications of ∆ depends on its eigenvalues. If all eigenvalues are strictly smaller than one in absolute value, repeated multiplications of ∆ dampen shocks. If at least one eigenvalue exceeds one, shocks are amplified and the system becomes unstable.

Application to financial networks: DebtRank The matrix ∆ summarizes how distress propagates from one bank to another. Bardoscia et al. (2015) model contagion by transmitting only the newly incurred distress at each step. The update rule is

Here h i (t) represents the relative equity loss of bank i at time t. When bank j suffers an additional equity loss, the counterparties i that are exposed to j must reduce the value of their interbank assets accordingly. This is captured by the matrix ∆, which scales the shock according to the size of exposures relative to each bank's equity base. In this way, distress is linked directly to a deterioration of capital positions and thus to the valuation of exposures. Each bank's distress is capped at h i (t) ≤ 1, reflecting the fact that no institution can lose more than 100% of its equity. Suppose each bank is hit at time t = 1 by an initial shock h(1) = 0.5%. At this stage, the losses are simply h(1). At t = 2, the first round of contagion takes place: the initial shock is transmitted once through the network, giving an additional contribution ∆h (1). The cumulative distress is then (1).
At t = 3, the second round of contagion occurs: the shock passes along paths of length two, adding ∆ 2 h(1). The cumulative distress is
Continuing in this way, after t steps we have
We have
For scalars you know that
The matrix version is analogous:
div.formula: S t = (I -∆ t )(I -∆) -1 , provided that I -∆ is invertible.
div.p: If all eigenvalues of ∆ are strictly smaller than one in modulus, then the infinite sum converges and the long-run impact of the shock is finite:
with I the identity matrix. If instead at least one eigenvalue exceeds one, the sum diverges and the shock is amplified without bound.

div.head: Cholesky decomposition
div.p: Cholesky decomposition is a powerful mathematical tool often used in econometrics.
• Real Matrix: A matrix, A, is described as "real" when all of its elements are real numbers.
• Symmetric Matrix: A matrix, A, is symmetric if it remains unchanged upon transposition. In other words, the matrix and its transpose are the same: A = A T , where T denotes the transpose operator.
• Positive Matrix: A matrix, A, is positive if all of its elements are non-negative (equal to or greater than zero).

• Real Positive-Definite Matrix: A matrix is considered positive-definite if, for any nonzero real column vector z, the value produced by z T Az is positive. This can be formally stated as: ∀z ∈ R n \ 0 : z T Az > 0.

div.head: Cholesky decomposition:
div.p: For a matrix A that is a real positive-definite, the Cholesky decomposition breaks it down into two components. It represents A as the product of a lower triangular matrix, L, and its transpose, L T . So, the decomposition can be represented as: A = LL T What's striking about this is that the matrix L is unique, with real and positive diagonal entries. This uniqueness is paramount, especially when dealing with SVARs in econometrics, as it ensures consistent and unambiguous results.

div.head: Creating multivariate distributed rvs with the Cholesky decomposition
div.p: As a reminder of matrix and random variables: if X is a collection of rvs such that its mean is µ and its variance-covariance matrix is V ar(X) = Σ. Now if we take a matrix composed of constant elements U and define a new random variable vector
div.formula: Y = U X, then E(Y ) = U µ and V ar(Y ) = U V ar(X)U T = U ΣU T .
div.p: A Cholesky decomposition can be used useful to generate multivariate normally distributed random vectors. If Z = [Z 1 , . . . , Z N ] T is a vector where ∀i, Z i ∼ N (0, 1) and are independent,
div.formula: then if U is a (N × N ) matrix, U T Z ∼ M N (0, U T U ) (17
div.p: If C is the covariance matrix we are looking to introduce, then it is a real symmetric positivedefinite matrix and its Cholesky decomposition is C = U T U .

If we follow the spirit of Kousky and Cooke (2012) and the lecture of Martin Haugh. I generate 3 iid rvs Z from a standard normal law, with samples of size 10 5 . Then I want to make those rvs dependent with a covariance matrix:
1.38 0.05 0.05 0.05 1.38 0.05 0.05 0.05 1.38   Then I extract U T from the Cholesky decomposition of C and multiply U T Z

div.head: Computational Complexity
div.p: Computational complexity 17 concerns the resources required to solve computational problems, primarily time and space. The theory classifies algorithms according to their performance in relation to the input size. Let us denote the input size by n.
To begin, let us define the Big O notation. For a given function g(n), we say that
div.formula: f (n) = O(g(n))
div.p: if there are positive constants c and n 0 such that 0 ≤ f (n) ≤ c•g(n) for all n ≥ n 0 . This notation helps us describe an upper limit on the growth rate of the runtime or space requirements of an algorithm. Consider a simple example: an algorithm that checks each pair in an array to find duplicates. This algorithm would have a time complexity of O(n 2 ) because, in the worst case, it performs a comparison for each pair of elements in the array. P (Polynomial Time): This class contains problems that can be solved by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. An example is matrix multiplication, which can be solved in time O(n 3 ) using the standard algorithm, though faster algorithms exist.

div.head: Matrix Multiplication
div.p: Consider two 2 × 2 matrices A and B defined as follows: Each element of matrix C is the sum of products of elements from a row of A and a column of B.
div.formula: A = a 11
div.p: For a general n×n matrix, this calculation involves n multiplications and n-1 additions for each of the n 2 entries, resulting in a total of n 3 operations, assuming the addition and multiplication have a constant time complexity. Hence, the naive matrix multiplication complexity is O(n 3 ).

div.head: Strassen Algorithm for Matrix Multiplication
div.p: The Strassen algorithm, introduced in Strassen (1969), reduces the computational complexity of matrix multiplication. This algorithm is particularly effective for large matrices and is based on a divide-and-conquer approach that reduces the number of recursive multiplications. Consider the same 2 × 2 matrices A and B, instead of performing eight multiplications as in the naive approach, the Strassen algorithm performs only seven multiplications by introducing intermediate products:
These intermediate products are used to compute the entries of the product matrix C as follows:

By reducing the number of multiplications, the Strassen algorithm improves the overall time complexity. For general n × n matrices, the algorithm recursively applies itself to smaller matrices, leading to a complexity of O(n log 2 7 ) ≈ O(n 2.807 ). This improvement is significant, particularly for very large matrices. This topic is still improved, as in Duan et al. (2023) and Williams et al. (2024).

div.head: Resample our DataFrame (from monthly to yearly observations)
div.p: There is the possibility to resample our DataFrame from one frequency to another using the resample() function. We need to indicate the desired frequency described in the Offset Aliases section of the time series documentation available here. We list some of the more commonly used:
• H hourly frequency
• D calendar day frequency • W weekly frequency • Q quarter end frequency • A year end frequency

The we need to chose a "method" for resampling, for example .sum() or .mean() In our case, if we want to move to yearly frequency and get the average over the 12 months. If we plot the obtained DataFrame, we see that the plot is slightly smoother.

div.p: Now you can plot and compute monthly changes.

1994 1999 2004 2009 2014 0.00 0.05 0.10 0.15 0.20 0.25 0.30 0.35 Monthly change of French population in percent 12.7 Descriptive statistics You can get some descriptive statistics on the population and the monthly population changes: Population in million Population growth rate in percentage count 273 272 mean 62.78 0.05 std 2.44 0.02 min 59.07 0.00 max 66.79 0.37 12.8 Exercise with import, DataFrame and matrices: Leontief Input-Output model Wassily Leontief is widely known for developing the Input-Output model of the economy. His foundational work on the subject began with Leontief (1949). For a comprehensive collection of his major papers on this model, one can refer to the compilation presented in the book Leontief (1986).

The essence of the model lies in its sectoral division of the economy. Every sector is dependent on others for its input to produce its output. Thus, the production of a sector isn't just shaped by external demand (from end consumers) but also by internal demand (from other sectors).
To crystallize this concept using matrix notation, let's define the following:
• P : A vector representing the production of each of the N sectors, denoted as [p 1 , . . . , p N ].
• D: A vector representing the external demand for each sector, presented as [d 1 , . . . , d N ].
• M : A matrix showcasing the internal demand, where each element m i,j illustrates the demand by sector i for the production from sector j.
With these definitions in place, the relationship between internal and external demands in determining sectoral production is given by:
div.formula: P = M P + D(18)
div.p: Rearranging the above equation, we get:

Here, I represents the identity matrix. If the matrix (I -M ) is invertible, we can confidently assert that our equation has a unique solution equation 20. On the contrary, if it's non-invertible, the model becomes indeterminate, meaning there could be no solution or potentially infinite solutions. 20) 12.8.

1 Simplified example Let's imagine we have the simplified economy: Agriculture Manufacturing Services External demand, D Agriculture 35 5 5 40 Manufacturing 5 60 20 60 Services 10 25 40 130 Total gross ouput 85 160 220 We use the consumption matrix M after dividing by the Total gross output. We can now estimate following equation 20Now, if there is a one unit increase in the demand for agriculture output, this should have a positive impact of .2 on the manufacturing production. This is a step-by-step way of doing it 21 Actually, the most difficult part is to divide each column of a matrix by a given value from a vector, three approaches are suggested (as seen during class):

1. Create Mgross as the matrix before division by gross output 2. Create D the demand vector • Here notice that the name you give to index and columns are important when you will want to perform matrix multiplication (e.g. agriculture should match agriculture). -Compute P -Now, imagine the external demand for Oilseed farming (1111A0) increases by one unit. What is the impact on the production for the Oil and gas extraction (211000)?

div.head: Create the

13.9 Web scraping with python, a use case with IFP

We do not intend to provide here a full lecture on web scraping with python. We provide one use case: getting a list of Fintech that are IFP from ORIAS website. Lending in France is regulated by law under what is known as monopole bancaire 24 and is regulated at the European level by the CRDIV 25 on taking deposits or other repayable funds and by the CRR 26 for granting credits for its own account. This regulation is a barrier to entry into the market but has been relaxed by the introduction of new categories of actors in France in 2014 27 . For the French market, the ACPR 28 has the authority to deliver accreditation 29 to intermediaries in participatory financing (IFP). ORIAS provides information on the IFP but to gather the information, one has to research and select the data. We suggest to use python libraries for web scraping to feed a data frame:

14 Data Assurance Quality

In the domain of Machine Learning, as well as in econometrics, data assurance quality-encompassing activities like data cleaning, preparation, and organization-typically constitutes a staggering 80% of the overall workload. Beyond just the effort involved, ensuring data quality is pivotal. It not only enhances the accuracy and reliability of the model but also furnishes insights into the inherent strengths and potential vulnerabilities of the dataset.

div.head: Winsorizing
div.p: Ottonello and Winberry (2020) works on a panel of firms:
We winsorize our sample at the top and bottom 0.5% of observations of investment, leverage, and distance to default in order to ensure our results are not driven by outliers.

div.head: Case Study: US Adult Census Data
div.p: To elucidate this crucial aspect, wefoot_14 turn our focus to the US Adult Census dataset, which correlates income with sociodemographic attributes such as age, education, race, and other pertinent features. This dataset is grounded in the seminal work of (Kohavi, 1996). For those seeking to delve deeper, the data set can be accessed here.

div.head: Initial Data Exploration
div.p: An indispensable starting point is to familiarize oneself with the dataset and promptly identify any missing values:

Feature Missing values count age 0 workclass 1836 fnlwgt 0 education 0 education_num 0 marital_status 0 occupation 1843 relationship 0 race 0 gender 0 capital_gain 0 capital_loss 0 hours_per_week 0 native_country 583 income_bracket 0

A pertinent question arises: are the NaNs indicative of non-working individuals, or are they the result of data collection errors? If it's the latter, the optimal course of action might be to discard the affected rows.

Upon examining the 'native_country' feature, an anomaly surfaces-a country labeled "South". A detailed inspection, especially when cross-referenced with the 'race' attribute, strongly suggests that this likely corresponds predominantly to South Korea.

div.head: Feature Engineering
div.p: Feature engineering can serve as a potent tool to enhance the data's utility. For instance:
• Combining 'capital_gain' and 'capital_loss' into a singular metric might offer a holistic perspective on capital dynamics.
• Categorizing 'marital_status' to distinguish between individuals living solo versus those in relationships might furnish richer sociodemographic insights.

div.head: Data Analysis Tasks
div.p: We 31 did some preliminary work as a starting point.
• Question 14 -Engage in the following analyses:
1. Deploy K-means and Hierarchical clustering methodologies.
2. Construct and interpret a Dendrogram, subsequently demarcating an appropriate threshold.
3. Implement a logit model. Subsequently, leverage Lasso regression to ascertain if overfitting occurs.
4. Utilize the Random Forest algorithm.

div.head: Experiment with Boosting techniques.
div.p: 31 vansteenberghe_ML_wage.py 15 Python: when to use parametric econometrics and not other (more flexible) machine learning methods?

Section 39 we explored in great details a parametric model m, linear and at best quadratic for a variable of interest (y: log of wage) using explanatory variables also called features (X: gender, education, experience) in y = m(X) + ϵ. We might wonder why not to use machine learning for our regression problem.
We suggest an answer following (Charpentier et al., 2018), considering that econometrics (statistics)
has developed around the principle of inference (or to explain the relationship linking y to variables x)

, to make inference, we are making assumptions about the data generating process (cf. section 46). If we had to distinguish machine learning from econometrics, we would say that machine learning is focused on predictive power and does not make any assumption on the data generating process.
In both econometrics and machine learning, having data sets [y i , X i ] the goal is to "search" for a model that minimizes a loss function:
div.formula: min m L (y i , m(X i ))(21)
div.p: under some constraint on m. Wefoot_15 present some machine learning technicsfoot_16 applied to some data set with the python code.

div.head: Econometric models transparency
div.p: In the words of Andrews et al. (2017):

Linear regression analysis is popular in part because it is transparent. Estimates depend on a set of intuitive variances and covariances, and it is straightforward to assess how these moments would change under violations of the identifying assumptions. Well-understood properties of linear models-most prominently, the omitted variables bias formula-make it easy for readers to guess how these changes translate into bias in the estimates. We do not need to have access to the data to know that a regression of wages on education would be biased upward by omitted skill, and we can form a guess about how much if we have a prior on the likely covariance properties of the omitted variable.
and their work expand this to nonlinear econometric models. Such transparency is not straightforward for machine leaning models.

15.2 Before we introduce machine learning models -False positive concept and why Tour de France bikers might be cheating Some French reporter investigated the testing methods used by the Union Cycliste Internationale (UCI) to test whether there were hidden motors in bicycles before a race. The reporter questioned the efficacy of the methods used... Could it be that there are engines used during the Tour de France?
The UCI replied with a published note. What is very interesting, is that reading this written reply, I started to have strong doubt... The UCI methods could be inefficient.
The UCI is quoting a report performed by a laboratory in the United States of America, the Microbac report, available here, under the code name UCI17D0410_rp1_Neutral.pdf.
What is quite disturbing is that the report is actually what led me to doubt the methods at UCI:

When the detection tool is used on a bike, at 10 millimeters from the bike frame, there are 7 instance of "10" out of 4 × 4 = 16 tests. A "10" means that you have a strong signal, a positive. Out of those positive, 4 where bike with actual engines (in yellow)... but 3 of those "10" had no engine inside.
In other terms, testing the bikes, the Microbac found 3 false positive out of 16 tests, that is a ratio of 3 16 .

If you read carefully the UCI answer, they performed "over 4,000 controls undertaken during the 2017 Tour de France", this means that -if the Microbac tests have any meaning-the UCI testers have found an expected 750 positive signals (all false positive, as no scandal arose from a true positive engine in a bike during the Tour de France 2017).

The question I would have is: what did the UCI do of those close to 1,000 positive alarms during the 2017 Tour de France? Did they take the time to perform thorough testing (computer tomography)... or just let the biker race? 34

div.head: What we have learned
div.p: A model becomes inefficient if it yields a disproportionately high ratio of False Positives. In such scenarios, there might be a need to manually differentiate between True and False Positives.
For supervisory entities:

• Transparency is essential in your historical actions but not necessarily in your testing protocols: questions like who are your suppliers and what unforeseen and thorough tests you've conducted in the past are relevant.

34 I actually sent an e-mail to the UCI team, they answered along the lines that using the test device isn't just as simple as seeing a 10 on the screen and assuming it is a positive. The size and shape of the reading on the test device screen also needs to be interpreted which is why they deliver 3-4 hours of training to each operator. They claimed that they would not get many false positives because there is more to using the device than just seeing the number on the screen. They would occasionally get a positive reading that appears to be false and dismantle the bikes but they replied that it is very rare and not statistically significant.
• Testing protocols should undergo unannounced changes. Relying on the same tools and methods consistently could allow those under supervision to game the system.

• It's not always prudent to engage in expensive contracts with American firms to "validate" your practices. Numerous reporters and economists are available and willing to fortify your methods at no charge.

A relevant case to consider is the Dieselgate scandal. This matter is of high importance as it pertains directly to public health. Were testing protocols for this consistently the same? Did it never occur to testers to sporadically test vehicles on roads? Was their incentive structure built around identifying dishonest auto manufacturers? Did organizations like the UCI genuinely have an interest in identifying deceitful racers, even if it meant another public scandal?
These incentive and testing protocol inquiries are equally relevant for financial supervisors. Additionally, testing protocols should be objective and not constructed based on a desired result.

One certainty is that when using train services, the companies appear motivated to identify and penalize fare evaders. They maintain secrecy about their checking methods, including the frequency, methodology, and locations of inspections.

div.head: Confusion matrices and metrics
div.p: We will first train our model on the Train data set, then use the model to predict the default Y on the Test data set. We want that our model predict correctly default and non-default: Y pred = Y , we define true positive, true negative, false positive and false negative as follow:

div.head: Confusion matrix
div.p: Predicted 0 Predicted 1 Actual 0 TN FP Actual 1 FN TP From the confusion matrix, we can define several metrics, here we focus on three:

div.head: False Positive Rate
div.p: False Positive Rate: F RP = F P T otal

div.head: Classification Accuracy:
div.p: Classification Accuracy: Overall, how often is the classifier correct? CA = T N +T P T otal

div.head: Sensitivity
div.p: Sensitivity: When the actual value is positive, how often is the prediction correct? S = T P F N +T P

div.head: AUC, ROC, and F1 score
div.p: With our model, we are attributing two probabilities, one for each state : default (0) and non-default (1). For illustration, if with our model we have Proba(0) > Proba(1) but Y = 1, then our model doesn't predict a default while in fact a default was observed, it is a false negative.

The Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve (Hanley and McNeil, 1982;Fawcett, 2006) is a graphical representation used to evaluate the performance of a binary classifier system as its discrimination threshold is varied. Specifically, the ROC curve plots the True Positive Rate (sensitivity) against the False Positive Rate (1-specificity) for different threshold values. It serves as a tool to illustrate the trade-offs between sensitivity and specificity for a given classifier. We also define:
div.formula: • Precision P = T P T P +F P • Recall R = T P T P +F N • F1 score 2 × P R P +R

div.head: Choosing the right estimator
div.p: The scikit learn community provides a comprehensive guide to machine learning techniques. The first question one would have when faced with a problem is the way to choose the estimator. They provide a flow chart on their website. Scikit-learn also provide a user guide that is quite exhaustive. We will mainly work on the data set introduced section 78.2 and as in the paper (Yeh and Lien, 2009), we want to test the following data mining techniques:
• K-nearest neighbor classifiers (KNN)
• Logistic regression (LR)
• Discriminant analysis (DA) -we use linear

• Naïve Bayesian classifier (NB) -we use Gaussian • Artificial neural networks (ANNs) -we use Multi-layer Perceptron classifier • Classification trees (CTs) -we use Decision Tree Classifier We add to that list: • Gradient Boosting Classifier • Random Forest Classifier 15.6 Naive Bayes Classifiers Wefoot_17 use the data set from Schooling, Experience and Earnings of Mincer 1974 work. We find a sample with 534 observations. div.head: Determining the Sex of Observation 6
div.p: To determine the sex of observation 6, let's denote the sex as S, where S = 1 stands for male and S = 0 for female. The variables W , A, and E represent Wage, Age, and Education, respectively.

div.head: Formulating the Question
div.p: Our primary objective is to determine if observation 6 is male. Formally, this can be expressed as:

div.head: Using Bayes' Theorem
div.p: To determine the above probabilities, we can use Bayes' theorem, which states:
Here:
• P (S) is the prior probability of the sex being male or female.
• P ((W, A, E) | S) is the likelihood of observing the given wage, age, and education given the sex.
• P (W, A, E) is the overall probability of observing the given wage, age, and education.
From our sample, we know that: P (S) = 54%

div.head: Dealing with the Curse of Dimensionality
div.p: If we discretize Wage, Age, and Education into x segments, we then create a 3-dimensional array which results in 10 x values. This expansion of values due to multiple dimensions is often termed as the 'curse of dimensionality'.

div.head: Calculating Conditional Probabilities
div.p: The conditional probability of observing W , A, and E given the sex can be represented as:
However, to simplify calculations and reduce the complexity, we can make the naive assumption that wage, age, and education are conditionally independent given the sex. Therefore:
div.formula: P ((W, A, E) | S) = P (W | S) × P (A | S) × P (E | S)
div.p: With this assumption, we are left to estimate only 3 × x values. Nota bene: In equation 22, we don't need to compute the denominator. It serves as a normalizing constraint to ensure that the probabilities of being male or female sum to one, given that we consider only binary categories for individuals.

div.head: Navie Bayes Classifier -estimation
div.p: We make the assumptions that Wage, Age, and Education are normally distributed, once we have the mean and the standard deviation, then the probability can be simply derived using:
We obtain the following confusion matrix: Predicted 0 Predicted 1 Actual 0 126 119 Actual 1 94 195 Which yields a F1 score of 0.65, we tend to accept this rather simple model.

div.head: K-nearest neighbors
div.p: KNN is a "lazy" learner, meaning that it doesn't learn a discriminative function but rather memorize the training data set. The KNN algorithm follows the following logist and steps: In this illustrative example, k = 6 and we use the Euclidean distance (assuming a two dimension problem). The value of x will be 2, by majority.

div.head: K-nearest neighbors -illustration
div.p: If wefoot_18 use again Mincer's 1974 work.
We take a random sample of 21 elements from the data set. We change the last observation SEX value to 99 and we want to predict its value (0 or 1) using a 5-nearest neighbors method.
We then compute the Euclidean distance between this point (99) and the rest of that sample.
We then count the SEX of the 5 nearest neighbors and decide on the SEX of our point based on majority vote. In a two dimensions example the Euclidean distance is simply:

div.head: Decision Trees
div.p: If In our case, we have limited the depth of our tree to 3. This divide our 2-Dimension space as follow:

div.head: WAGE
div.p: In practice, we are using a binary tree, where each parent has two children. We can in this case simply define the entropy as:
div.formula: Ent = -p(l) log 2 p(l) -p(r) log 2 p(r)
div.p: where p(l) is the proportion of the parent node that belong to the left node, p(r) to the right. The algorithm maximize this entropy, which in our case can be seen as information gain.

div.head: Random Forest
div.p: Random Forest is an ensemble of decision trees which algorithm (as described in (Raschka and Mirjalili, 2019):
1. Draw a random bootstrap sample of size n (randomly choose n samples from the training set with replacement).
2. Grow a decision tree from the bootstrap sample. At each node: a) Randomly select d features without replacement.
b) Split the node using the feature that provides the best split according to the objective function, for instance, by maximizing the information gain.
3. Repeat the steps 1 to 2 k times.
4. Aggregate the prediction by each tree to assign the class label by majority vote.
15.11 Before neural network, the "traditional" Ordinary Least Squares for linear regressions
If we take the usual linear regression with no constant, we are searching for the weights W =
div.formula: w 1 i , i ∈ [0, 2]. The variables X 1 = X 1 i , i ∈ [0, 2] are called features.
div.p: We have observations of X 2 0 and we want to estimate them from the features
div.formula: X 1 i , i ∈ [0, 2], that is finding X2 0 = W ′ X 1 . Input layer X 0 1 Output layer X 1 1 X 2 1 X 0 2 w 0 1 w 1 1 w 2 1
div.p: To estimate the weights, that is finding ŵ1 i , i ∈ [0, 2], the Ordinary Least Squares method. Our programme is:

With first order conditions, we can find the estimate of w 1 i , i ∈ [0, 2] and we could prove that it is linear, unbiased, and efficient. For simplicity, if there were only one feature, the result would be:
which is echoing equation 63, we could also add a constant in our model.

div.head: Adding an activation function -perceptron algorithm
div.p: In his work in (Rosenblatt, 1957), Frank Rosenblatt proposed in 1957 a perceptron algorithm where for a binary classification task, an activation function σ() is added in series.
div.formula: Input layer X 0 1 Output layer X 1 1 X 2 1 X 0 2 w 0 1 w 1 1 w 2 1 X0 3 σ( )

div.head: Additional layer
div.p: Frank Rosenblatt defined a threshold θ for which:
div.formula: σ(W ′ X) = 1, if W ′ X ≥ θ -1, otherwise(24)
div.p: This linear classification might not be satisfactory for scattered data sets and we might need to either use more complex activation function or add some layers. In which case we move to Artificial Neural Network.
We 38 propose a simple illustration of the perceptron algorithm. We generated two features, X 1 and X 2 and we define the output Y as:
div.formula: Y = 1, if α 1 X 1 + α 2 X 2 -θ > 0 -1, otherwise(25)
div.p: That is indeed splitting the "observation" as follow:
With our perceptron rule, we want to find the estimates α1 , α2 , and θ (pretending we did not know their values).
We follow the steps:
1. we initialize with vectors of 0s or random small values of the estimates 2. we loop until Ŷ -Y < ϵ or until we reach the maximum number of allowed iteration (our algorithm might never converge):
• for each observation i in our data set:
• compute the output value Ŷ
• compute the estimation error for observation i
• update the weights according to the learning rate η
div.formula: w j = w j + η( Ŷ (i) -Y (i))X j , j = 1, 2 θ = θ + η( Ŷ (i) -Y (i))
div.p: We propose as an illustrative exercise to implement this algorithm with: ϵ = 10 -4 and η = 10 -2 and limit our iteration to 10 4 . In (Raschka and Mirjalili, 2019) you can find this algorithm in a more pythonic way, defining classes which can be found here and illustrated:
This separates the data in two categories as follow:
We seperate our data set into a train and test one and compare our prediction accuracy with the scikit-learn Perceptron algorithm one.
• Question 15 -Applyl a non-linear rule for the categorization, try to find a better algorithm to fit a non linear rule.

div.head: Adaline algorithm
div.p: With the perceptron algorithm, we updated observation by observation for each iteration. The threshold activation function we used is not differentiable. The ADAptive LInear NEuron algorithm add a cost function: 26) as this cost function is convex, we can use the gradient descent algorithm introduced section 8.5, we derive the cost function with respect to each weight, we find an update:
div.formula: w = w + ηX ′ Ŷ (i) -Y (i)(27)
div.p: You should find this algorithmfoot_21 much faster than the simple perceptron.
15.12 Artificial Neural Network characteristics
• Layer: set of unconnected similar neurons
• Neuron: processing unit
• Parameters: edges weights (α i and β j )
• Input layer: X
• Hidden layer: In a simple example, we could have σ(x) = 1 1+e -x as we use in a logistic regression for the activation function. We would then have:
div.formula: Z m = σ(α 0m + α T m X), with σ() an activation function • Output layer: T k = β 0k + β T k Z and Y k = g k (T ) = f k (X),
div.p: The same philosophy goes for the next layer:

div.head: Deep learning
div.p: We call it deep when there are multiple hidden layers, in order to estimate those models, we use deep learning techniques:

div.head: Input layer
div.p: Hidden layer 1 Hidden layer 2 Output layer

div.head: Support Vector Machine
div.p: A support vector machine (SVM) constructs a hyper-plane or set of hyper-planes in a high or infinite dimensional space, which can be used for classification, regression or other tasks. Intuitively, a good separation is achieved by the hyper-plane that has the largest distance to the nearest training data points of any class (so-called functional margin), since in general the larger the margin the lower the generalization error of the classifier. We suggest to follow the scikit-learn section on SVM to go further. We illustrate 40 an implementation of a SVM on the same data set as what we used for the perceptron example and the logistic regression using (Raschka and Mirjalili, 2019) typology:

1.0 0.5 0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 X1 1 0 1 2 3 4 X2 SVM -1 1 test set 1.0 0.5 0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 X1 1 0 1 2 3 4 X2 Perceptron -1 1 test set 40 code SVM_illustration.py 1.0 0.5 0.0 0.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 2.5 3.0 X1 1 0 1 2 3 4 X2 Logistic regression -1 1 test set 1 0 1 2 3 4 X1 1 0 1 2 3 X2 KNN -1 1 test set

div.head: SVM kernel for non-linearly separable data
div.p: We generate non-linearly separable data:
div.formula: α 1 cos(X 1 -.1) + α 2 sin [(1 + X 1 )X 2 ] X 2
div.p: We then use the radial basis function (rbf) kernel that transform the data set before apply the SVM:
div.formula: 1 0 1 2 3 4 X1 1 0 1 2 3 X2 SVM non linear -1 1 test set 1 0 1 2 3 4 X1 1 0 1 2 3 X2 SVM -1 1 test set 1 0 1 2 3 4 X1 1 0 1 2 3 X2 logistic regression -1 1 test set 1 0 1 2 3 4 X1 1 0 1 2 3 X2
div.p: Gaussian Naive Bayes -1 1 test set We find the following accuracy scores: logistic regression 85% linear SVM 85% Gaussian Naive Bayes 91.6% Decision tree 95% KNN 96.6% SVM with a rbf kernel 100%

div.head: Linear Discriminant Analysis
div.p: Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) is a method similar to Principal Component Analysis (PCA). They reduce the dimensions in a dataset. LDA is unsupervised while PCA is supervised. LDA reduce the dimension by finding feature subspace that optimizes class separability.

div.head: Principal Component Analysis
div.p: We 41 can resort to a principal component analysis to reduce the dimensions of a data set to a minimum set of common factors. In the case where one factor would explain more than 70% of the variance, we would keep only one factor. For this exercise, we focus on financial data of the French biotech sector over 5 year since September 2012 with a capitalization over EUR 100 million. We use data directly from Euronext and focus on the following companies: ABIVAX, STALLERGENES GREER, GENFIT, ERYTECH PHARMA, VIRBAC, BOIRON, NICOX REGROUPEMENT, VETOQUINOL, INVENTIVA, VALNEVA, ADOCIA, CELLECTIS, ONXEO, GUERBET, DBV TECHNOLOGIES, GEN-SIGHT BIOLOGICS, PCAS, INNATE PHARMA, POXEL, PHARNEXT, NANOBIOTIX, AB SCIENCE, TRANSGENE, GENKYOTEX.

div.head: Method
div.p: We standardize our returns (centre-reduit):
div.formula: R cr i,t = R i,t -Ri σ i
div.p: where, for each stock i, Ri is the average and σ i its standard deviation, and assume the their covariance matrix is Σ.
The first principal component is
div.formula: F 1,t = w ′ 1 R cr t
div.p: , where w 1 is the eigenvector associated with the largest eigenvalue λ 1 of Σ. This value of w 1 solves the problem max w w ′ Σw subject to the normalization w ′ w = 1. The eigenvalue λ 1 equals V AR(F 1,t ) = w ′ 1 Σw 1 . The jth principal component solves the same problem, but under the additional restriction that w ′ i w j = 0∀i < j. The solution is the eigenvector associated with the jth largest eigenvalue λ j (which equals V AR(F j,t ) = w ′ j Σw j ). We extract factor 1 and 2 (F 1,t and F 2,t ) with a principal component analysis :
Explained variance ratio per PCA component 0 Then for each stock returns i, we use the following linear regression:
div.formula: R cr i,t = α i F 1,t + β i F 2,t + ϵ i,t(28)
div.p: 41 PCA_example.py
We assume that the factors and the residuals are following normal distributionfoot_22

div.p: ) 15.15.2 Shock generations -normal distribution assumption We chose a number S of simulations we want to run and follow those 4 steps to generate a shock on the portfolio: 1. generate simulation for F s 1,t+1 , F s 2,t+1 and ϵ s i,t+1 2. estimate the standardize returns for each stock: Rcr,s i,t+1 = α1 F s 1,t+1 + α2 F s 2,t+1 + ϵ s i,t+1 3. compute the actual returns Rs i,t+1 = Rcr,s i,t+1 σ i + Ri 4. compute the volume of the shock for the portfolio i Rs i,t+1 A i,t , with A i,t the volume of equity in the portfolio for the stock i at time t, before the shock 15.15.3 Exercise: PCA on EBA stress test scenario in 2016 Work on the EBA stress test scenario that were used in 2016. You have the GDP, inflation and unemployment information stored in unemployment_EBA_2016.csv, GDP_EBA_2016.csv, and inflation_EBA_2016.csv Find the most important factor behind this scenarios of 2016. Once available, compare them to the 2018 scenarios.

div.head: Boosting
div.p: Boostingfoot_23 describes a set of methods which transform, or boost, a weak learner into a strong one (model). This means that one iteratively applies models to the data which individually have weak generalisation properties, but the final ensemble of models generalises well. Two popular boosting techniques are gradient boosting and adaptive boosting, or adaBoost. In gradient boosting, one sequentially updates the estimator in the direction of change of the loss function, i.e. a form of error correction. For adaBoost, one gives larger weights to misclassified or badly fitted observations at each consecutive step until one reaches a certain stopping criterion. Stopping criteria can be linked to target test or a flattening out-of-bag performance. AdaBoost requires a potentially large amount of data to converge and is susceptible to noise in the data. Boosting is often applied to tree models.

div.head: Train and test sets
div.p: We can randomly separate our data set into two sets: a Train and a Test data set. The Train data set will have two third of the data, and the Test set the rest. The practice is then to fit a model on the Train data set, then to use prediction on the test data set and see if the predictions are "close" to our empirical observations.

div.p: We refer directly to sklearn tutorial on Tuning the hyper-parameters of an estimator and provide a detailed practical example section 15.19.1

div.head: Python: Entertaining application -Perudo game
div.p: To apply the skills developed so far, we suggest to play Liar's Dice and play the Perudofoot_24 version and take the rules from perudo.com, but with some twists to make it simple:
The object of perudo is to be the last player with a die or more.

Perudo is played in rounds. Each player receives a cup and five dice. Each round begins by all players rolling their dice around in the cup. After shaking the dice, players turn the cups over on a table top, so that the dice are rolled and under the cups. Each player may peek in his own cup.
Players bid, guessing at the number of rolls. When a player believes that another player has over-estimated, they say Dudo, which means "I doubt" in Spanish.

The first player announces a number, and then the next player has the choice of doubting it, by saying Dudo or raising the bid, either by the number of dice or by the value of the dice (or by doing both). For example, if player one bid three twos, then player two could bid three threes, four twos, four fours, or even ten sixes.

The next player can also announce "calza" which means that he believes that the announcement of the player just before him was the correct guess. In fact, any player can call "calza" at any time even on his own announcement. If the player calling "calza" is correct, then we give him another dice, but if he is wrong then he loses one of his dice.
After player 2 bids, play goes on to the left.
If a player calls Dudo, and is correct, then the player must show the dice, and each player must show their dice to verify whether the number was indeed too high.

If there are enough dice of that number, then the player who called Dudo must place a die in the discard pile. If there are not, then the player who made the last bid must place a die in the middle. In either case, a new round begins.
The player who lost a die in the last round is the first player in the new round. If the player lost his last die, then the player to his left plays first instead.
The last player left with at least one die wins the game. ("Perudo" is a registered trademark of University Games Corporation, Burlingame, CA.)

17.1 Should you call Calza, raise the bet or call Dudo? 17.1.1 If you don't use the information provided by the players Suppose there are 6 players in a game, which means there are initially 30 dice in play (let's denote this number by n dice

). The goal is to determine the most probable number of dice that show the same face value. This can be achieved through a Monte Carlo simulation where numerous random draws ("tirages") are performed. The outcome of a single dice roll can range from 1 to 6. However, it's crucial to remember that an ace (represented by the value 1) is special and can stand for any value between 2 and 6, making it analogous to a joker.
From the simulations, we confirm that the average count of similar dice is 10. But what about the probability associated with making a "Dudo" call?

A "Dudo" is correctly called when the actual count of a particular dice face is strictly less than a player's announced count. We can accumulate all such counts that validate a "Dudo" call. For this particular game setup, if your predecessor announces a count of 13, calling a "Dudo" gives you an over 80% probability of being correct. However, it's essential to recognize that prior players might have provided hints about their dice counts (even though they might be bluffing). This additional information can change the odds. Now before you speak, several players did before you, but be careful, they might be bluffing. Let's imagine that you have three dices of 2, one ace and so far two players spoke and told the audience 3 twos and then 12 twos. If you say 13 twos, are the odds still at 80% against you?
• Question 17
-Now consider that you are a player and so you know the values of your 5 dices, this changes the odds, you randomize the other players dices only.
-Also consider the 5 other players with 5 dices. Some of those other players spoke befor you have to speak. Assign for each player a probability to "bluff".

* As an illustration, if a player calls "there are 13 twos". If he is not bluffing, then he believes that there are round(25 dices / 3) so 8 twos in the 25 other dices and you can deduct that this player has 13-8 = 5 twos in his hidden hand (or some twos and some aces to be correct). This is highly unlikely, but this player can take into account what two players just before him said, maybe the players before him called "11 twos" then "12 twos" so it can be believed that those two players had 5 twos in their 10 dices and they both believed that the others (except them both) had round(20 dices / 3) = 6 twos... Well, the point is that guesses are iterative.

* You have to be conservative and believe that sometimes players are too optimistic. So maybe a player that announces "there are 13 twos" actually is bluffing and only has 3 twos in his hidden hand... To take this into account, you can use a random variable taking value between [0, 1] that will diminish your estimation of dices based on players bet.
18 Python : Pobability, population, samples and absolutely continuous distributions and maximum likelihood estimation
In the following we will study the probability laws governing random variables (rv), their distributions. We are interested in their cumulative distribution function P (X ≤ x).
If you really want to understand probability, you'd need to dig to measure theory Tao (2011) to be able to understand the following definition:
A probability space is a measure space of total measure 1.
In this book, you'll need to get to the following notions:
• interval, length

• box, volume • elementary set • elementary measure • Jordan measurable set • Riemann integral (Darboux integral) • Lebesgue measurability • unsigned Lebesgue measurable function, integral • Boolean algebra • σ-algebra, measurable space • Borel σ-algebra, Borel measurable set • additive measure • measurable functions
• integration on a measure space
• probability space and probability measure For an introduction to probability, rv, see Hansen (2022).

The probability that a discrete rv X equals the value x, P (X = x), is called the probability mass function. The expectation of X is the probability-weighted average. For a discrete rv, taking the value
div.formula: x i such that P (X = x i ) = p i : E(X) = ∞ i=1 p i x i . For a continuous rv, if P (X ≤ x) is differentiable, their probability density function P (X = x) = dP (X≤x)

div.head: Frequency and Statistics or Probability?
div.p: We follow Kaplan and Garrick (1981) to define frequency versus probability. Frequency is an objective, measurable quantity defined as the outcome of repeated (actual or conceptual) experiments; it belongs to the empirical domain of data and statistics. Probability, by contrast, is a numerical measure of uncertainty or degree of belief, representing a state of knowledge rather than a physical property of the world. Uncertainty is assumed to be comparable across propositions, which allows it to be quantified on a calibrated scale. Frequencies provide the reference standard for this calibration but probability, once defined, applies more broadly, including to unique or non-repeatable events where no frequency data exist. Although probability is subjective in that it reflects information rather than reality, it is objective conditional on information: given the same background knowledge, rational agents must assign the same probabilities. Accordingly, statistics is the science of handling data (frequencies), whereas probability is the science of reasoning under incomplete information. Far from being unusable with limited data, probability is precisely the appropriate tool in such settings. Einhorn and Hogarth (1985) writes: Ellsberg (1961) used the following example to show that the uncertainty people experience contains several aspects, one of which is not captured by probabilities: Imagine two urns, each containing red and black balls. In Urn 1, there are 100 balls, but the proportions of red and black are unknown; Urn 2 contains 50 red and 50 black balls. Now consider a gamble such that, if you bet on red and it is drawn from the urn you get USD 100; similarly for black. However, if you bet on the wrong color, the payoff is USD 0. Imagine having to decide which color to bet on if a ball is to be drawn from Urn 1; that is, the choices are red (R 1 ), black (B 1 ), or indifference (I). What about the same choices in Urn 2: (R 2 ), (B 2 ), or (I)? Most people are indifferent in both cases, suggesting that the subjective probability of red in Urn 1 is the same as the known proportion in Urn 2 -namely, .5. However, would you be indifferent to betting on red if Urn 1 were to be used versus betting on red using Urn 2 (R 1 , vs. R 2 )? Similarly, what about B 1 versus B 2 ? Many people find that they prefer R 2 over R 1 , even though their indifference judgments within both urns imply that p(R 1 ) = p(R 2 ) = .5. Furthermore, the same person who prefers R 2 over R 1 may also prefer B 2 over B 1 . This pattern of response is inconsistent with the idea that even a rank order of probabilities can be inferred from choices. Thus, if R 2 is preferred over R 1 , this implies that p(R 2 ) > p(R 1 ). Moreover, because red and black are complementary events, this means that p(B 2 ) < p(B 1 ). However, if B 2 is preferred over B 1 then p(B 2 ) > p(B 1 ), which contradicts the preceding inequality.

div.head: Ellsberg paradox
div.p: [. . .] From our perspective, the importance of Ellsberg's paradox lies in the difference in the nature of the uncertainty between Urns 1 and 2. In Urn 1, whereas one's best estimate of the probability may be .5, confidence in that estimate is low. In Urn 2, on the other hand, one is at least certain about the uncertainty in the urn. Although it may seem strange, and even awkward, to speak of uncertainty as being more or less certain itself, such a concept captures an important aspect of how people make inferences from unknown, or only partially known, generating processes.

[. . .] one could argue that the cost of urn models, coin-flipping analogies, and the like, is that they obscure the fact that most real-world generating processes are not precisely known. Einhorn and Hogarth (1985) defines 0 ≤ θ ≤ 1 the amount of ambiguity and β an individual's attitude toward ambiguity. The judged probability of an initial guessed probability p A is expressed as:
div.formula: S(p A ) = p A + θ 1 -p A -p β A (31)
div.p: To explain the Ellsberg's paradow, they suggest taking θ > 0 and β < 1

div.head: Concepts of Data-Generating Process and iid
div.p: For any data set, as an empiricist, we are making assumptions. The strongest and least credible assumptions we make are on the data-generating process (DGP) 47 where we assume that there is an underlying mechanism that is generating the observations under study. This is discusses in Breiman (2001) and we discuss further such approaches with machine learning section 15.

In our example, this means that we assume that the CAC 40 daily returns are generated by a process, we will introduce possible distributions in the following sections. We further initially assume that the return at date t is drawn from the DGP independently with respect to the outcome at date t-1, but for time series we will need later to tackle the fact that this hypothesis doesn't hold. Further, when applying an ordinary least square method to estimate a linear regression, we will assume (and test) that the residuals have mean 0, were generated by the same underlying distribution, and are independent of each other. For the last two conditions, we say that the residuals are independent and identically distributed (iid). This is often violated: residuals are serially correlated or their variance differ for some observations (heteroskedasticity). Is economic science inductive? Does it begin from observation? Is it not simply a theoretical structure whose coincidence with reality is doubtful? He answers them in the following manner: yes, economic science has become inductive; the knowledge of economic phenomena is developed in the same way as that of physical phenomena, that is, through a continual back and forth [va-et-vient] between observation and theory; given the particular conditions in which it operates, economics employs an inductive approach which is, in part, original, and thanks to which it progressively ensures a better correspondence with reality. he stresses the three steps which were preconditions to the progress of an inductive economic science:

1. the accumulation of data, 47 Davidson et al. (1978) consider that "one could characterise 'econometric modelling' as an attempt to match the hypothetical data generation process postulated by economic theory with the main properties of the observed data."
2. the elaboration of descriptive syntheses such as series of indices and national accounting, 3. the diffusion of a method use to establish laws on the basis of observed data, namely, econometrics.

[. . . ] What naivety, is there not, in supposing, as I have seen it done repeatedly, that the abundance of data, certainly a necessary condition, is also sufficient for understanding phenomena and for good forecasting.
And he gives this recommendation:

In order to make the best use of the observations at his disposal, the economist must first draw all the consequence of what he already knows, that is, construct a model expressing his initial knowledge, in terms of which he can circumscribe properly the supplementary information to be gleaned from the data. Effective research demands this type of approach. To advance on the path of knowledge, the economist can and must first proceed with a deductive analysis of the potential causes of phenomena. The model is precisely the result of this deductive analysis.

[. . . ] To gain knowledge of reality, the economist must continually rationalize and refine his theoretical constructions as he deciphers the information contained in the increasingly abundant data at his disposal.

div.head: Distribution function, mean, variance
div.p: We follow section 13.4 and use the history of CAC 40 index including dividends (net returns) 48 . Now if as an investor I believe that the history or daily returns are representative of the likely upcoming daily returns, I might want to know to likelihood of a future daily return of 0.045 (i.e. 4.5%). I can approach this question two ways:
1. I build an empirical histogram 2. I fit a parametric 49 distribution (normal, student, etc.) on the empirical data
In formal terms, I consider the daily returns to be a continuous rv X and believe that I can find a pdf and cdf that is stable over time and can help me predict the realizations.

div.head: Empirical histogram
div.p: If we build an empirical histogram, we have to chose the granularity of the bins. If doing it manually, we can start by limiting our representation to 2 bins: ] -∞, 0] and ]0, +∞[. In our representation, we have: neg pos count 3762 4072 We can normalize this so that the sum of the bins is equal to 1 (100%):
neg pos count 0.48 0.52
In other words, empirically, I have 52% of probability to have a positive daily return 50 . P (X > 0) = 0.52.

48 vansteenberghe_samples.py 49 usually, the density function is not known, here we select an arbitrary function and determine the appropriate parameter for this function to describe the distribution of the sample 50 before concluding, we would need to perform statistical test to see if this is indeed statistically different than 50%

We can use directly the python command and chose both the number of bins (20 in this example) and whether we normalize the histogram or not: 0.100 0.075 0.050 0.025 0.000 0.025 0.050 0.075 0.100 0 5 10 15 20 25 30 CAC 40 net return close So to answer our initial question on the likelihood to have a daily return of 4.5%, we can zoom in: 0.042 0.044 0.046 0.048 0.050 0.0 0.2 0.4 0.6 0.8 CAC 40 net return close

For a continuous rv X, the pdf P (X = 0.045) = 0, (Hansen, 2022), we are only able to quantify the probability for X to fall in an interval. We visually observe a probability around 0.3% (so on the y-axis between 0.2 and 0.4) which we confirm by counting how many daily return fell inside the bucket [0.04, 0.05] of size 0.01 over the total count of daily return: 0.38%. You can look at equation (1.6) in Li and Racine (2023).

div.head: Parametric density estimation
div.p: We will present various parametric distributions in the coming sections 51 , we need to believe that it is possible to fit a "function" P (X = x) that gives us the probability that the daily return is x. Note that the goodness of fit of that function will be relevant if we want to trust the answer on the probability, for this there exist various tests, one of which is presented section 22.6.

div.head: Mean
div.p: We define the expectation as a weighed average:
div.formula: E(X) = ∞ -∞ xP (X = x)dx or in unified notation E(X) = ∞ -∞ xdF X (x) in our case E (X) = 0.038%.

div.head: Standard Deviation
div.p: We define the standard deviation as:
div.formula: s(X) = ∞ -∞ [x -E(X)] 2 P (X = x)dx
div.p: in our case we find 0.013

div.p: We introduce the normal distribution N (µ, σ 2 ). Its probability density function, P (X = x), is
in simple words this function gives us the probability that a random variable X takes the value x if the random variable follow the normal distribution, we typically write X ∼ N (µ, σ 2 ).

For illustration, we take the parameter µ and σ as the empirical mean and standard deviation observed on the CAC 40 daily returns: ∂x = g ′ (x)e g(x) for any function g. Show that the (unique) maximum of the normal pdf is µ.
The mean or first moment of the distribution can be computed following Section 18.5.1. We start from

div.head: Introduction to Maximum Likelihood Estimation
div.p: Let's imagine that you have a sample of size n, [x 1 , . . . , x n ], taken from a population governed by a normal law. We want to estimate two parameters of the parametric law, µ and σ. This means you are searching for the parameters that are making a normal law most likely to have generated the sample you observe. We call this method the Maximum Likelihood Estimation. Said differently:

The maximum likelihood estimator is the value of the parameter vector that maximizes the likelihood function, that is, the value of the parameter vector under which the observed data were most likely to have been observed.
The probability density function for a sample of n independent identically distributed normal random variables is the likelihood we want to maximize:
as the random variables are independent and identically distributed, equation 33 can be written:
div.formula: L(µ, σ) = n i=1 f (x i ; µ, σ)(34)
div.p: To say it differently, the probability that the random variable X took the value x i , for any i (∀i) is by definition the probability density function and here we use a parametric density function with two parameters that have to be estimated:
div.formula: P r (X = x i ) = f (x i ; µ, σ)(35)
div.p: We want to choose α and µ that maximize L(µ, σ):
div.formula: max µ,σ L(µ, σ)(36)
div.p: As the logarithm function is continuous strictly increasing and it is easier to deal with the log-likelihood, we maximize:
div.formula: log (L(µ, σ)) = - n 2 log 2πσ 2 - 1 2σ 2 n i=1 (x i -µ) 2
div.p: In a maximization problem, we can apply first order conditions, they yields the following parameters that will maximize our probabilities:
div.formula: μ = n i=1 x i n σ2 = 1 n n i=1 (x i -μ) 2
div.p: It can be demonstrated that the estimator of the variance is biased:

Intuition. Why is there a bias? The key point is that in the sample variance formula we subtract the sample mean μ, rather than the true mean µ. Because μ itself is estimated from the data, it tends to be closer to the sample points than the true µ. This makes the deviations (x i -μ) 2 systematically smaller than the deviations (x i -µ) 2 , and therefore the variance estimator underestimates the true variance on average.
If we divide by n as in the maximum likelihood formula, we get this downward bias of factor n-1 n . That is why the unbiased sample variance uses n -1 in the denominator:
so that

Interpretation. The difference between dividing by n and n -1 becomes negligible for large n (since n-1 n → 1 as n → ∞). But for small samples, the correction is important: the unbiased variance estimator avoids the systematic underestimation that comes from using μ in place of µ.

div.head: Confidence interval of MLE estimators
div.p: We follow Ruppert and Matteson (2015). The matrix of second partial derivatives of a function is called its Hessian matrix, so the Fisher information matrix is the expectation of the Hessian of the negative log-likelihood:
div.formula: F ij (θ) = -E ∂ 2 ∂θ i ∂θ j log (L(θ))(39)
div.p: The standard errors are the square roots of the diagonal entries of the inverse of the Fisher information matrix:
div.formula: s θi = F θ -1 ii(40)
div.p: And finally, to be able to estimate a confidence interval:

Under suitable assumptions, for large enough sample sizes, the maximum likelihood estimator is approximately normally distributed with mean equal to the true parameter vector and with covariance matrix equal to the inverse of the Fisher information matrix.
For a normal law, we have:
div.formula: ∂ 2 L ∂µ 2 = -i 1 σ 2 = - n σ 2 hence F (μ) = n σ 2 and s μ = σ √ n . Thus, μ ∼ N µ, σ 2 n or: μ -µ σ √ n ∼ N (0, 1)(41)
div.p: and
and remember that z .05/2 = -z 1-.05/2 .

div.head: Estimate the probability of occurrence of a given return
div.p: With our DGP in mind (a normal law for the returns) and our empirical observations, we can answer the previous question for the probability to see a daily return of 4.5%, or more specifically, the probability that our CAC 40 daily return will fall between 4 and 5%:
• from empirical distribution, the probability is 0.38%;
• from a parametric normal distribution, the theoretical probability is 0.12%.
With a normal distribution we tend to underestimate the probability of having absolute large daily returns.

div.head: Cumulative distribution function
div.p: The cumulative distribution function (cdf) answers the question: "What is the probability that my returns are below or equal to x?"
div.formula: P (X ≤ x) = x -∞ P (X)dX
div.p: We can apply a normal law cdf to the CAC 40 daily return:

With the empirical distribution function, for the cumulative up to 4.5% daily return, we obtain 99.6%, but for the normal distribution we find 99.95% where again with the normal distribution we underestimate the probability of having daily returns exceeding 4.5%: in practice we had 0.4% of the daily returns above 4.5% but the normal distribution function estimates that probability at 0.05%.
• Question 19 -Find the return x i so that at this return, the probability to have daily return below is 0.4%.
* We'll see section 59.1, that you are then searching for a Value at Risk (VaR).
18.10 What is the mean of the absolute of a normally distributed random variable?

Now let's imagine that we have a random variable X that follows N (0, σ 2 ), we might wonder the mean of the |X| random variable, E(|X|). Writing x = σz where conveniently Z ∼ N (0, 1), we have E(|X|) = σE(|Z|). And:
div.formula: E(Z) = +∞ -∞ x √ 2π e -x 2 2 dx E(|Z|) = 2 √ 2π +∞ 0 xe -x 2 2 dx E(|Z|) = 2 π -e -t 2 2 ∞ O = 2 π Finally, E(|X|) = σ 2 π
div.p: We can verify this by drawing a sample and computing the formula to compare.

18.11 What is the largest observation you expect to have in a sample? Let's imagine that we have a random variable X that follows N (µ, σ 2 ). If you have a sample of size N , what is the largest value you expect to observe in this sample? • Question 20 -Show 52 that this is close to the formula equation 43: * Each student chose her own µ and σ, then plot the largest value expected as a function of N ; * Generate N ∈ [1, 10, 10 2 , 10 3 , 10 4 , 10 5 ] observations from a N (µ, σ 2 ), use the numpy random seed 53 at arbitrary 7 so you all have the same results (except for your choices of µ and σ); * Plot both the expected function and the "empirical" largest values. div.head: Exponential distribution
div.p: Exponential distributions are a particular case of gamma distribution. We write the probability density function of the exponential distribution:
div.formula: f (x; α) = αe -αx , for x ≥ 0 0 for x < 0
div.p: For illustration here the exponential probability density function:
0.00 0.02 0.04 0.06 0.08 Variable value 0 20 40 60 80 100 Probability of occurence
The likelihood function on a sample of size n is:
div.formula: L(x 1 , . . . , x n |α) = α n exp -α n i=1
div.p: x i 52 inspired by Jon Keating's lectures 53 Note that numpy Random Generator should be the future option Deriving this expression with respect to α to find an optimum α o :
div.formula: nα n-1 o exp -α o n i=1 x i - n i=1 x i α n o exp -α o n i=1 x i = 0
div.p: an optimum is thus find for the reciprocal of the sample mean:
div.formula: α o = n n i=1 x i
div.p: For the exponential law the first moment (the mean) is:
div.formula: µ 1 = α +∞ 0 xe -αx dx = α e -αx -αx -1 α 2 ∞ 0 = 1 α
div.p: and we can demonstrate that the variance of the exponential law is 1 α 2 .
We can fit this to our negative CAC 40 return (we need to take the absolute values to be able to fit our exponential distribution):
0.00 0.02 0.04 0.06 0.08 0 20 40 60 80 100 18.12.1 Compound Poisson-Exponential distributions

Typically, in insurance claims modelling (non-life), we model the occurrence of claims as a Poisson 54 process of parameter λ and the magnitudes of claims as an exponential law of parameter α. Writing that the claim epoch follow a Poisson process is equivalent to writing that the interoccurrence time follow an exponential distribution with rate parameter λ and a probability density function of λe -λx for x ≥ 0. We model an insurance company facing claims arriving over time with a Poisson law N t and which claim size C i follow and exponential law. We have the compound process:
div.formula: X t = Nt i=1 C i and its estimate Xt = Nt i=1 Ĉi . We have: E[X(t)] = E[N (t)]E[C] = λ α and V ar[X(t)] = E[N (t)]E[C 2 ] = λ(σ 2 + µ 2 ) = λ( 1 α 4 + 1 α 2 )
div.p: 54 the probability density function of a Poisson distribution is P (Nt = k) = e -λt (λt) k k! , λ > 0

div.head: Gamma distribution
div.p: We write the Gamma distribution with distribution function f (x) = r s Γ(s) x s-1 e -rx , x > 0, s > 0 the shape parameter, r > 0 the rate parameter and Γ(s) = +∞ 0 u s-1 e -u du, we have the property 55 Γ(s + 1) = sΓ(s) and Γ(s+1) β s+1 = +∞ 0
u s e -βu du. Such distribution can be useful in insurance modelling (claims size distribution). If s > 1 then it is alike a log-normal distribution:

0.00 0.02 0.04 0.06 0.08 0.10 0 20 40 60 80 100 120 140 160 Gamma distribution as a function of shape parameter, rate param = 100 0.5 1.0 1.5 3.0 0.00 0.02 0.04 0.06 0.08 0.10 0 20 40 60 80 100 Gamma distribution as a function of rate parameter, shape param = 1.5 0.5 1.0 10.0 100.0 200.0
For a random variable following a gamma distribution, we have:
div.formula: E(X) = +∞ 0 r s Γ(s) u s e -ru du = r s Γ(s) +∞ 0 u s e -ru du E(X) = r s Γ(s) Γ(s + 1) r s+1 = s r
div.p: For the variance, we have
div.formula: V ar(X) = E(X 2 ) -(E(X)) 2 and E(X 2 ) = s(s+1) r 2 so V ar(X) = s r 2 .

div.head: Probability Integral Transformation
div.p: Let X be a continuous rv with a continuous and invertible (strictly increasing) cdf. Then the random variable Y = F X (x) has the support [0, 1] and F Y (y) = y and f Y (y) = 1, (Hansen, 2022).
55 some elements of demonstration here
This means that from a uniform rv, we can generate rv following other distribution. Let's imagine we want to generate a rv following the Pareto cdf:
div.formula: F X (x) = 1 - x m x α (48)
div.p: with x ≥ x m > 0 and α > 0. We take 56 Y ∼ U with U the uniform cdf over [0, 1]. We want y = 1 -xm x α so we take x = x m (1 -y) -1 α . We start by generating a sample uniformly distributed, so we can check that as expected our expectation is 0.5 and the standard deviation .25, remembering that P (X = x) = 1 on [0, 1]

19 Python: Sample statistics, Law of Large Numbers and Central Limit Theorem 19.1 Mean and Standard Deviation in theory

If we have a sample of size n from a population, we considerfoot_26 the random variable X which have the outcome values x (that would be x ∈ [0, ∞[ for a stock price), each outcome having the probability of occurring of p(x). We can consider that this random variables are taken from a greater population with mean µ and standard deviation σ (although a price process would never follow a normal law). In practice, we do not have access to the full population and have to work with finite samples. Not that the outcome values X are considered a random sample of independent and identically distributed random variables with the same probability distribution function. We will see in section 59 possible distributions function for financial returns.
In practical terms, if over 5 days we observe the following price values x t for a stock X, we have the probabilities:

This is a very basic example and in practice you would need much more observations to work with plausible probability values. The expected value of your random variable X is called the sample mean, we can show that it is unbiased compared with the population true mean:
div.formula: E( Xn ) = E 1 n n i=1 X i = 1 n n i=1 E(X i ) = 1 n nµ = µ
div.p: We can also write:
div.formula: E Xn = x xp(x)
div.p: In our example, this would be:
We can state the weak law of large numbers:

div.head: Weak Law of Large Numbers (WLLN)
div.p: Statement: Let X 1 , X 2 , . . . be a sequence of i.i.d. random variables with expected value E(X i ) = µ and variance V ar(X i ) = σ 2 < ∞. Then, for any ϵ > 0,
div.formula: lim n→∞ P 1 n n i=1 X i -µ > ϵ = 0
div.p: Illustration: Imagine flipping a fair coin, where Heads is '1' and Tails is '0'. The expected value is µ = 0.5. If you flip the coin many times and take the average of the results, this average will converge to 0.5 as the number of flips goes to infinity.
Another more mathematical way of writing the WLLN: If we have
div.formula: X i , i ∈ [1, n] iid, with E (|X|) < ∞, then Xn = 1 n n i=1 X i p → E(X).
div.p: If we further assume E(X) = µ and σ(X) = σ. The variance of the mean of our sample is biased:
div.formula: V ( Xn ) = V 1 n n i=1 X i = 1 n 2 V n i=1 X i = 1 n 2 nV (X) = σ 2 n
div.p: If we want an unbiased standard deviation 58 measure from our sample, one can demonstrate that it can be written as:
div.formula: S 2 n = 1 n -1 E X i -Xn 2
div.p: The variance of the sample is:
div.formula: V (X) = E X -E(X) 2 = x p(x) x -E(X) 2
div.p: In our example, this would be:
div.formula: V (X) = (1.1-1.2) 2 × 1 5 +(1.2-1.2) 2 × 3 5 +(1.3-1.2) 2 × 1 5 = 0.004 19.3 Central Limit Theorem (CLT) Statement: Let X 1 , X 2 , . . . be i.i.d. random variables with E(X i ) = µ and V ar(X i ) = σ 2 < ∞.
div.p: Then, as n → ∞, the random variable
div.formula: Z n = n i=1 X i -nµ σ √ n
div.p: converges in distribution to the standard normal distribution, i.e.,
• Question 21

-Consider rolling a six-sided die. The expected value is µ = 3.5 and the variance is σ 2 = 35 12 (cf. Discrete uniform distribution). If you roll the die many times, sum the results, subtract n × 3.5, and then divide by the standard deviation times the square root of n, illustrate visually that the result will converge in distribution to the standard normal distribution as n goes to infinity.

A more compact way to write the CLT: If we have X i , i ∈ [1, n] iid, with E (|X|) < ∞, with E(X) = µ and σ(X) = σ, then if we define Zn = Xn-µ σ 2 n , then Zn converges in distribution, n → ∞ to the standard normal distribution N (0, 1).
We suggest to illustrate this with samples taken from a population that has a uniform distribution function, with its probability distribution function:
div.formula: f (x) = 1 b-a , for a ≥ x ≥ b 0 for x < a or x > b
div.p: we take a = 0 and b = 1.

div.head: Gosset and its Student
div.p: Gosset has established under the pseudonym Student that:
div.formula: Xn -µ σ 2 n ∼ N (0, 1)
div.p: and t n-1 being the t-Student distribution with n -1 degrees of freedom:
div.formula: Xn -µ S 2 n n ∼ t n-1
div.p: And remember that for a t-Student distribution with ν degrees of freedom t ν , then when ν → ∞, then it becomes a standard normal distribution N (0, 1):
