This means that the greater our sample size n, the closer to the actual population mean and standard deviation we will measure. This can be rewritten and is called the Central Limit Theroem:

div.head: Skewness, Kurtosis and covariance in theory
div.p: The standard deviation of our sample is defined as:
div.formula: σ X = V (X)
div.p: its skewness is defined as:
div.formula: S(X) = E X -E(X) σ X
div.p: its kurtosis is defined as:
div.formula: K(X) = E X -E(X) σ X 4
div.p: The covariance between two random variables X and Y indicates how likely they are to occur together:
div.formula: Cov(X, Y ) = E [(X -E(X)) (Y -E(Y ))] = E(XY ) -E(X)E(Y )

div.head: Mean, Standard Deviation and Correlation in practice
div.p: If we look at an observation vector X = [x 0 , x 1 , . . . , x T -1 ] (like the close price of a company's stock), we can call E(X) the expected price, which in practice will be the average over the sample:
div.formula: E(X) = 1 T T -1 i=0 x i
div.p: The standard deviation is a measure of risk expressed in %:
The covariance of X with another vector of observations Y = [y 0 , y 1 , . . . , y T -1 ] can be computed as:
div.formula: cov(X, Y ) = 1 T T -1 i=0 [x i -E(X)] [y i -E(Y )]
div.p: The Pearson coefficient of correlation between X and Y is then:
div.formula: corr(X, Y ) = cov(X, Y ) σ(X)σ(Y )

div.head: Correlation Measures techniques
div.p: We can detail the correlation measures:
div.formula: • Pearson correlation coefficient ∈ [-1, 1]: r = (x i -x)(y i -ȳ) (x i -x) 2 ) (y i -ȳ) 2 )
div.p: • Kendall rank correlation coefficient:
div.formula: τ = n c -n d 1 2 n(n -1)
div.p: • Spearman rank correlation coefficient:
with:
• n: number of value in each data set
• n c : number of concordant, a pair (x i , y i ) is said to be concordant with the pair (x j , y j ) if either both x i < x j and y i < y j or x i > x j and y i > y j
• n d : number of discordant, a pair (x i , y i ) is said to be discordant with the pair (x j , y j ) if either x i < x j and y i > y j or x i > x j and y i < y j
• d i : the difference between the ranks 59 of corresponding values x i and y i
One might wonder: should correlation be applied to prices or returns? You can find detailed advice to apply correlation on asset returns here, here and here.
59 think of ranking from first, second to last, with possibly similar ranking when the values are equal, then you need to adjust for ties (take the mean rank not the upper nor lower rank of the tie)
I follow here Johnson (1987).

div.head: Inverse distribution function method
div.p: We have seen how to generate random vectors from a uniform distribution. Now we want to generate a variable X whose cumulative distribution function is specified by F . The inverse distribution function method has two steps:
• Generate a variable U that is uniform;
div.formula: • Let X = Sup x [F (x) ≤ Y ].
div.p: In the event that F is strictly increasing, then F -1 is well defined and step 2 becomes: X = F -1 (U ). I test 60 the following two:
• exponential:
When no closed form of F -1 is available, the following recursion 61 frequently converges to a fixed point x * :
div.formula: x n+1 = x n - F (x n ) -U f (x n )

div.head: Normal generator
div.p: A standard normal law can be generated with two independent uniform vectors U 1 and U 2 :
div.formula: X = -2 ln(U 1 ) cos(2πU 2 ) or X = -2 ln(U 1 ) sin(2πU 2 )
div.p: Once a standard normal variable X is generated, a normal variable Y ∼ N (µ, σ 2 ) can be generated with Y = σX + µ Finally, a log-normal variable can be generated from a normal variable taking its exponential.

div.head: Multivariate normal distribution
div.p: A p-dimensional random vector X = (X 1 , . . . , X p ) ′ is defined to have a multivariate normal distribtuion if and only if every nontrivial linear combination of the p-components of X has a univariate normal distribution. The distribution of X is denoted N p (µ, Σ), with µ i = E(X i ) and Σ i,j = cov(X i , X j ). If Σ has full rank p, then the density function of X exists. Let L be the Choleski factorization which is then unique lower triangular such that LL ′ = Σ. Take p univariate independent random vector Z = (Z 1 , . . . , Z p ) ′ with Z ∼ N p (0, I) if I define X = LZ + µ, then X ∼ N p (µ, Σ).
60 numpy_exercise_part4.py 61 Cf the Newton's method equation 5.

div.head: Multivariate distributions -introduction to NORTA
div.p: As shown in Cario andNelson (1997) andHenderson et al. (2000), it is not easy to determine the required Σ Z to get Σ X .
The NORTA generation procedure works as follow:
1. Determine the lower triangular matrix L such that LL ′ = Σ Z 2. Generate W = (W 1 , . . . , W n ) ′ iii following N (0, 1)
div.formula: 3. Z = LW 4. Generate our desired X such that X i = F -1 X i [Φ (Z i )]
div.p: with F X i the desired cdf, and indeed, the central problem is to select Σ Z that gives Σ X and for most applications there is no closed form:
div.formula: Σ ij X = cov(X i , X j ) = cov F -1 X i [Φ (Z i )] , F -1 X j [Φ (Z j )]
div.p: 21 Probability distributions from option prices or surveys
We aim to recover the subjective probability distribution of average inflation over n years. Let π(n) be the n-year average annual CPI inflation, f Q n π(n) (•) its pdf under the n-forward measure.

There are two complementary routes. First, use zero-coupon inflation caps/floors across strikes to extract the risk-neutral (forward-measure) distribution by differentiating option prices with respect to strike (Breeden-Litzenberger) and discounting with the n-year zero rate (Breeden and Litzenberger, 1978;Ross, 1976b;Kitsul and Wright, 2013). Second, use survey data to obtain a physical or stated-belief distribution and compare it to the options-implied one.
To extract a probability distribution of future inflation from option prices, remember three key distinctions:
1. What we study. π(n) is the average inflation over n years. When n = 1, this reduces to one-year-ahead inflation, π(1).

2. How probabilities are measured. Option prices reflect probabilities under the riskneutral (or forward ) measure Q n , not real-world beliefs. Under Q n , expected returns are adjusted for risk so that all assets earn the risk-free rate. Hence, the pdf we recover from prices is a market-implied, not a subjective, distribution.

3. What enters the estimation. We use pricing inputs such as the term structure (for discounting) and option quotes across strikes, then apply estimation choices to smooth the data and enforce no-arbitrage conditions (for example, monotonicity and convexity) using tools like local polynomial regression.
1. Observed: Inflation caps/floors ⇒ option prices P t (k, n).
2. Differentiate:
div.formula: f Q n π(n) (k) ∝ ∂ 2 P t (k, n) ∂k 2 (Breeden-Litzenberger). 3. Interpret: f Q n = market-implied (risk-neutral) pdf.
div.p: 4. Compare: With survey-based (physical) pdf to infer pricing kernel.

div.head: Options-implied inflation probability density functions
div.p: Kitsul and Wright (2013) define a zero-coupon inflation cap as a contract entered into at time t. The seller of the cap promises to pay a fraction

of a notional underlying principal as a single payment in n years' time, where π(n) denotes the average annual total consumer price index (CPI) inflation rate from t to t + n (not seasonally adjusted) and k denotes the strike of the cap. Without loss of generality, the notional underlying principal is normalized to 1. In exchange for this, the buyer makes an up-front payment of P t (k, n). The contract is effectively a call option on inflation. If π(n) < k, then the option is out-of-the-money.
Consider a one-year zero-coupon inflation cap with strike k = 2%. If realized inflation is π(1) = 3%, the payoff is
that is, one cent per dollar of notional. If both parties are certain that π(1) = 3% (i.e. P[π(1) = 3%] = 1) and there is no risk premium, then the fair price equals the discounted expected payoff:
Discounting: why the factor e -ny(n) appears

Principle. A price equals the expected present value of its future payoff under a pricing measure. If y(n) is the continuously compounded n-year zero rate observed at t = 0 (so here we take a bond with known return at time t up to maturity and not the risk-free rate, which is unknown at time t up to t + n), the price of one unit delivered at t = n is
div.formula: B(0, n) = e -ny(n) .
div.p: This follows from continuous compounding: investing e -ny(n) today (t = 0) at rate y(n) for n years grows deterministically to 1 at t = n. Suppose I receive m interest payments at a rate of r m per annum. After t years, I will receive:

1 + r m mt which can be rewritten as exp mt log 1 + r m ≃ exp (rt), when r m is in the vicinity of 0. We can get the same approximation with a Taylor expansion. If you have the amount M (t) and invest it at interest rate r, then
div.formula: M (t + dt) = M (t) + dM (t) dt t + dt -t 1! + . . . hence M (t + dt) -M (t) = M (t)rdt ≃ dM (t)
div.p: dt dt, and
div.formula: dM (t) dt = rM (t)
div.p: which has the solution M (0)e rt .
Applied to the cap. The zero-coupon inflation cap pays only at t + n. Therefore
div.formula: P t (k, n) = e -ny(n) E Q n payoff at t + n .(49)
div.p: For n = 1, the payoff is (π(1) -k) + , so
div.formula: Integral form. Let f Q 1 π(1) be the pdf of π(1) under Q 1 . Then P t (k, 1) = e -y(1) ∞ k (π -k) f Q 1 π(1) (π) dπ. (51
div.p: Meaning of Q 1 π(1) > k . Q 1 denotes the 1-forward (or risk-neutral) probability measure.
Thus
div.formula: Q 1 π(1) > k = 1 -F Q 1 π(1) (k) is the tail probability of π(1) under Q 1 .
div.p: Differentiation with a k-dependent lower limit. From the integral form
div.formula: P t (k, 1) = e -y(1) ∞ k (π -k) f Q 1 π(1) (π) dπ, set g(k) := ∞ k (π -k) f Q 1 π(1) (π) dπ. Apply Leibniz's rule with h(π, k) := (π -k)f Q 1 π(1) (π), a(k) = k, and b(k) = ∞: d dk b(k) a(k) h(π, k) dπ = h b(k), k b ′ (k) -h a(k), k a ′ (k) + b(k) a(k) ∂h ∂k (π, k) dπ.
div.p: Here
div.formula: b ′ (k) = 0, a ′ (k) = 1, and h a(k), k = (k -k) f Q 1 π(1) (k) = 0, while ∂h ∂k (π, k) = -f Q 1 π(1) (π).
div.p: Here, we have
Recovering implied pdfs from option prices Kitsul and Wright (2013) write:
We wish to use quotes on inflation caps at different strike prices to find the pdf for n-year inflation π(n) that solves the equation
div.formula: P t (k, n) = exp -ny(n) E Q n [max((1 + π(n)) n -(1 + k) n , 0)] ,(52)
div.p: where P t (k, n) denotes the price of an n-year inflation cap with a strike of k and y(n) is the continuously compounded n-year zero-coupon bond yield at time t.

The idea for forming implied pdfs is based on the result that the second derivative of the price of a call option with respect to the strike price represents the risk-neutral pdf (Breeden and Litzenberger, 1978;Ross, 1976b).

The specific method used for solving for the implied pdf follows Li and Zhao (2009), but without conditioning information, and it is based on the local polynomial regression of Aıt-Sahalia and Duarte (2003).

We first filter the data, replacing the prices of inflation floors and caps by the closest prices that satisfy slope and convexity constraints. Consider a set of L inflation caps on a given day at a given maturity. Let y i denote the price of the ith cap, with a strike of k i . We seek to approximate the price of an inflation cap at a strike price k ′ in a neighborhood around k by a locally linear function
We estimate the coefficients as β0 (k), β1 (k) = arg min
div.formula: β 0 (k),β 1 (k) L i=1 y i -β 0 (k) -β 1 (k)(k i -k) 2 K k i -k h /h,
div.p: where K(•) is a kernel function and h is a bandwidth. The bandwidth is optimal for the minimization of integrated mean squared error, as described in Aıt-Sahalia and Duarte (2003).

The second derivative of the cap price can then be obtained as β′ 1 (k). Using the nominal Treasury term-structure data set of Gürkaynak et al. (2007) to obtain the risk-free n-year yield, we then have the pdf of inflation under the forward measure. This method constructs the risk-neutral pdf on a single day at a single maturity from options at different strike prices.

div.head: From option quotes to implied pdfs: what each paper contributes
div.p: Step 0: Notation. Let π(n) be the average annual CPI inflation from t to t + n. For a zero-coupon n-year inflation cap with strike k, define
The cap payoff at t + n is (X -K) + .

Step 1: Pricing under the forward measure (foundation in (Ross, 1976b)). Let y(n) be the continuously compounded n-year zero yield and Q n the n-forward measure. The time-t price equals discounted forward-measure expectation:
div.formula: P t (k, n) = e -ny(n) E Q n (X -K) + .(53)
div.p: Step 2: Breeden-Litzenberger identity on strikes (Breeden and Litzenberger, 1978;Ross, 1976b).
div.formula: View C(K) ≡ e -ny(n) E Q n [(X -K) + ] as a call on X with strike K. Then ∂C ∂K = -e -ny(n) Q n (X > K), ∂ 2 C ∂K 2 = e -ny(n) f Q n X (K),(54)
div.p: where
div.formula: f Q n X is the pdf of X under Q n .
div.p: Step 3: Chain rule to work in inflation strike k. Since K = (1+k) n , define P (k)
div.formula: ≡ P t (k, n) = C(K(k))
div.p: . By the chain rule,

Step 4: Nonparametric recovery method (Li and Zhao, 2009). Given option quotes {(k i , y i )} L i=1 at one maturity and date, recover a smooth function k → P t (k, n) whose second derivative yields the pdf via 56. The Li-Zhao framework estimates P under slope/convexity constraints implied by 55-56. Higher convexity of P (k) at a given strike means markets assign more probability mass near that strike. Left-skewed pdf ⇒ greater deflation concern.

Step 5: Local polynomial regression to estimate derivatives (Aıt-Sahalia and Duarte, 2003). Estimate P (k) and its derivatives by local polynomial regression. Around a target k, fit a quadratic locally:
div.formula: ( β0 (k), β1 (k), β2 (k)) = arg min β 0 ,β 1 ,β 2 L i=1 y i -β 0 -β 1 (k i -k) 2 K k i -k h ,(57)
div.p: with kernel K and bandwidth h. Then
Step 6: Putting it together with discounting (Gürkaynak et al., 2007). Obtain y(n) from the Treasury zero-coupon term structure. Then, for n = 1,
For n > 1, combine 56 with the change of variables
div.formula: f Q n π(n) (π) = n(1 + π) n-1 f Q n X (1 + π) n .
div.p: No-arbitrage filters used in practice. Impose P ′ (k) ≤ 0 (monotonicity in strike) and P ′′ (k) ≥ 0 (convexity) before derivative extraction. Replace observed quotes by nearby values that satisfy these constraints, then re-estimate via 57.

Main takeaway for students. Recover the pdf from option quotes in three moves: (i) price as discounted forward-measure expectation 53; (ii) differentiate in strike (Breeden-Litzenberger) 54 and map to inflation strike via the chain rule 56; (iii) estimate the required derivatives nonparametrically with local polynomials 57, using y(n) from the term structure.

div.head: Preparing the implementation
div.formula: X = (1 + π(n)) n , K = (1 + k) n , B(0, n) = e -ny(n) , C(K) = B(0, n) E Q n (X -K) + , ∂ 2 C ∂K 2 (K) = B(0, n) f Q n X (K), f Q n π(n) (π) = n(1 + π) n-1 f Q n X (1 + π) n , Cap(K) -Floor(K) = B(0, n) E Q n [X] -K , n = 1 : ∂ 2 P t (k, 1) ∂k 2 = e -y(1) f Q 1 π(1) (k).

div.head: Python: Weight and height distributions
div.p: Let's consider H (height) and W (weight) two continuous rv, the joint distribution function of (H, W ) is F (h, w) = P (H ≤ h, W ≤ w). The rv H and W are statistically independent if
div.formula: ∀h, w F (h, w) = F H (h)F W (w)(59)
div.p: If we believe that height and weight are statistically independent, then we should 62 just study both rv separately. We apply a Chi-square test of independence of variables and reject H 0 : "there is no significant relationship between weight and height".

In the following, we will try to estimate a relationship between height and weight. (Hermanussen et al., 2001) found that Body weight is not normally distributed, but skewed to the right. Also power transformation was inadequate to sufficiently describe the shape of this distribution. The right tail of weight distributions declines exponentially, beyond a cut-off of +0.5 standard deviations.

We further explore the concept of DGP introduced secion 18.3. Now, the weight and height of an individual is likely to be related to his parent's, but appart from those relations, we are closer to an i.i.d. process than with a time series.
In the following, we

use Indian 2005-6 Demographic and Health Surveys and would like to thank Professor Dean Spears for his help on collecting the data: age weight in .1 kg height in mm count 15494 15494 15494 mean 32.2 567.5 1573.6 std 9.4 121.1 67.8 min 15.0 234.0 1267.0 25% 24.0 486.0 1530.0 50% 32.0 546.0 1573.0 75% 40.0 625.0 1617.0 max 49.0 1599.0 1967.0 Our sample body weights are not normally distributed, but skewed to the right: 200 400 600 800 1000 1200 1400 1600 0.000 0.001 0.002 0.003 0.004 62 code: vansteenberghe_height_normal_distrib.py

We apply a tow-sided t-test and we do not reject the null hypothesis H0: the expected value of this sample made of (presumably) independent observations is equal to µ = 567.5. We apply a two-sided Kolmogorov-Smirnov (cf. section 22.6, alternatively we can use the Anderson-Darling test) test on our sample with the null hypothesis: H 0 the sample was taken from a normal distributed population and we reject the null hypothesis.

div.head: The Lilliefors Test for Normality
div.p: The Lilliefors test is a modification of the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) test, designed to test whether a sample comes from a normal distribution when the mean and variance are unknown and must be estimated from the data.
Step 1. Null hypothesis. We want to test
for some unknown parameters µ and σ 2 .
Step 2. Standardization. From the data we compute the sample mean and variance:
div.formula: μ = 1 n n i=1 X i , σ2 = 1 n -1 n i=1 (X i -μ) 2 .
div.p: We then standardize:
div.formula: Z i = X i - μ σ .
div.p: Step 3. Empirical distribution. Order the standardized data:
Define the empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF):
div.formula: F n (z) = 1 n n i=1 1{Z i ≤ z}.
div.p: Step 4. Compare with fitted normal CDF. Let Φ(z) denote the CDF of the standard normal N (0, 1). Compute the Kolmogorov-Smirnov type statistic:
div.formula: D n = sup z∈R F n (z) -Φ(z) .
div.p: Step 5. Distribution under H 0 . If µ and σ were known, D n would follow the classical KS distribution. But here they are estimated from the sample, which makes the critical values different. Lilliefors (1967) computed these critical values by Monte Carlo simulation.
Step 6. Decision rule. Reject H 0 at significance level α if
div.formula: D n > c α (n),
div.p: where c α (n) is the Lilliefors critical value.

div.head: Log of the weights
div.p: If taking the log of the weight seems closer to a normal distribution, it still fails the KS test: We might want to ask ourselves the question: Are the weights normally distributed? For the normal law, this is so common that several tests have been developped.

div.head: Jarque-Bera test
div.p: We can test normality of the distribution with a Jarque-Bera test, with the test statistics that follows asymptotically a χ 2 2 :
div.formula: JB = T 6 skewness 2 + T 24 (kurtosis -3) 2
div.p: The statistics is above the critical χ 2 2 threshold and we reject H 0 the null hypothesis of normal distribution.

div.head: Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test
div.p: The chi-square test tests if a sample of data came from a population with a specific distribution. The null hypothesis of the test is H 0 : the data follow a specified distribution.
For the chi-square goodness-of-fit computation, the data are divided into k bins and the test statistic is defined as:
div.formula: χ 2 = k i=1 (O i -E i ) 2 E i (60)
div.p: where O i is the observed frequency for bin i and E i is the expected frequency for bin i.

We reject H 0 , and visually: Pearson's chi-squared test is used to test for independence between categorical variables. It compares the observed frequencies of events to the frequencies expected under the null hypothesis. In the context of testing whether a coin is fair, we can use this test to check whether the observed frequencies of heads and tails are consistent with the expected frequencies of a fair coin.
The null hypothesis is that the coin is fair, meaning that the probability of heads (p H ) and tails (p T ) are both 0.5. This leads to the following hypotheses:
div.formula: H 0 : p H = p T = 0.5 H 1 : p H ̸ = p T
div.p: To perform the Pearson's chi-squared test, we conduct a series of coin flips and observe the number of heads and tails. Let O H and O T represent the observed counts of heads and tails, respectively, and let E H and E T be the expected counts under the null hypothesis. The expected counts are calculated based on the assumption that the coin is fair. For a total of n flips, we expect:
div.formula: E H = E T = n 2
div.p: The test statistic is then calculated as:
div.formula: χ 2 = (O H -E H ) 2 E H + (O T -E T ) 2
div.p: E T This statistic follows a chi-squared distribution with 1 degree of freedom. If the test statistic exceeds the critical value from the chi-squared distribution for a chosen significance level (e.g., α = 0.05), we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that the coin is not fair. In practice, we would compute this test after conducting the experiment and then compare the result to a chi-squared critical value table.

While both the chi-square goodness-of-fit test and Pearson's chi-squared test are used to compare observed and expected frequencies, the goodness-of-fit test is used to check whether data follows a specific distribution, whereas Pearson's chi-squared test is used to assess the independence between categorical variables. Pearson's chi-squared test can also be applied in the context of classification problems, where the objective is to test whether the frequencies of true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives deviate from what is expected under the assumption that the classifier is random or unbiased. This is particularly useful in evaluating the performance of binary classifiers in fields such as medical diagnostics, where true and false positives matter greatly. In this context, the observed data is presented in a 2 × 2 contingency table, which shows the counts for the following outcomes:

div.head: Predicted Positive Predicted Negative Actual Positive
div.p: True Positives (TP) False Negatives (FN) Actual Negative False Positives (FP)
True Negatives (TN)

The null hypothesis in this case is that the predictions are independent of the actual classification, meaning that the proportions of true positives, false positives, true negatives, and false negatives do not deviate from what would be expected under a random or unbiased classification. This leads to the hypotheses:
div.formula: H 0 : Predicted classification is independent of actual classification H 1 : Predicted classification is not independent of actual classification
div.p: To perform the Pearson's chi-squared test, we calculate the expected counts for each of the four cells in the contingency table under the assumption of independence. Let O ij represent the observed frequency for cell (i, j) and E ij the expected frequency for the same cell. The expected frequency for each cell is calculated as:
div.formula: E ij = (Row Total i )(Column Total j )
div.p: Total Observations The test statistic is then computed as:
div.formula: χ 2 = 2 i=1 2 j=1 (O ij -E ij ) 2 E ij
div.p: This statistic follows a chi-squared distribution with 1 degree of freedom. If the test statistic exceeds the critical value from the chi-squared distribution for a given significance level, we reject the null hypothesis and conclude that there is a significant relationship between predicted and actual classifications.
In the context of true and false positives, this test helps determine whether the classifier's performance is better than random chance or whether there is a bias in the predictions.

div.head: Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests
div.p: The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test allows us to compare the distribution of two observed returns series.

We can implement a two-sample Kolmogorov-Smirnov test, where the null hypothesis is that both groups were sampled from populations with identical distributions. The two-sided test uses the maximum absolute (vertical) difference between the cumulative distribution function (CDF, denoted F1 and F2 here) of the distributions of the two data vectors. The test statistic is:
div.formula: KS = max x | F1 (x) -F2 (x)|
div.p: If the K-S statistic is small (but be cautious in how you define small as we talk about differences in distribution functions) or the p-value is high, then we cannot reject the hypothesis H 0 that the distributions of the two samples are the same.
We reject the hypothesis that the daily mean return of the CAC 40 index are normally distributed:

Note that when we apply a two-sample test with a randomly generated normal sample, we do not systematically reject the null hypothesis at the 1% threshold that both sample were taken from the same distribution 63 .
22.7 Q-Q plots and probability plots
div.formula: 22.7.1 Quantile-Quantile Plot (Q-Q Plot) Definition: A Q-Q plot is
div.p: a graphical tool used to compare the quantiles of two distributions. Typically, one of the distributions is empirical (from the data), and the other is theoretical. By plotting the quantiles of the empirical distribution against the quantiles of the theoretical distribution, we can visually assess how well the data conforms to the theoretical distribution. If the data lies along a straight line (usually a 45-degree line), it suggests that the data is well-represented by the theoretical distribution. Illustration: Imagine we have a dataset and want to check if it follows a parametric distribution. We can:
1. Rank the data in ascending order. 63 we later move on to the Lilliefors test 2. Calculate the quantiles of the empirical data.
3. Calculate the corresponding quantiles for this parametric distribution (using the inverse of the cumulative distribution function).
4. Plot the empirical quantiles against the theoretical quantiles.

If the points in the Q-Q plot lie roughly along a straight line, it suggests that the data generating process could be the parametric distribution. Quantile-Quantile plots 64 are an important tool to select a theoretical distribution to apply on a given sample. It has both the advantage and the drawback to be a visual tool.
In our approach, we assume that we are observing realisations x t from a random variable X with theoretical probability distribution function F , a quantile of order p is defined as 65
div.formula: Q(p) = F -1 (p) = min x ∈ R : F (x) ≥ p = min x ∈ R : P r(X < x) ≥ p
div.p: If we have n observations, we can write:
div.formula: Value x 1 x 2 . . . x n Probability 1 n 1 n . . . 1 n
div.p: If there are i, j such that x i = x j , then we regroup them into y 1 , . . . , y k for k ≤ n, we now have, with n j the number of x's regrouped in y j :
div.formula: Value y 1 y 2 . . . y k Probability n 1 n n 2 n . . . n k n
div.p: We define the empirical distribution function (EDF) as
div.formula: Fn (x) = 1 n n i=1 1 {X i ≤x} ,
div.p: where 1 {X i ≤x} denotes the indicator function.
According to the law of large number 66 , this empirical function converges to the theoretical repartition function in probability.
We can now define the empirical quantile of order p:
div.formula: Qn (p) = min{x ∈ R : Fn (x) ≥ p} Qn (p) converges in probability to Q(p).
div.p: Given an ordered sample of independent observations:
A quantile-quantile plot consists of the points:
64 vansteenberghe_qqplots.py 65 when the random variable X is continuous, then P r(X < x) = P r(X ≤ x) 66 cf. section 19

div.head: Q-Q plot from a standard normal population
div.p: If we take n observations from a standard normal population, we can order those observations (we should also regroup the observations of same values). Then for each ordered observation i, we can compute the quantile of the theoretical standard normal distribution of probability i n .
div.formula: 3 2 1 0 1 2 3 empirical quantiles 3 2 1 0 1 2 3 theoretical quantiles

div.head: Linear regression on weight and height
div.p: We study herefoot_27 linear regression estimated by Ordinary Least Squares (OLS).

div.head: Linear regression
div.p: Definition. Linear regression models the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more explanatory variables. In the simple linear case, the model writes
div.formula: y i = β 0 + β 1 x i + ε i , i = 1, . . . , n,
div.p: where:
• y i is the dependent variable (e.g. weight),
• x i is the explanatory variable (e.g. height),
• β 0 is the intercept,
• β 1 is the slope coefficient,
• ε i is the error term.
In matrix form:
div.formula: y = Xβ + ε.
div.p: In Python or R, the formula notation is: y ~x which implicitly includes an intercept. To estimate a regression without an intercept:
div.formula: y ~x -1
div.p: Omitting the intercept imposes the restriction E(y|x = 0) = 0. If this restriction is false, the model is misspecified and slope estimates may be biased. It is generally unclear why an economist would estimate a regression without including an intercept. In academic papers, the intercept is sometimes omitted from the tables for brevity, but it was nevertheless included in the actual estimation. In such cases no bias arises, since the intercept is estimated but simply not reported because it is not relevant for the specific point being discussed.

div.head: Classical linear regression assumptions
div.p: We state the standard assumptions underlying OLS in the cross-sectional case.

div.head: (A1) Linearity in parameters
div.p: The model is linear in the coefficients:
div.formula: y i = β 0 + β 1 x i + ε i .
div.p: Nonlinear transformations of x i are allowed (e.g. x 2 i , log x i ), provided the model remains linear in β.
(A2) Random sampling
div.formula: {(y i , x i )} n
div.p: i=1 is a random sample from the population.

div.head: (A3) No perfect multicollinearity
div.p: The regressors are not perfectly linearly dependent. In the simple regression case, this requires
(A4) Zero conditional mean (exogeneity)
div.formula: E(ε i | x i ) = 0.
div.p: This is the key assumption ensuring unbiasedness of OLS. It implies
It is not tested by a t-test on residuals; rather, it is a structural assumption about the datagenerating process.
(A5) Homoskedasticity
div.formula: Var(ε i | x i ) = σ 2 .
div.p: Under homoskedasticity, OLS is the Best Linear Unbiased Estimator (BLUE). A common diagnostic is the Breusch-Pagan test based on
div.formula: ε2 i = α + γx i + ν i ,
div.p: with null hypothesis H 0 : γ = 0.
(A6) No autocorrelation (for ordered data)
div.formula: Cov(ε i , ε j | X) = 0, i ̸ = j.
div.p: In time series settings, this can be assessed using Durbin-Watson or Breusch-Godfrey tests.

div.head: Normality (for exact finite-sample inference)
div.p: For exact t and F inference in small samples, one may additionally assume
Normality is not required for unbiasedness or consistency, but simplifies hypothesis testing in finite samples.

div.head: Properties of the OLS estimator
div.p: Under (A1)-(A4):
• β is unbiased:
div.formula: E( β) = β;
div.p: • β is consistent:
div.formula: β p - → β.
div.p: Under (A1)-(A5), OLS is BLUE (Gauss-Markov theorem). With normality, exact finite-sample inference based on t and F statistics is valid.

div.head: Deriving β1 and showing
div.p: Consider the simple linear regression model
div.formula: y i = β 0 + β 1 x i + ε i , i = 1, . . . , n,
div.p: and define the OLS estimators ( β0 , β1 ) as the minimizers of the sum of squared residuals:
div.formula: ( β0 , β1 ) = arg min β 0 ,β 1 S(β 0 , β 1 ), S(β 0 , β 1 ) = n i=1 y i -β 0 -β 1 x i 2 .
div.p: Step 1: First-order conditions (FOC)
Differentiate S(β 0 , β 1 ) with respect to β 0 and β 1 .
FOC w.r.t. β 0 .

div.head: ∂S ∂β
div.formula: 0 = n i=1 2 y i -β 0 -β 1 x i (-1) = -2 n i=1 y i -β 0 -β 1 x i = 0. Equivalently, n i=1 y i -nβ 0 -β 1 n i=1 x i = 0 =⇒ β0 = ȳ -β1 x, where ȳ = 1 n n i=1 y i and x = 1 n n i=1 x i . FOC w.r.t. β 1 . ∂S ∂β 1 = n i=1 2 y i -β 0 -β 1 x i (-x i ) = -2 n i=1 x i y i -β 0 -β 1 x i = 0.
div.p: Step 2: Solve for β1
Substitute β0 = ȳ -β1 x into the second FOC:
div.formula: n i=1 x i y i -(ȳ -β1 x) -β1 x i = 0. Use n i=1 x i (y i -ȳ) = n i=1 (x i -x)(y i -ȳ) (because n i=1 (y i -ȳ) = 0), and note that n i=1 x i (x i -x) = n i=1 (x i -x) 2 .
div.p: Therefore,
div.formula: n i=1 (x i -x)(y i -ȳ) -β1 n i=1 (x i -x) 2 = 0, so that β1 = n i=1 (x i -x)(y i -ȳ) n i=1 (x i -x) 2
div.p: Step 3: Express β1 in terms of β 1 and the errors
Start from the model and its sample mean:
div.formula: y i = β 0 + β 1 x i + ε i , ȳ = β 0 + β 1 x + ε, ε = 1 n n i=1 ε i .
div.p: Subtract the mean equation from the individual equation:
div.formula: y i -ȳ = β 1 (x i -x) + (ε i -ε).
div.p: Plug this into the expression of β1 :
div.formula: β1 = n i=1 (x i -x) β 1 (x i -x) + (ε i -ε) n i=1 (x i -x) 2 = β 1 n i=1 (x i -x) 2 + n i=1 (x i -x)(ε i -ε) n i=1 (x i -x) 2 = β 1 + n i=1 (x i -x)(ε i -ε) n i=1 (x i -x) 2
div.p: . Now simplify the numerator:
div.formula: n i=1 (x i -x)(ε i -ε) = n i=1 (x i -x)ε i - ε n i=1 (x i -x). But n i=1 (x i -x) = 0, hence n i=1 (x i -x)(ε i -ε) = n i=1 (x i -x)ε i .
div.p: Therefore the key decomposition is
div.formula: β1 = β 1 + n i=1 (x i -x)ε i n i=1 (x i -x) 2 .
div.p: Step 4: Unbiasedness of β1
Take expectations conditional on X = (x 1 , . . . , x n ). Under the exogeneity assumption E(ε i | X) = 0, we obtain
div.formula: E( β1 | X) = β 1 + n i=1 (x i -x)E(ε i | X) n i=1 (x i -x) 2 = β 1 .
div.p: Hence, by iterated expectations,

div.head: Variance of β1 and convergence to zero
div.p: Recall the decomposition (simple regression with intercept):
div.formula: β1 = β 1 + n i=1 (x i -x)ε i n i=1 (x i -x) 2 .
div.p: Define the shorthand
div.formula: S xx = n i=1 (x i -x) 2 . Then β1 -β 1 = 1 S xx n i=1 (x i -x)ε i .

div.head: Conditional variance given X
div.p: Compute the variance conditional on X = (x 1 , . . . , x n ):
div.formula: Var( β1 | X) = Var 1 S xx n i=1 (x i -x)ε i X = 1 S 2 xx Var n i=1 (x i -x)ε i X .
div.p: Assume (i) homoskedasticity and (ii) no conditional correlation:
div.formula: Var(ε i | X) = σ 2 , Cov(ε i , ε j | X) = 0 (i ̸ = j). Then V ar n i=1 (x i -x)ε i X = n i=1 (x i -x) 2 V ar(ε i | X) = σ 2 n i=1 (x i -x) 2 = σ 2 S xx . Therefore, V ar( β1 | X) = σ 2 S xx .

div.head: Unconditional variance (optional)
div.p: By the law of total variance,
div.formula: V ar( β1 ) = E V ar( β1 | X) + V ar E( β1 | X) .
div.p: Under exogeneity, E( β1 | X) = β 1 , so the second term is zero and
div.formula: V ar( β1 ) = E σ 2 S xx .
div.p: For consistency, it is typically enough to work with the conditional variance.
As n → ∞, the variance goes to zero
Rewrite S xx as
div.formula: S xx = n • 1 n n i=1 (x i -x) 2 .
div.p: Under random sampling with V ar(x i ) = σ 2 x ∈ (0, ∞), we have the probability limit
div.formula: 1 n n i=1 (x i -x) 2 p - → σ 2 x , so S xx p - → nσ 2 x . Hence, V ar( β1 | X) = σ 2 S xx ≈ σ 2 nσ 2 x , which implies V ar( β1 | X) p - → 0 and in particular V ar( β1 ) = O 1 n → 0.
div.p: Interpretation. As the sample grows, S xx increases proportionally to n (provided x has nonzero variance), so the sampling variability of β1 shrinks at rate 1/n. This is the core variance argument behind the consistency of OLS slope estimates.

div.head: R-square and t-student "interpretation"
div.p: For a simple 69 regression model:
div.formula: y i = βx i + ϵ i ,
div.p: T the length of our data set, our Ordinary Least Square programme is:
div.formula: min β i (y i -βx i ) 2
div.p: With first order conditions, we can find the estimate of β and we could prove that it is linear, unbiased, and efficient:
div.formula: β = i x i y i i x 2 i (63
div.p: 69 Here, we omit the intercept to simplify the mathematics, but in fact you need to work with an intercept and the mathematics is just slightly longer to write down.
We can demonstrate that, if var(ϵ) = σ 2 :
div.formula: var(β) = σ 2 i x 2 i
div.p: The best estimate for the standard deviation of the residuals is:
div.formula: σ2 = i ( ŷi -y i ) 2 T -2
div.p: And the standard deviation for the estimated parameters are 70
div.formula: σβ 0 2 = σ2 i xi 2 T i x 2 i -( i x i ) 2 σβ 1 2 = σ2 T T i x 2 i -( i x i ) 2
div.p: By decomposing the variance and with enough observations, it can be demonstrated that the total variance is decomposed by the variance of the model and the error terms:
div.formula: var(y i ) = var( ŷi ) + var( εi )
div.p: we then define R 2 as the ratio between the variance of the model and the total variance: 1], the closest to 1, the more explanatory our model (if our econometric methods were correct 71 ), meaning the ability of our model to explain the movements of the dependent variable. The t statistic for a coefficient is defined as:
div.formula: R 2 = i ( ŷi -ȳ) 2 i (y i -ȳ) 2 = i ( ŷi -ȳ) 2 i ( ŷi -ȳ) 2 + i (y i -ŷi ) 2 R 2 ∈ [0,
t = β σ β
div.p: This statistics follow a Student law with N -k degree of freedom (N being the number of observations and k the number of estimated parameters). The null hypothesis H 0 is : β = 0, we can use a threshold of 5%, as it is two-sided, we search for t * such that: Prob(X < t * ) = 0.025
If |t| > t * , then we can reject H 0 , again, if our econometric methods were correct, x has a significant influence over y.
For a model with an intercept, the results become:
div.formula: β1 = i x i (y i -ȳ) i x i (x i -x) (64) β0 = ȳ -β1 x
div.p: And we can show that: R 2 = corr(yi, xi) 2 70 more details can be found here 71 for example, if the series have not a zero mean and we nonetheless force the intercept to be null, then the R 2 will seems larger than what the explanatory power of the model
div.formula: • Question 23
div.p: -Compute the variance of β, the R 2 and the t statistic. Compare the results from the linear regression with and without constant term (intercept).
We might want to link the weight of a person with her height:
As we will describe in more detail section 76.8, we do not seem to validate the homoscedasticity hypothesis, we take the log of weight so our data set better fit this hypothesis:

• Question 24 -Sort the data set by ascending heights. Do two regressions on the first and second half of the sorted data set. Compare the residual distribution for each regression, this should give you a hint about homoscedasticity.
We assume that log(w) and h follow given Data Generating Processes and might want to fit a linear regression:
div.formula: log (w i ) = α + βh i + ϵ i (65)
div.p: From the literature on the subject, we know we are missing explanatory variables (parents' height and weight at a minimum, but we do not have that information).

div.head: Log-linear Regression
div.p: Linear regressions are powerful tools, but they often stumble when the relationship between variables isn't strictly linear or when the data is highly skewed. One of the strategies to mitigate such issues is the log-linear regression.

The logarithm is a monotonic and concave function. This concavity property aids in reducing the skewness in datasets (for further details, refer to Section 22.2). By transforming the dependent variable with a logarithm, we can often achieve a more linear relationship with the predictor and ensure that the assumptions of linear regression are better met. This improved linearity often translates into more efficient and robust regression estimates.
For our study of the relationship between weight and height, the log-linear regression is particularly useful. The relationship can be expressed as:
div.formula: log(y) = α + βx + ϵ(66)
div.p: Where:
• log(y) is the natural logarithm of the dependent variable.
• x is the independent variable (in our case, height).
• α and β are the intercept and slope coefficients, respectively.
• ϵ is the error term.
Illustration: We hypothesize that the logarithm of an individual's weight, log(w i ), is linearly related to their height, h i . Our model is:
div.formula: log(w i ) = α + βh i + ϵ i
div.p: This model suggests that the percentage change in weight is linearly related to changes in height. Cohn et al. (2022) in their Takeaway 2, demonstrate that bias due to heteroskedastic model error can cause log-linear regression coefficients to have the wrong sign. For this, they suppose that y = exp (βx) η where x is normally distributed and η is log-normally distributed with a standard deviation depending on the realization of x (this is the modeling of heteroskedasticity), σ η (x) = exp (δx) with δ a constant and they show that if δ > 2β then this bias can cause the sign flip. Note on Model Completeness: From prior research, we understand that crucial explanatory variables, such as parents' height and weight, influence an individual's weight. However, in this model, we don't account for these variables due to a lack of data. Such omission may lead to omitted variable bias. Dep. Variable: logv437 R-squared: 0.197 Model: OLS Adj. R-squared: 0.197 Method: Least Squares F-statistic: 3801. Date: Wed, 25 Mar 2020 Prob (F-statistic): 0.00 Time: 16:16:21 Log-Likelihood: 4646.1 No. Observations: 15494 AIC: -9288. Df Residuals: 15492 BIC: -9273. Df Model: 1 coef std err t P> |t| [0.025 0.975] Intercept 4.2587 0.033 127.231 0.000 4.193 4.324 v438 0.0013 2.13e-05 61.654 0.000 0.001 0.001 Omnibus: 1317.052 Durbin-Watson: 1.664 Prob(Omnibus): 0.000 Jarque-Bera (JB): 1972.605 Skew: 0.669 Prob(JB): 0.00 Kurtosis: 4.125 Cond. No.

div.head: 3.66e+04
div.p: Given the regression output:
-Intercept (α): 4.2587. This is the expected logarithm of weight for an individual with a height of 0 (which is not meaningful in real-world context but provides a baseline for the model).
div.formula: Coefficient of h i (β): 0.0013. Because d log w i dx = 1 w i dw i
div.p: dh i , β measures the proportional change in y: a one-unit increase in h i increases w i by about 100 × β = 0.13%, holding other factors constant.

-R-squared: 0.197. This means only 19.7% of the variability in the logarithm of weight is explained by height based on this model. Given the statistically significant p-values for both intercept and slope, we infer that the relationship between height and the logarithm of weight is statistically significant. However, with an R-squared value of 0.197, height explains a relatively small fraction of the variation in the logarithm of weight.

div.head: Log (one plus)-linear regression
div.p: When a data set has many 0s (e.g. 69% fo Compustat firms are granted zero patents in a given year). To take those 0s into account, one might want to do a log1plus regression, because then 0s remain 0s with the log transformation.
div.formula: log (1 + y) = α + λx + ϵ (67)
div.p: Cohn et al. ( 2022) demonstrates that the log1plus estimates is linked to the log-linear estimates by
div.formula: λ = E [y|x] 1 + E [y|x]
div.p: β
When E [y|x] is large then the two estimates are almost equal, but the authors note that when E [y|x] is large then it is unlikely that many observations are 0s.

For illustration only, here we perform a log1plus regression and as E [y|x] is large, then the two specification, log1plus and log-linear are similar and log1plus here doesn't add any value as we do not have any real 0 in our data set.

div.head: Poisson Regression
div.p: While linear and log-linear regressions are commonly used for continuous data, count data or non-negative integer values often demand different models. One such model is the Poisson regression, typically used for count data.

In this context, citing Cohn et al. (2022), we introduce the Poisson regression mainly for illustrative purposes. It's worth noting that while our data of weight isn't typical 'count' data, the Poisson regression can still provide valuable insights. The specification of the Poisson regression model is:
div.formula: log (E[y|x]) = α + ωx + ν(68)
div.p: Where:
• E[y|x] represents the expected value of y given x.

div.head: • log (E[y|x]
div.p: ) is the natural logarithm of the expected value of the dependent variable given the predictor.
• x is the independent variable.
• α and ω are the intercept and slope coefficients, respectively.
• ν is the error term.

It's crucial to interpret the coefficients of Poisson regression in terms of percentage change, given its multiplicative nature. Bellego et al. (2022) provide some criticsfoot_28 of the Poisson approach and suggest an alternative iOLS that goes back to log (one plus)-linear regression.

div.head: Random Forest regressor
div.p: We can also use machine learning techniques, but these will be oriented toward answering the question: given a height h i what is the predicted weight w i , but the model being nonparametric, we cannot easily derive a sensitivity.
As an illustration, we fit a Random Forest regression model on the sample, we might want to restrict our fit not to overfit:
1300 1400 1500 1600 1700 1800 1900 2000 height 5.8 6.0 6.2 6.4 6.6 6.8 predicted log weight OLS RF RF restricted
knowing its limitation, the linear model allows an interpretation of the impact of height on weight.

div.head: Stationarity, Unit Roots
div.p: Stationarity, Dickey-Fuller test (original and augmented), auto-regressions 73 . Before introducing the theory of stationarity and the estimation of AR, MA, ARMA, ARIMA, SARIMA, ARIMA-GARCH, and SARIMA-GARCH models, we address a set of frequently asked questions.

• What is the appropriate order of transformations and model fitting for a univariate time series? -Primary recommendation. Conduct a thorough literature review and replicate, as closely as possible, the transformations and specifications adopted in top academic journals for comparable time series. Statistical testing should be used to validate that similar treatments are appropriate for your data.

-In the absence of guidance from the literature. This typically indicates a novel dataset. In that case, transformations and dynamic components should be specified jointly, rather than sequentially. 74 * If the series exhibits seasonality but no clear deterministic trend, estimate a SARIMA model. 75 Recall that a SARIMA model for a non-stationary series is equivalent to an ARMA model applied to the appropriately differenced (stationary) series.

* If a trend is present, first-order differencing is often sufficient. As a conservative approach, estimating a SARIMA model allows one to address both integration (I) and potential seasonality (S) simultaneously.
* If conditional heteroskedasticity is observed, consider variance-stabilizing transformations such as the Box-Cox transformation, of which the logarithmic transformation is a special case.
-The Box-Jenkins methodology provides a useful general framework, though it should not be applied mechanically.
• What if the time series is intended for use in a multivariate model?
-If available, use the seasonally adjusted series.
-If no adjusted series exists and the literature is silent, begin with a univariate analysis of the raw series to understand its properties, typically starting with a SARIMA-GARCH specification.
Formal decomposition of a SARIMA-GARCH model. Let {y t } denote a univariate time series. A SARIMA(p, d, q) × (P, D, Q) s -GARCH(r, s) model can be written as
div.formula: Φ P (L s ) ϕ p (L) (1 -L) d (1 -L s ) D y t = Θ Q (L s ) θ q (L) ε t , ε t = σ t z t ,
div.p: where z t ∼ i.i.d. (0, 1) and σ 2 t follows a GARCH process.

73 stationarity_adf_ar.py 74 This is analogous to the ARMA(p, q)-GARCH(r, s) case (Section 77), where one should not estimate an ARMA model first and a GARCH model on the residuals afterward, but rather estimate an ARMA-GARCH system simultaneously. 75 For pedagogical purposes, we often proceed step by step: (i) remove seasonality, (ii) test for unit roots and difference if needed, (iii) estimate an ARMA-GARCH model.
• Autoregressive (AR) component. An AR(p) process captures linear dependence on past realizations:
div.formula: ϕ p (L)y t = p i=1 ϕ i y t-i .
div.p: • Moving-average (MA) component. An MA(q) process models dependence on past shocks:
div.formula: θ q (L)ε t = q j=1
div.p: θ j ε t-j .
• Integrated (I) component. Differencing operators (1 -L) d and (1 -L s ) D remove stochastic trends and seasonal unit roots, respectively.
• Seasonal (S) component. Seasonal AR and MA polynomials Φ P (L s ) and Θ Q (L s ) capture dependence at multiples of the seasonal frequency s.
• GARCH component. Conditional variance dynamics are modeled as
div.formula: σ 2 t = ω + r i=1 α i ε 2 t-i + s j=1 β j σ 2 t-j ,
div.p: allowing for time-varying volatility and volatility clustering.

div.head: Definition (White noise).
div.p: A stochastic process {ε t } t∈Z is said to be a white-noise process with mean zero and variance σ 2 ε , denoted
div.formula: ε t ∼ WN(0, σ 2 ε ),
div.p: if it satisfies:
1. Zero mean:
div.formula: E[ε t ] = 0 for all t;
div.p: 2. Constant variance:
div.formula: Var(ε t ) = σ 2 ε for all t;
div.p: 3. No serial correlation:
div.formula: Cov(ε t , ε t-k ) = 0 for all k ̸ = 0.
div.p: Definition (Moving Average process). A time series {y t } t∈Z is said to follow a moving-average process of order q, denoted MA(q), if it can be written as
div.formula: y t = µ + ε t + q j=1 θ j ε t-j , ε t ∼ WN(0, σ 2 ).

div.head: Concept of stationarity
div.p: Before regressing (or for AR), we need to go step by step on the integration order and the cointegrationfoot_29 of the variables.
A time series {y t } is said to be weakly stationary (or second-order stationary) if it satisfies the following conditions for all time points t and for all lags s:
1. The mean E(y t ) is constant over time, that is,
2. The variance Var(y t ) is finite and constant over time, that is,
3. The covariance between y t and y t+s depends only on the lag s and is constant over time, that is,
div.formula: Cov(y t , y t+s ) = γ(s) < ∞, ∀t.(71)
div.p: These conditions ensure that the time series has stable moments (mean and variance) across different time points and does not exhibit trends or seasonality in its first and second moments.
It can be shownfoot_30 that the following empirical moments are consistent estimators of the above moments when T → ∞:
div.formula:      μ = 1 T T t=1 y t γ0 = 1 T T t=1 (y t -μ) 2 γs = 1 T -s T t=1+s (y t -μ) (y t-s -μ) with T ≫ s (72)
div.p: The most basic weakly stationary processes are white noises and moving averages. A time series {y t } is said to be strictly stationaryfoot_31 if the joint distribution of (y t , y t+1 , . . . , y t+k ) is identical to (y t+h , y t+h+1 , . . . , y t+h+k ) for all time points t and for all lags h and k, that is, F (y t , y t+1 , . . . , y t+k ) = F (y t+h , y t+h+1 , . . . , y t+h+k ), ∀t, h, k.
In other words, the distribution of the series does not change over time when shifted along the time axis, making the series consistent in distributional properties at all time points.

div.head: Concept of Integration of order d
div.p: If ∆ is the difference operator defined as ∆y t = y t -y t-1 , ∆ 2 y t = ∆(∆y t ), etc., we say that y t is integrated of order d or I(d) if y t , ∆y t , . . ., ∆ d-1 y t are non-stationary and ∆ d y t is stationaryfoot_32 .

div.head: Wold decomposition theorem
div.p: The Wold Decomposition Theorem plays a pivotal role in the theory of stationary time series. This theorem essentially describes any stationary time series as a combination of two orthogonal components: a deterministic component and a stochastic component. Let {y t } be a zero-mean weakly stationary time series. According to the Wold Decomposition Theorem, {y t } can be decomposed as
div.formula: y t = ∞ j=0 b j ε t-j + η t ,(74)
div.p: where
div.formula: • {ε t } is a white noise process, implying that E(ε t ) = 0, Var(ε t ) = σ 2 < ∞, and Cov(ε t , ε s ) = 0 for t ̸ = s,
div.p: • {b j } are coefficients such that the series ∞ j=0 b 2 j converges, assuring that the linear combination of white noise processes is well-defined,
• {η t } is a deterministic component that could represent a polynomial trend or seasonality,
• and {ε t } and {η t } are uncorrelated processes, meaning that Cov(ε t , η s ) = 0 for all t and s.

The first component ∞ j=0 b j ε t-j is known as the innovations or moving-average representation of {y t }. The deterministic component η t is typically of less interest and is often taken as zero, especially when the focus is on the stochastic properties of the time series. More compact wording for part of this theorem:
div.formula: ϵ ∼ WN(0, σ 2 ) and ∞ i=0 |b i | < ∞.
div.p: What does it mean for an infinite sum to converge? An infinite sum ∞ j=0 a j is said to converge if the sequence of partial sums
div.formula: S N ≡ N j=0 a j
div.p: approaches a finite limit as N → ∞. Intuitively, this means that adding more and more terms eventually has a negligible effect on the total sum.

div.head: Deterministic intuition. Consider the geometric series
div.p: Each additional term becomes smaller and smaller, and the total sum converges to 1/(1 -ρ).
In contrast, if |ρ| ≥ 1, the terms do not decay fast enough and the sum diverges.

div.head: Impulse Response Function
div.p: Impulse Response Function aims at answering how much our process y t is affected at time t by an innovation (a shock) that happened h periods before t.
The Impulse Response Function traces the effect of a one-unit shock to ε t on the time series y t at various time horizons. Mathematically, the h-step-ahead impulse response is given by
where h represents the horizon of interest.

Graphically, the IRF represents the response of y t to a one-unit impulse in ε t , holding all other past ε t-i constant at zero, illustrating how an external shock or innovation permeates and affects the time series over subsequent periods.

From the Wold theorem, for a stationary process the innovation at time t -h is ϵ t-h and the Impulse Response Function if ∂yt ∂ϵ t-h which is thus b h . Consider a linear time series model (like an ARMA model):
div.formula: y t = ϕ(L)y t-1 + θ(L)ε t ,
div.p: where y t is the time series, ϕ(L) and θ(L) represent polynomial lag operators, L is the lag operator, and ε t represents white noise.

div.head: AR(1) stationary process
div.p: We consider a stationary AR(1):
div.formula: y t = θy t-1 + ϵ t(75)
div.p: with 0 < |θ| < 1 and ϵ ∼ N (0, 1). We can rewrite:
div.formula: y t = t-1 i=1 θ i ϵ t-i + θ t y 0 we have:      E (y t ) = θ t y 0 → 0 V ar (y t ) = t-1 i=1 θ 2i σ 2 → 1 1-θ 2 σ 2 Cov (y t , y t-s ) = θ s σ 2 1-θ 2
div.p: We say that an AR( 1) is asymptotically stationary, and usually just say stationary. Another way to look at it, is that if this process is to be weakly stationary, then, we need:
div.formula:    µ(1 -θ) = 0, as E (ϵ t ) = 0 γ 0 (1 -θ 2 ) = σ 2 Cov (y t , y t-s ) = γ s which has solution if 0 < |θ| < 1,
div.p: as a variance is necessarily positive. We can rewrite:
div.formula:    µ = 0 γ 0 = σ 2 1-θ 2 Cov (y t , y t-s ) = γ s
div.p: If θ = 1 either we have no solution, either σ = 0, which would then mean that the error term is a constant ∀t, ϵ t = 0 and then ∀t, y t = y 0 which is stationary but presents no interest for study.

From our initial exploration of the AR(1) model, we can derive certain insights, especially when applied to financial data like asset prices. First, to ensure that E (y t ) → 0, we should refrain from using the price levels directly. If we were to use them, it might erroneously suggest a model where all prices converge to zero, implying a rather grim scenario where every company defaults. Instead of price levels, it's more practical and meaningful to work with price returns, thereby de-trending the data to respect the condition E (y t ) → 0. For the constant volatility assumption inherent in the AR(1) model, let's consider the CAC 40 index time series introduced in Section 13.4. Upon visual inspection, one can observe periods of 'volatility clustering', which implies that volatility isn't constant through time. This observation suggests that the basic AR(1) model might not be sufficient to capture the underlying dynamics of such financial time series. To address this, more advanced models that account for evolving volatility, such as the ARCH (Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity) model, become necessary. 1 9 9 2 1 9 9 6 2 0 0 0 2 0 0 4 2 0 0 8 2 0 1 2 2 0 1 6 date 0.100 0.075 0.050 0.025 0.000 0.025 0.050 0.075 0.100 CAC 40 net return close

div.head: Finite-order lag polynomial
div.p: Equation 75 can be written using the lag operator L as
div.formula: (1 -θL)y t = ε t .
div.p: The root of the autoregressive polynomial (1 -θL) is L = 1/θ. If |θ| < 1, this root lies outside the unit circle, and the lag polynomial is invertible. 80 In that case,
and equation 75 admits the moving-average representation
div.formula: y t = ∞ i=0 θ i ε t-i .
div.p: Since ∞ i=0 |θ| i < ∞, the process {y t } has finite variance and is weakly stationary. This generalizes to an AR(p) process written in lag-polynomial form as
div.formula: Φ(L)y t = ε t , Φ(L) ≡ 1 -ϕ 1 L -• • • -ϕ p L p .
div.p: 80 The polynomial ϕ(L) = 1 -θL is called the autoregressive polynomial of {yt}.
Let {λ k } p k=1 denote the roots of the characteristic equation
div.formula: Φ(z) = 0. If |λ k | > 1 for all k = 1, . . . , p,
div.p: then Φ(L) can be factorized as
div.formula: Φ(L) = p k=1 (1 -λ k L),
div.p: and therefore
div.formula: Φ(L) -1 = p k=1 (1 -λ k L) -1 . Since |λ k | > 1 implies |λ -1 k | < 1
div.p: , each factor admits a geometric-series expansion,
so the product is a well-defined power series in L,
div.formula: Φ(L) -1 = ∞ i=0 a i L i .
div.p: Moreover, the coefficients {a i } decay geometrically, so in particular
and the AR(p) process admits the MA(∞) representation y t = ∞ i=0 a i ε t-i , implying weak stationarity.

div.head: Process with a unit root: a non-stationary process
div.p: If we follow section 24.3 but now set θ = 1, y t = y t-1 + ϵ t which is a Random Walk and chose y 0 = 100. We can rewrite the process with L the lag operator, (1 -L)y t = ϵ t , the root of the equation 1 -x is 1, hence we say that this process has a unit root. We can rewrite y t = t-1 i=1 ϵ t-i + y 0 and find that V ar (y t ) = tσ 2 → ∞ which is against our definition of weak stationarity.
• Question 25 -create a stationary AR(1) (θ < 1) and a unit root one, with 1000 observation for each. Compute the variance of the series and compare them to the expected variance as described above.
for the stationary AR( 1), introduce at time 500 an additional shock (innovation) and evaluate the impulse response function at time 505.

In simple terms, a time series has a unit root, or follows an integrated process, if shocks have permanent effects on the level, but not on the rate of change in the variable. Indeed, if at a time t choc the time series experience a shock ∆ = 50, then the future level of y t will be impacted (∀t ≥ t choc ) but not the rate of change y t+1 -yt yt . For illustration, we apply a shock ∆ at mid-period to our time series: In its simplest form, the Dickey-Fuller test the null hypothesis H 0 : ρ -1 = 0 with a Student test over the equation, with no autoregressive process:
Which can be re-written:
div.formula: ∆y t = γy t-1 + ϵ t(77)
div.p: The statistics γ Standard Error(γ) can be tested again the threshold of the standard t-distribution, this is discussed and confirm in (MacKinnon, 2010) for the simple version of the test. To find the critical value, you cant import the t-distribution function from the stats package and define a probability threshold and degree of freedomfoot_33 . This test is not powerful enough to have full confidence as there is likely to be autoregressive component in time series: use an augmented test.

div.head: Augmented Dickey-Fuller test
div.p: The Augmented Dickey-Fuller test can be used to test whether a series is stationary, and take autoregressive effects into account. H 0 : γ = 0. The formula being used for the augmented Dickey-Fuller test is of the form:
div.formula: ∆y t = β 0 + β 1 t + β 2 t 2 + γy t-1 + p-1 j=1 δ j ∆y t-j + ϵ t(78)
div.p: (MacKinnon, 2010) discuss the number of lags, selected via information criteria methods, and discuss the test statistics for the "no-constant" ('n'), "no-trend" ('c'), and "with-trend" ('ct') version of this test with reference to the table to be used.
With the python function adfuller in statsmodels, you can chose the parameter of the regression:
• 'c' : constant only (default),
• 'ct' : constant and trend β 2 = 0
• 'ctt' : constant, and linear and quadratic trend
div.formula: • 'n' : no constant, no trend β 0 = β 1 = β 2 = 0
div.p: The output of the test are:
• The statistics
• The p-value
• The number of lags used for the test
• The number of observations used
• The critical values with the null hypothesis: H 0 : the time series has a unit root.

To apply this test, you need to have a "testing strategy" as suggested in (Elder and Kennedy, 2001) which "exploits known information about the growth character of the variable under investigation". So:

• if your original times series is evolving erratically around a non-zero mean like the unemployment rate, i.e. it doesn't grow in the long run (β 0 ̸ = 0), then you do not assume any drift in the first difference and on conduct an F test on the joint null that β 0 = 0 and γ = 0;
• if the original times series has a constant rate of growth (β 1 ̸ = 0), we say that the series have a drift, therefore conduct a F test on the joint null that β 1 = 0 and γ = 0;
• (Elder and Kennedy, 2001) discard the option β 1 ̸ = 0 as it implies an ever increasing (or decreasing) rate of change.

Additional data, you can refer to the book (Lütkepohl and Krätzig, 2004) and the detailed answer posted here. (Elder and Kennedy, 2001) stress the importance of the correct model as if the trend term is erroneously omitted, the tests are biased toward finding a unit root. This is easy to explain. If there is a trend but no trend term included in the regression, the only way the regression can capture the trend is by estimating a unit root and using the intercept (drift) to reflect the trend. On the other hand, including a trend term in the regression when it is inappropriate reduces the power of the unit root tests, for the same reason that including irrelevant explanatory variables increases variance.
The monthly variables of unemployment, population and GDP are integrated of order 1.

div.head: Thorough augmented Dickey-Fuller test
div.p: If no obvious conclusion can be taken from observations of the data set, then we can resort to a thorough procedure for the augmented Dickey-Fuller test, with three competing models:
div.formula: ∆y t = β 0 + β 1 t + γy t-1 + p-1 j=1 δ j ∆y t-j + ϵ t (79
∆y t = β 0 + γy t-1 + q-1 j=1 δ j ∆y t-j + ϵ t (80
∆y t = γy t-1 + r-1 j=1 δ j ∆y t-j + ϵ t(81)
div.p: For each model, the lag order (p, q or r) can be selected by t-values of information criteria and one should check that with the chosen lag order the residuals have no serial correlation. Then one starts following (Dolado et al., 1990) with the model 79 and tests for the null of β 1 if the null is rejected, the augmented Dickey-Fuller test can be performed with this model. If the null is not rejected, then one move on to model 80. Note that for the null tests we must use the asymptotic distributions tabulated in (Dickey and Fuller, 1981). With the model 80, one tests for the null of β 0 , if the null is rejected, the aumented Dickey-Fuller test can be performed with this model. If the null is not rejected, then one move on to model 81 and perform the augmented Dickey-Fuller test with this model.

div.head: Illustration of a spurious regression
div.p: We can define two time series as in (Granger and Newbold, 1974):
div.formula: y t = y t-1 + ϵ t x t = x t-1 + ν t
div.p: with ϵ t and ν t two independent white noise from a normal law and we set y 0 = 0 and x 0 = 0. When regressing:
div.formula: y t = α + βx t + γ t
div.p: one would expect the β not to be significantly different than 0 and the R 2 of the regression to be low. This is not the case as both series present a unit root.
The OLS estimator for β is: βOLS = (X ′ X) -1 X ′ y.
Given the non-stationary nature of both series the quadratic mean grows with time (see also the formula section 24.3 to get a sense of why this variance tends to infinity with a unit root):
div.formula: E(y 2 t ) ∝ T, E(x 2 t ) ∝ T.
div.p: Thus, as T → ∞, the variances of both series grow without bound. The covariance between the two series will also grow with T due to their shared trending behavior, affecting the X ′ y term. Given the nature of the OLS estimator:
div.formula: βOLS = (X ′ X) -1 X ′ y,
div.p: the matrices X ′ X and X ′ y grow to infinity due to the non-stationary nature of x t and y t . This growth can lead to non-convergent behavior of βOLS even if the true β is zero (also get back to equation 64 to get a sense of this result). Moreover, the variance of the OLS estimator (given by the diagonal elements of the matrix σ2 (X ′ X) -1 , where σ2 is the variance of the error term) can also be impacted by the nonstationarity of the series. For the detailed of how to compute both the estimator and its variance, cf section 23.6.1, I implement it directly in the code to illustrate how the numerator and/or the denominator can 'explode '. Phillips (1986) demonstrates that he OLS estimator does not converge in probability as the sample size increases, the t-and F-test statistics do not have well-defined asymptotic distributions and the Durbin-Watson statistic converges to zero. The reason is that, with y t and x t being I(1) variables, the error term ϵ t will also be a nonstationary I(1) variable.
We illustrate this with an example of two draws and their R 2 , coefficients, t-values and Durbin-Watson statistics:

0 200 400 600 800 1000 30 20 10 0 10 20 Two independent time series, with both a unit root yt xt R 2 19% Intercept coefficient 9.1 Intercept coefficient t-value 25.3 β 0.4 β t-value 15.2 Durbin-Watson statistics 0.038

The R 2 seems "attractive" 82 , both the intercept and β seems to have high t-value which means that they seems significantly different than 0, but there is a low Durbin-Watson statistics which suggest that the residuals of the regressions are strongly autocorrelated. These are the symptoms of two I(1) series not cointegrated being regressed on one another, a spurious regression where two independent time series seems related. So in fact the true value of β is zero but as demonstrated by Philips (1986) the estimate of β doesn't converge in probability to zero (estimates of the regression coefficients are inefficient). Also the usual significance tests on the coefficients are invalid. This condition (high R 2 and low Durbin-Watson statistics) is not a necessary condition for a regression to be spurious as demonstrated in (Granger and Newbold, 1974).

div.head: Durbin-Watson test and statistics
div.p: Autocorrelation, specifically serial correlation, refers to the correlation of a variable with itself through different time lags. This phenomenon violates the ordinary least squares (OLS) assumption that the error terms are uncorrelated, leading to inefficiency in estimators, and potentially distorting statistical inference.

The Durbin-Watson (DW) test is a formal statistical test of whether autocorrelation is present in the residuals of a regression analysis. It is commonly used in time-series data to detect the presence of serial correlation. The Durbin-Watson statistic is calculated using the following formula:
div.formula: d = T t=2 (ε t -εt-1 ) 2 T t=1 ε2 t
div.p: where:
• εt is the residual at time t,
• T is the number of observations.
The test statistic d usually lies between 0 and 4, where:
div.formula: • d ≈ 2 indicates no autocorrelation,
div.p: • d < 2 suggests positive autocorrelation,
• d > 2 suggests negative autocorrelation.

Specific critical values for the DW statistic can be found in statistical tables, which can be used to more formally accept or reject the null hypothesis of no autocorrelation. Spurious regression often occurs when working with time-series data. It refers to the scenario where two or more time series variables are correlated due to shared trends over time, rather than because there is a genuine relationship between them.

A classic symptom of spurious regression is a very high R 2 value along with significant t-ratios, despite no theoretical or logical connection between the variables. Additionally, residuals from spurious regressions often exhibit patterns, violating the assumption of no autocorrelation and potentially leading to the rejection of the null hypothesis in the Durbin-Watson test even in the absence of a meaningful relationship between the variables. Identifying and rectifying spurious regressions typically involves:
82 and the time series have a correlation of 43%
• Investigating the theoretical foundation of the relationship.
• Employing de-trending methods or differencing the series to achieve stationarity.
• Utilizing cointegration tests when dealing with non-stationary series to ascertain if a longrun relationship exists between the variables.
24.9 AR(2) process, stationarity, and unit roots
Consider the AR(2)
div.formula: y t = α + β 1 y t-1 + β 2 y t-2 + ε t ,(82)
div.p: where ε t is white noise (hence stationary).

Example β 1 = 1.6, β 2 = -0.6. Here β 1 + β 2 = 1, hence there is a unit root. Indeed the eigenvalues of A solve r 2 -1.6r + 0.6 = 0, giving r 1 = 1 and r 2 = 0.6, hence λ 1 = 1 and λ 2 = 1/0.6 ≈ 1.67. Because |λ 1 | = 1, condition 86 fails: the level y t is not stationary.
