30 Financial returns distributions and forecast 30.1 CAC 40 index data: mean and two-sided t-test, histogram
We fetch 93 CAC 40 index historical data from 1995-1-16 to 2017-1-16. We compute the daily return on business days only.

div.head: Modelling and forecasting the CAC 40 daily return
div.p: One of the simplest question we could be interested in when it comes to financial time series is: "what is its value most likely to be tomorrow?" If we assume (wrongly) that the daily returns are iid and follow a Gaussian DGP, then we only need to look at the histogram of the observed daily returns and can follow the logic presented section 18.4.1, we model the CAC 40 return as a white noise section 30.3. But if we call y t = Pt P t-1 -1 the daily returns, we can expect that the realization of y t will depend on the realization of y t-k , k ∈ N. One would take the historical data available for this time series, in our example, we have 5 638 observations from 1995-1-16 to 2017-1-16. We could choose the parameters based on the first 60% of our data set (train our model) and then verify with the remaining 40% observation that our model performs well (test our model). We might also want to cross-validate it, more on this topic can be found here.

div.head: White Noise: model and tests
div.p: A white noise is a is a sequence of independent and identically distributed random variables y t with finite mean and variance. Most of the time we take the assumption that y t is normally distribution We use the simple model:
div.formula: y t = µ + ϵ t
div.p: with ϵ t ∼ N (0, σ 2 ) and as we are working on modelling and forecasting the CAC 40 returns: µ is the mean of the daily returns.
As defined in section 24.1, this series is by definition stationary and as expected, when we perform an augmented Dickey-Fuller test: the series comes out as stationary.

div.head: Trend stationarity
div.p: We modify our model with a trend:
div.formula: y t = µ + δt + ϵ t with ϵ t ∼ N (0, σ 2 ).
div.p: We take δ = 0.1%.
We perform an augmented Dickey-Fuller test taking the trend into account: the series is stationary.
If we are interested in the significance of the trend component of this test, we might have to do it by hand. Let's consider:
div.formula: ∆y t = β 0 + β 1 t + γy t-1 + δ∆y t-1 + ϵ t 93 code: ARIMA_vansteenberghe.py
div.p: We find that in this case, the trend component is significant.
We might want to remove the trend. We can start with a first difference.
We also study quadratic trend model.

div.head: Random Walk
div.p: If we think that the CAC 40 returns are following a random walk, then:
div.formula: y t = y t-1 + ϵ t s.t. ϵ ∼ N (0, σ 2 )
div.p: Here it is just that we use the previous business day's return to predict today's return:
div.formula: E(y t ) = y t-1
div.p: This brings us to some of the measurements of forecasting errors:

div.head: Forecast error measures
div.p: • Mean Forecast Error: average the differences between the forecast and the actual data
div.formula: Mean Forecast Error = 1 N N i=1 ( ŷt -y t ) • Mean Absolute Forecast Error Mean Absolute Forecast Error = 1 N N i=1 | ŷt -y t | • Root Mean Square Forecast Error Root Mean Square Forecast Error = 1 N N i=1
div.p: ( ŷt -y t ) 2

div.head: Auto Regressive model
div.p: We follow section 24.3 that introduced AR(1) models. Before starting, intuitively, an AR(1) model would mean that y t and y t-1 related, therefore we can test for serial correlation of our daily returns, e.g. with a Ljung-Box test introduced section 25.1.

There are two reasons to ditch the CAC 40 returns for an AR( 1) model (no serial correlation, our series looks more like a withe noise) and the AR(1) parameters are not significant. For a further study of ARCH effect in CAC 40 returns, see section 76.10.1.
We introduce a stationary AR(1) on time series of better returns candidates and for stationary, we check that: 0 < |θ| < 1:
div.formula: y t = c + θy t-1 + ϵ t
div.p: with ϵ t ∼ N (0, σ 2 ), thus by definition (or assumption in our model if we apply it to a real time series) we assume that y t and ϵ t are uncorrelated. We can search for the optimal value of ϕ, once we re-ordered the AR(1), detrending the y t time series to get rid of the intercept in the AR(1):
div.formula: y t -µ = ϕ(y t-1 -µ) + ϵ t
div.p: If ϕ < 1 we can iterate backward :
div.formula: y t -µ = ∞ j=0 ϕ j ϵ t-j If we note x t = y t -µ, E t (x t ) = ϕx t-1 .
div.p: That is why AR( 1) is usually a benchmark model against which one assess the relevance of a more complicated model. If a complex model cannot beat an AR(1) forecast, then we might just stick to an AR(1) model to base our predictions on.
div.formula: γ(h) = cov(x t+h , x t ) = σ 2 ϕ h 1 -ϕ 2
div.p: Hence the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) of an AR( 1) is: 1) process is explosive.
div.formula: ρ(h) = γ(h) γ(0) = ϕ h , h ≥ 0 If |ϕ| > 1 the AR(
div.p: • Question 35 -as defined in section 24.1, show numerically that this process is weakly stationary for a certain range of values for ϕ.

div.head: AR(p)
div.p: The definition of an AR(p) model is simply:
div.formula: y t = ϕ 1 y t-1 + . . . + ϕ p y t-p + ϵ t

div.head: Moving Average model
div.p: MA(1) model:
div.formula: y t = ϵ t + θϵ t-1 with E(y t ) = µ ϵ ∼ N (µ, σ 2 ) If we note: γ(h) = cov(y t+h , y t ) γ(h) =      (1 + θ 2 )σ 2 , if h = 0 θσ 2 , if h = 1 0, if h > 1 (99)
div.p: Hence the ACF of an MA( 1) is:
We can demonstrate that there is a problem of non-uniqueness of a MA(1) model (take θ or 1 θ and an adjusted value of σ for example).

div.head: MA(q)
div.p: The definition of an MA model of order q is simply:
div.formula: y t = ϵ t + θ 1 ϵ t-1 + . . . + θ q ϵ t-q
div.p: 30.8 ACF and PACF
The autocorrelation function (ACF) of a stationary time series is:
div.formula: ρ k = corr(y t , y t+k )
div.p: We call it a partial autocorrelation function (PACF) of a stationary process when we filter out the effect of the random variables y t+1 , . . . , y t+k-1 . The PACF is used to identify the order (p) of an autoregressive model, i.e. AR(p).
div.formula: Φ k = corr(y t+k+1 -ŷt+k+1 , y t+1 -ŷt+1 )(101)
div.p: and

with ŷt+k+1 (respectively ŷt+1 ) being the orthogonal projection of y t+k+1 (respectively y t+1 ) on y t+1 , . . . , y t+k . We can use the ACF and the PACF as visual tools to identify respectively the order of the Moving Average and the Auto Regressive one:
• if for a time series the auto-correlation are non significant ∀h > q, we choose q as the order of the MA component of our model.
• if for a time series the partial auto-correlation are non significant ∀h > p, we choose p as the order of the AR component of our model.

div.head: ARMA model
div.p: A process y t is an ARMA(p,q) if it is stationary and:
div.formula: y t = ϕ 1 y t-1 + . . . + ϕ p y t-p + ϵ t + θ 1 ϵ t-1 + . . . + θ q ϵ t-q
div.p: with ϕ p ̸ = 0 and θ q ̸ = 0
We can follow the Box-Jenkins methodology:
1. identify the order of the model p and q, this can be done with the help of ACF and PACF following section 30.8. We should also investigate for seasonality, see section 26.
2. estimate the model with the maximum likelihood method 94 .
3. model residuals diagnosis, e.g. with Breusch-Godfrey test described in section 76.7.
For our time series, we reject H 0 for the Breusch-Godfrey test and residuals seems to be autocorrelated, our model is not acceptable and we would need to search for a better model.

Topics to further explore: parameters redundancy, causality, invertibility 94 by default, the ARIMA funcion maximizes the conditional sum of squares likelihood 30.9.1 Selection of the orders p and q Cairns (2000):

the simplicity postulate (often known also as the principle of parsimony). It is well known that people prefer simple models or explanations to complex ones. Jeffreys states "that the simplest law is chosen because it is most likely to give correct predictions". Here the simplest law refers to the striking of a balance between consistency of a model with the observed data and the prior degree of belief in a model. Jeffreys' view was that the prior degree of belief in a model should be inversely related to the number of parameters.
For the orders selection, section 32.2 also provide some guidelines (in the VAR context).

div.head: ARIMA
div.p: Most time series can be decomposed in a nonstationary trend component and a zero-mean stationary composant:
div.formula: y t = µ t + x t such that µ t = β 0 + β 1 t x t is stationary thus ∆y t = β 1 + ∆x t is stationary. A process x t is said to be ARIMA(p,d,q) if ∆ d x t is ARMA(p,q).

div.head: R: Box-Jenkins methodology for the ARIMA
div.p: We followfoot_37 the Box-Jenkins methodology to fit an ARIMA model as introdued in (Box and Jenkins, 1970).

div.head: Identification
div.p: 1. To identify the order of the ARIMA(p,d,q), we plot the ACF and PACF.
2. We difference the time series until the ACF looks like one of a stationary process.
3. We use the inverse autocorrelation function to check for over-differencing.
• Following the details here, we compute the dual model with the Yule-Walker method.
The ACF of this model is called the inverse autocorrelation function, if this latter decays slowly it is likely we over-differenced the original time series.

div.head: Parameters estimation
div.p: Once we identified the orders p,d and q of the ARIMA, we estimate the parameters, e.g. using AIC criterion and we can simulate our model. Again, here we are not looking for a perfect reproduction of the historical time series, rather we want to check that our model can replicate the behavior of the original time series.

div.head: Checking the model
div.p: Once estimated, we check the error terms using the Ljung-Box 96 test to test the null hypothesis: H 0 : the error terms are independent.
• Question 36
-Us the long history of U.S. GDP available here, apply the tests and then an ARIMA model. Discuss the performance of your model.
30.12 Main take away and the need for ARCH

We want to forecast the the returns and for this explore a DGP that would be 'behind' the observations. If the observations were iid, then E (y t |y t-1 ) = E (y t ) = ȳ. Here this is not the case and a simple AR(1) model beat a forecast only based on the mean. We next need to explore the residuals of this AR(1) model and check if they are iid with a 'well behaved' DGP.
If we observe the residuals ϵ t of an ARMA(1,1) model of the daily returns, we observe as in Mandelbrot (1963) writes it:
large changes tend to be followed by large changes -of either sign-and small changes by small changes and following Black (1976) volatility tends to rise with "bad" news and fall with "good" news:
To account for those properties in our residuals, we follow Nelson (1991) for the model notations.
30.13 Testing for ARCH effects (conditional heteroskedasticity)
30.13.1 Setup: mean and variance equations Let y t denote (demeaned) asset returns. A standard decomposition writes
div.formula: y t = µ t + ε t , ε t = σ t z t , E[z t ] = 0, V[z t ] = 1,(103)
div.p: where µ t captures predictable dynamics in the conditional mean (often an ARMA(p, q)), while σ 2 t is the conditional variance (conditional on past/selected information: σ 2 t = σ 2 (F t-1 )).
96 see section 25.1 for more detail on this test

div.head: ARCH(m) model
div.p: The ARCH(m) model (Engle, 1982a) specifies the conditional variance as a function of past squared innovationsfoot_38 :
The ARCH(1) case is the special case
Unconditional variance (ARCH( 1)
div.formula: ). Using E[z 2 t ] = 1 and ε t = σ t z t , E[ε 2 t ] = E[σ 2 t ] = α 0 + α 1 E[ε 2 t-1 ]. (106
div.p: If 0 ≤ α 1 < 1, the stationary solution yields
which is constant over time even though
div.formula: σ 2 t = V(ε t | F t-1
div.p: ) varies with past shocks.

div.head: How to test for ARCH in practice
div.p: ARCH effects are tested on the residuals from the model. Let εt denote those residuals. Under the null of no ARCH, the conditional variance is constant:
Two common diagnostics are:
(i) Portmanteau test on squared residuals. If {ε t } is i.i.d., then {ε 2 t } should be serially uncorrelated. One can therefore apply Ljung-Box tests to ε2 t (Section 25.1).
(ii) Engle's ARCH LM test (recommended). Run the auxiliary regression
div.formula: ε2 t = α 0 + m i=1 α i ε2 t-i + u t ,(108)
div.p: and test
Let R 2 be the coefficient of determination from 108 and let T be the sample size used in the regression. The LM statistic is
A small p-value indicates rejection of H 0 and supports the presence of ARCH effects, motivating an ARCH/GARCH-type variance specification.
Choice of the order m. In practice, m should be large enough to capture potential shortrun volatility clustering, but not so large as to lose power. Several pragmatic approaches are common:
• Rule of thumb: try a grid of small values (e.g. m = 1, 2, 5, 10, 20) and assess robustness of the rejection.
• Data-driven upper bound: set m proportional to the sample size, e.g. m ≈ ⌊T 1/3 ⌋ or ⌊log T ⌋, as in other portmanteau-type tests.
• Graphical guidance: inspect the sample autocorrelation function of ε2 t and choose m to cover the lags where autocorrelations appear economically or statistically significant.
In empirical work, it is good practice to report results for several values of m and verify that conclusions about the presence of ARCH effects are not driven by a particular lag choice.

(iii) Li-McLeod test (robust portmanteau for heavy-tailed samples). In finite samples with heavy-tailed innovations, the Ljung-Box statistic applied to ε2 t may exhibit size distortions. Li and McLeod (1981) propose a modified portmanteau statistic that remains valid under weaker moment conditions and performs better in small samples. Let ρi denote the sample cross-correlation matrix at lag i of the (possibly multivariate) residual process, and define b i = vec(ρ ′ i ). Let ρ0 be the sample contemporaneous correlation matrix and ⊗ denote the Kronecker product. The Li-McLeod statistic is
div.formula: LMc = T 2 m i=1 1 T -i b ′ i ρ-1 0 ⊗ ρ-1 0 b i ,(110)
div.p: which under H 0 (no serial dependence up to lag m) satisfies
div.formula: LMc H 0 ≈ χ 2 (k 2 m),
div.p: where k is the dimension of the residual vector.
Univariate case. When k = 1, the statistic simplifies substantially. Let r i denote the sample autocorrelation of ε2 t at lag i. Then
div.formula: LMc = T 2 m i=1 r 2 i T -i H 0 ≈ χ 2 (m).
div.p: This test can be viewed as a heavy-tail-robust portmanteau test on squared residuals and is particularly useful in financial applications where excess kurtosis is common.

div.head: GARCH models
div.p: We use the notation in Nelson (1991). Let ξ t denote the one-step-ahead innovation (prediction error) from the conditional mean equation, and let σ 2 t denote its conditional variance given the information set F t-1 :
div.formula: E[ξ t | F t-1 ] = 0, V(ξ t | F t-1 ) = σ 2 t . Assume the standardized innovation is z t : ξ t = σ t z t , z t i.i.d. with E[z t ] = 0, V[z t ] = 1,(111)
div.p: where z t is often taken Gaussian, Student-t, or GED (Generalized Error Distribution) to allow for heavy tails.

div.head: GARCH(p, q)
div.p: The GARCH(p, q) model generalizes ARCH by letting the conditional variance depend on both past squared innovations and past conditional variances:
div.formula: σ 2 t = ω + q i=1 β i σ 2 t-i + p j=1 α j ξ 2 t-j ,(112)
div.p: with parameter restrictions ω > 0,
div.formula: α j ≥ 0, β i ≥ 0.
div.p: (Equivalently, since ξ 2 t-j = σ 2 t-j z 2 t-j , the ARCH term can be written as p j=1 α j z 2 t-j σ 2 t-j .)

Interpretation. The α j terms capture the short-run impact of past shocks on volatility (news effect), while the β i terms govern volatility persistence through variance feedback. The most common specification is GARCH(1, 1):
Second-order stationarity (GARCH (1,1)). If α 1 + β 1 < 1, then {ξ t } has finite unconditional variance and
When α 1 + β 1 is close to one, volatility is highly persistent, producing long clusters of high or low variance.
Remark on distributions. Assuming a non-Gaussian z t (Student-t, GED) typically affects likelihood-based inference and tail risk measures, but not the conditional variance recursion 112.

div.head: GARCH-M model
div.p: For the GARCH-M model, we add the following equation
div.formula: R t = a + bσ 2 t + ξ t 30.15 AR(1)-ARCH(1)
div.p: Nelson (1991) details how to use maximum likelihood methods to fit an ARMA(p,q) (and potentially exponential) ARCH model and discuss the regularity conditions to be checked, but as in this paper, we will not check this as this stage ans assume that the ML estimator is consistent and asymptotically normal.
We fit an AR(1)-ARCH( 1)
div.formula: σ 2 t = ω + α 1 z 2 t-1 σ 2 t-1 and an AR(1)-GARCH(1,1) σ 2 t = ω + β i σ 2 t-1 + α 1 z 2 t-1 σ 2 t-1
div.p: model on the financial index daily returns.

div.head: AR(1)-EGARCH
div.p: EGARCH models were introduced by Nelson (1991) to accommodate three empirical features often observed in returns: (i) leverage/asymmetry (negative shocks increase future volatility more than positive shocks), (ii) fewer parameter restrictions than standard GARCH (the variance is positive by construction), and (iii) a more transparent separation between size and sign effects of shocks.

div.head: Specification
div.p: Let R t denote returns. Consider an AR(1) conditional mean and an EGARCH(p, q) conditional variance:
div.formula:              R t = a + bR t-1 + ξ t , ξ t = σ t z t , z t i.i.d. with E[z t ] = 0, V[z t ] = 1, log(σ 2 t ) = ω + p i=1 α i |z t-i | -E|z t | + p i=1 γ i z t-i + q k=1 β k log(σ 2 t-k ).(115)
div.p: Normal benchmark. If z t ∼ N (0, 1), then E|z t | = 2/π, hence the centering term |z t-i | -2/π ensures that the intercept ω controls the unconditional level of log-variance.

div.head: Interpretation
div.p: The EGARCH decomposition is convenient:
• The α i terms capture the effect of the magnitude of standardized shocks (volatility response to "surprises").
• The γ i terms capture the sign effect (asymmetry/leverage). Typically γ i < 0 implies that negative shocks raise volatility more than positive shocks of the same magnitude.
• The β k terms govern volatility persistence in log-variance.
Because the model is written for log(σ 2 t ), σ 2 t = exp(log(σ 2 t )) > 0 automatically, so nonnegativity constraints on α i , β k are not required (unlike standard GARCH).

div.head: A parsimonious workhorse: EGARCH(1,1)
div.p: The most common specification is EGARCH (1,1):
div.formula: log(σ 2 t ) = ω + α |z t-1 | -E|z t | + γz t-1 + β log(σ 2 t-1 ). (116
div.p: 30.16.4 Remark on cσ 2 t in the mean Some formulations allow R t to depend on σ 2 t (a risk-return effect). For lecture-note simplicity and to focus on volatility dynamics, we set c = 0 and estimate an AR(1)-EGARCH model as in 115. Model orders (p, q) can be selected using information criteria (AIC/BIC) and checked via residual diagnostics on standardized innovations ẑt = ξt /σ t . Nelson (1991) suggest the EGARCH model:
div.formula:        R t = a + bR t-1 + cσ 2 t + ξ t ξ t = σ t z t ln σ 2 t = ω + p i=1 α i |z t-i | -2 π + o j=1 γ j z t-j + q k=1 β k ln σ 2 t-k(117)
div.p: as in Nelson (1991), we keep c = 0 and we would need to select the order p, j, and q with information criterion.
• Question 37 -Are the standardized residuals z t of the AR(1)-GARCH or EGARCH model iid?

In equation 117, we have a linear form of "GARCH-in-mean" with the constant c. We could also include any type of function (possibly non-linear) g() and update equation 117 with R t = a + bR t-1 + g (σ t ) + ξ t . We will explore section 84.2.2 how with the package rugarch we can use the options in "mean.model" with "archm Whether to include ARCH volatility in the mean regression" and "archpow Indicates whether to use st.deviation (1) or variance (2) in the ARCH in mean regression".
30.17 GARCH model checking

30.17.1 Significance of the coefficients Beyond checking coefficients p-values, there is the Lagrange Multiplier Test (LM Test), for a parametric model with true parameters θ 0 , H 0 : Rθ 0 = r, where we can test that some coefficients in θ 0 are null (imposing some 0s in R and r). Following Francq and Zakoïan (2009), use the Wald, LM or Likelihood Ratio statistics. 30.17.2 Test on the residuals and squared residuals Ljung-Box test on the residuals (auto-correlation), squared residuals (ARCH) and ARCH LM test (presence of ARCH).

div.head: Test of second order stationarity
div.p: To test the second order stationarity, you want to test H 0 : i β i + j α j < 1, with the parameters taken from equation 112, against H 1 : i β i + j α j ≥ 1.

div.head: ARCH/GARCH Models and the Measurement of Uncertainty
div.p: In many financial and macroeconomic time series, the conditional mean may be relatively stable, while the conditional variance exhibits substantial fluctuations over time. Periods of calm alternate with periods of turbulence, a phenomenon commonly referred to as volatility clustering. Models with time-varying second-order moments provide a natural framework to formalize and measure such uncertainty. Uncertainty is modeled as the conditional variance of shocks. In Autoregressive Conditional Heteroskedasticity-type models, uncertainty is not an exogenous index but an endogenous, model-implied object:
div.formula: Uncertainty t ≡ σ 2 t = E(ε 2 t | F t-1 ). (118
div.p: where F t-1 is the information set available at time t -1. Thus, ARCH/GARCH models deliver a real-time measure of perceived risk based solely on past realizations. This makes them particularly useful for:
• financial volatility modeling (e.g. stock returns),
• macroeconomic uncertainty proxies (e.g. inflation or output shocks),
• risk management and Value-at-Risk calculations,
• testing whether uncertainty affects economic decisions (investment, credit supply, consumption).

Application to Inflation Uncertainty. While ARCH-type models were initially developed for financial returns, they are equally relevant for macroeconomic variables such as inflation. Inflation dynamics typically exhibit persistence in the mean and episodes of heightened volatility (e.g. oil shocks, regime shifts, pandemic episodes). ARCH-type models have been used to measure inflation uncertainty, as in Grier and Perry (2000). A natural specification therefore combines:
1. a dynamic mean equation for inflation, 2. a time-varying conditional variance capturing inflation uncertainty.
Let π t denote (annualized) inflation. A standard specification is:
div.formula: π t = µ t + ε t ,(119)
µ t = ϕ 0 + p i=1 ϕ i π t-i ,(120)
div.p: where D may be Gaussian or Student-t to allow for fat tails.
Inflation uncertainty is then defined as:
div.formula: Inflation Uncertainty t ≡ σ 2 t = E(ε 2 t | F t-1 ).(122)
div.p: A GARCH(1,1) specification,
captures both the immediate impact of inflation surprises (α) and the persistence of inflation uncertainty (β).

Economic Interpretation. In this framework, inflation uncertainty is the conditional variance of unexpected inflation. It reflects how difficult inflation is to forecast given available information. Importantly:
• High σ 2 t does not necessarily imply high inflation; it implies high unpredictability. • Inflation may be close to target, yet uncertainty can be elevated.
• Episodes such as the Great Inflation, the Zero Lower Bound period, or the post-pandemic inflation surge are characterized by sharp movements in σ 2 t .
Mean-Variance Interactions. A central question in macroeconomics concerns the relationship between the level of inflation and its uncertainty. Two influential hypotheses are:
• Friedman-Ball hypothesis: Higher inflation leads to higher inflation uncertainty (policy credibility channel). (Friedman, 1977;Ball, 1992).
• Cukierman-Meltzer hypothesis: Higher uncertainty may induce policymakers to generate surprise inflation. (Cukierman and Meltzer, 1986).
Empirically, this can be tested by augmenting the mean equation with conditional variance:
div.formula: π t = ϕ 0 + p i=1 ϕ i π t-i + γσ 2 t + ε t ,(124)
div.p: where γ captures the effect of uncertainty on inflation dynamics.
Distributional Considerations. Inflation shocks may exhibit fat tails during crisis periods.
Replacing the normal distribution with a Student-t distribution often improves model fit and produces more robust measures of uncertainty:
with degrees of freedom ν estimated jointly with the volatility process.
31 Spurious Regression, Cointegration, and Robust Inference

div.head: Before regressing or computing correlation, plot your data
div.p: Beforefoot_39 applying any regression model or computing correlations, it is essential to visualize the data. In time-series analysis, plotting the levels and their transformations is the first diagnostic step: it allows us to detect trends, breaks, seasonality, outliers, and potential non-stationarity.
In this section, we refer to the second variable as "GDP" for notational simplicity. However, the series actually used in the code corresponds to:

Masse salariale versée -Total branches -Valeur aux prix courants -Série CVS published by INSEE, series 001689203. This variable is not equivalent to GDP. It measures the aggregate wage bill (in current prices, seasonally adjusted), whereas GDP captures total value added in the economy. The two series are economically related but conceptually distinct.
We use this wage-bill series for educational purposes only, in order to illustrate the mechanics of regression, correlation, unit-root testing, and cointegration in a macroeconomic context.

We perform the same data cleaning operations as for the population CSV file. Since the wage-bill series is quarterly, we align frequencies by converting the monthly population series to quarterly averages before merging the two datasets. We then concatenate the two time series using the date index (see the pandas merging documentation) and plot them, using a secondary y-axis for population.
You should obtain:

div.head: Growth rates
div.p: We work with quarterly growth rates:
div.formula: g t = X t -X t-1 X t-1 or equivalently g t ≈ ∆ log X t .
div.p: We compute the quarterly changes and expect a relatively low contemporaneous correlation between wage-bill growth (labeled "GDP" in the code) and population growth.
We then inspect a scatter plot of wage-bill growth against population growth:

Two observations clearly appear as outliers: one associated with a strongly negative wage-bill growth rate (macro shock) and another with an unusually high population growth rate. We discuss formal treatment of outliers in Section 45.1.

div.head: Correlation
div.p: Following the methodology detailed in Section 19.3.3, we compute the sample correlation between the two growth rates. We obtain a relatively low correlation of approximately 18%. This modest correlation illustrates an important empirical lesson: even variables that are economically related in levels (e.g. activity and demographics) may display weak short-run comovement in growth rates.

div.head: Unit Root test -Dickey-Fuller test
div.p: We test "manually" the GDP for a unit root following the test described in section 24.6. We test that both the French population and GDP time series are I(1).

div.head: Cointegration test
div.p: After we differenced the variables, we cannot check for their long term relation in level, when differenced the variables are only compared in short term models. With two I(1) series 99 y t and x t , level comparison via linear regression would be difficult because subject to spurious regression. But two I(1) series are said to be cointegrated, if there exist a linear combination 100 of them (e.g. β 0 + β y y t + β x x t ) that is I(0). This means that y t and x t share a common stochastic trend and the two time series, after having applied some coefficients, cannot drift too far apart from one another in the long-run 101 . If there exist 102 β y and β x such that E(β 0 + β y y t + β x x t ) = 0, then there is a long term relationship between y t and x t and one can test the following model:
div.formula: y t = α + βx t + ϵ t (126)
div.p: and from our statements above, one can deduct that the cointegrating residuals ϵ t have to be I(0) if y t and x t are cointegrated 103 . We can then test for cointegration between variable with a cointegration test: the null hypothesis is no cointegration. The second output give in the p-value of the test.
For our variables, we do not reject the null hypothesis and assume no cointegrations.

div.head: Building two cointegrated time series
div.p: We suggest to build two cointegrated time series with the system of equations:
div.formula: y t = β y y t-1 + β x x t + ϵ t x t = x t-1 + ν t(127)
div.p: with both ϵ t and ν t white noises. We can show that both series are I(1) and cointegrated. Visually the link might not be obvious: 99 we define by I(n) integrated series of order n called long-run equilibrium relationship unless the "forces" that kept them together in the past do not hold any longer βy and βz are not unique (one can add a constant and the relationship would still hold), also note that β0 can be null and the following still applies but as demonstrated in Phillips and Ouliaris (1990), one cannot use the traditional Augmented Dickey-Fuller for this test because of the spurious nature of the regression, furthermore, the the t-statistics for β will not be asymptotically normal if ϵt are serially correlated
Once we applied the cointegrating regression and found the coefficients, we can show the "forces de rappel" where there seem to be some forces that brings y t to "follow" x t :
div.formula: y t = γ 0 + γ 1 x t + χ t(
div.p: 128) 0 200 400 600 800 1000 0 20 40 60 80 Forces de rappel yt xt_modif
We can then study the following Error Correction Model, with a grain of saltfoot_40 with regard to the consistency of the estimation by OLS as detailed in (Zivot and Wang, 2003):
div.formula: ∆y t = α y 1 + α y 2 χ t-1 + α y 3 ∆y t-1 + α y 4 ∆x t-1 + ϵ y t ∆x t = α x 1 + α x 2 χ t-1 + α x 3 ∆y t-1 + α x 4 ∆x t-1 + ϵ x t (129)

div.head: Cointegrated variables -example
div.p: We take the Euribor 1 year (FM.M.U2.EUR.RT.MM.EURIBOR1YD_.HSTA) and the Euribor 3 months (FM.M.U2.EUR.RT.MM.EURIBOR3MD_.HSTA) from the ECB statistical warehouse, check for cointegration between the two time series and build an ECM if relevant. Noting that both time series are I(1) and cointegrated, there is no unit root in the error terms ϵ t of the regression:
div.formula: Y t = α + βX t + ϵ t
div.p: Remember that if our model is correct, then E(ϵ t ) = 0

div.head: Error Correction Model -examples
div.p: We first estimate the long run relationship from the spurious model:
div.formula: Y t = α + βX t + ϵ t
div.p: and we estimate the disequilibrium errors or the cointegrating residuals:
div.formula: εt = Y t -α -βX t
div.p: We then compute the error correction model were all variable are now stationary:
div.formula: ∆Y t = γ 0 + γ 1 ∆X t + γ 2 εt-1 + ν t
div.p: We can say that our model explains 78% of the variance of the observed changes. And that for a 1% increase in the Euribor 3 months rate there is a 0.92% increase in the Euribor 1 year rate (γ 1 = 0.92 and is significantly different than 0 with a t-value of 31 well above 1.96).
• Question 38 -In the ECM above, add the lagged variables of the expenditure and absorption, are the results still holding?
div.formula: ∆Y t = γ 0 + γ 1 ∆X t + γ 2 εt-1 + γ 3 ∆Y t-1 + γ 4 ∆X t-1 + ν t

div.head: Error Correction Model -consumption and income
div.p: Noting that the permanent income model implies cointegration between consumption and income, we use some proxy data from the Penn World Table and focus on data for Italy:
• cda: Real domestic absorption, (real consumption plus investment), at current PPPs (in mil. 2011USD)
• cgdpe: Expenditure-side real GDP at current PPPs (in mil. 2011USD)

div.head: Before regressing two I(1) time series, test for cointegration
div.p: We test the French population and GDP for cointegration. As they don't seem cointegrated we perform a linear regression.

div.head: Linear regression
div.p: Now we want to perform a simple linearfoot_41 regression of population change on French GDP change:
div.formula: ∆Y Pop,t = α + β∆X GDP,t + ϵ t(130)
div.p: We perform an Ordinary Least Square method.

div.head: Visuals
div.p: We can store the α and β value of that regression for future use and display our regression line over a scatter plot of the original data:
In our visual of the regression, we can identify visually at least two outliers. We would need to check for the robustness of our model if we remove those two outliers as a first robustness check.

div.head: Outlier detection and regression coefficient robustness
div.p: We identified visually two potential outliersfoot_42 . We want to determine if they influence the regression coefficients out of proportion. We identify one outlier with the minimum GDP growth rate and one with the maximum population growth rate. We create a dummy variable Dummy that has the value 0 for all observation except for the outlier one where the value is 1, we then do a new regression:
div.formula: ∆Y Pop,t = α + β∆X GDP,t + γDummy t (131)
div.p: We test for both outliers if the γ is significantly different to 0. Instead of using the typical t-Student threshold t 1-α 2 ,n-p-1 we rather use the Bonferroni correction t-Student threshold:
div.formula: t 1-α 2n ,n-p-1
div.p: We find that one of the outliers coefficient is significantly different to 0 and we remove it from the observations before running a new linear regression and notice that the GDP growth rate sensitivity to the population growth rate is significantly different and the explanatory power of our model is improved.

div.head: Q-Q plots of our linear regressions' normalized residuals against a normal law
div.p: From the orginal regression, we can check whether the residuals are normally distributed:
div.formula: 2 1 0 1 2 Theoretical quantiles 2 0 2 4 6 Ordered Values Normal Q-Q plot
div.p: We can see that if we remove the outlier number 2, then our residuals seems to be normally distributed and the model improved:
div.formula: 2 1 0 1 2 Theoretical quantiles Ordered Values Normal Q-Q plot, outlier 2 taken out

div.head: Robust regression
div.p: We complement OLS with robust regression methodsfoot_43 . Table 2 reports OLS regressions in columns ( 1)-( 2) and robust linear models (RLM) based on M-estimators in columns (3)-( 4). In addition, we illustrate a consensus-based approach (RANSAC) that explicitly separates inliers from outliers. In our application, we include a dummy variable equal to 1 for the atypical observation where the change in French population is unusually high (and 0 otherwise). This allows us to diagnose whether results are driven by that observation, and to compare (i) explicit outlier control (adding the dummy) to (ii) implicit robustness (downweighting or excluding atypical points).

0.010 0.005 0.000 0.005 0.010 0.015 0.020 French GDP changes 0.0000 0.0005 0.0010 0.0015 0.0020 0.0025 0.0030 0.0035 0.0040 0.0045 French Population changes Inliers Outliers OLS OLS no outliers median reg linear robust reg RANSAC
Setup. Let {(x i , y i )} n i=1 denote observations with x i = GDP i and y i = Population i . We consider the linear model
where ε i captures deviations from the linear relation. Let r i (α, β) = y i -α -βx i denote the residual.

div.head: OLS: squared-loss and sensitivity to outliers
div.p: In Section 31.3.7, we saw that OLS solves a least-squares problem:
div.formula: (α, β) ∈ arg min α,β n i=1 r i (α, β) 2 . (133
div.p: Equivalently, OLS is an M-estimator with loss ρ(u) = u 2 . The squared loss heavily penalizes large residuals: if one observation has a residual 10 times larger than another, it receives 100 times more weight in 133. This is efficient under homoskedastic Gaussian errors, but it makes OLS non-robust: a small number of aberrant observations can strongly affect ( α, β).
Dummy for the outlier. A simple diagnostic is to include a dummy d i for the suspected outlier:
div.formula: y i = α + βx i + γd i + ε i . (134
div.p: If d i flags a single observation, then γ absorbs a one-off level shift for that point, often stabilizing β relative to the baseline model.

div.head: Median (quantile) regression: absolute-loss at the median
div.p: Quantile regression generalizes least squares by targeting conditional quantiles rather than conditional means. For a quantile index τ ∈ (0, 1), the quantile regression estimator solves
div.formula: (α τ , βτ ) ∈ arg min α,β n i=1 ρ τ (r i (α, β)) , ρ τ (u) = u (τ -1{u < 0}) ,(135)
div.p: where ρ τ is the check (pinball) loss. For τ = 0.5 (the median), this reduces to an absolute-loss criterion:
div.formula: ( α0.5 , β0.5 ) ∈ arg min α,β n i=1 |r i (α, β)|. (136
div.p: Because |r| grows linearly rather than quadratically, median regression is less sensitive to large residuals than OLS. Intuitively, the fitted line is chosen so that about half the residuals are positive and half are negative, and extreme points do not dominate the criterion as they do under squared loss. Columns (3)-( 4) of Table 2 compare the baseline median slope and the median slope net of the flagged outlier.

div.head: Robust Linear Model (RLM): M-estimation and Huber loss
div.p: Robust linear models in statsmodels are implemented as M-estimators. The general Mestimation problem is:
div.formula: ( α, β) ∈ arg min α,β n i=1 ρ r i (α, β) s ,(137)
div.p: where:
• ρ(•) is a robust loss function (less aggressive than u 2 in the tails),
• s > 0 is a scale parameter used to standardize residuals (so that the notion of "large" residual is unit-free).
A canonical choice is the Huber loss:

which behaves like least squares for small standardized residuals (|u| ≤ k), but switches to an absolute-loss regime for large residuals (|u| > k). Hence, outliers are downweighted rather than allowed to dominate.
From minimization to iteratively reweighted least squares (IRLS). Huber M-estimation can be computed by IRLS. Define ψ k (u) = ρ ′ k (u) and weights

At each iteration, one (i) computes standardized residuals u i = r i /s, (ii) assigns weights w i = w k (u i ), and (iii) runs a weighted least squares regression. Large residuals (large |u i |) receive weights below 1.

The tuning constant k and the role of MAD. The constant k controls robustness: smaller k downweights more aggressively, larger k is closer to OLS. A conventional value for Huber is k = 1.345, which (under Gaussian errors) yields about 95% efficiency relative to OLS. Because residuals must be standardized, a robust scale estimate is needed. A common choice is the median absolute deviation (MAD),
div.formula: MAD = median i |r i -median j (r j )| ,
div.p: and, under normality, MAD/0.6745 is a consistent estimator of the standard deviation. Therefore, one often sets
so that u = r/s is comparable across samples. In practice, statsmodels performs this scaling internally for RLM, and the Huber threshold k = 1.345 is applied to these standardized residuals.

Implementation in Python (statsmodels). statsmodels.RLM expects a design matrix X; unlike formula-based OLS, we add an explicit intercept. This estimates the robust M-estimator 137 with ρ = ρ k in 138. Adding the dummy corresponds to replacing X = [1, x] with X = [1, x, d].
Comparing these two RLM estimates clarifies whether robustness alone is sufficient, or whether the flagged observation induces a distinct level shift better captured by 134.

div.head: RANSAC: consensus estimation by explicit inlier selection
div.p: RANSAC (RANdom SAmple Consensus) follows a different philosophy: rather than downweighting outliers smoothly, it attempts to identify inliers and fit the model on a subset that is consistent with the dominant linear relation.

div.head: Algorithm (linear regression case).
div.p: A simplified description is:
1. Randomly draw a minimal subset of points (for a line: a small subset sufficient to estimate α, β).
2. Fit the model on this subset.
3. Compute residuals for all observations and classify as inliers those with |r i | ≤ τ , where τ is a residual threshold.
4. Refit the model using all inliers; keep the model with the largest inlier set (or best inlier fit).
The method is robust when the data contain a large fraction of gross outliers, provided there exists a majority inlier structure.

Implementation in Python (scikit-learn). In the code, residual_threshold corresponds to the inlier cutoff τ : observations with residuals below this threshold are treated as inliers. The printed slope and intercept come from the final refitted inlier model stored in ransac.estimator_.
How to interpret differences across methods.
• OLS estimates the conditional mean slope but can be pulled by extreme observations.
• Median regression estimates the conditional median slope, reducing sensitivity to tail observations.
• RLM (Huber) estimates a robust mean-like relationship by downweighting large standardized residuals via 139.
• RANSAC estimates the slope of the dominant inlier relationship by selecting a consensus subset; results can change if the residual threshold is too tight/loose.

In practice, agreement between OLS, median regression, RLM, and RANSAC strengthens credibility; divergence indicates that atypical observations materially affect the fitted relation and should be investigated (data quality, structural breaks, or genuinely rare events).

div.head: Notes:
div.p: Columns ( 1)-( 2) report OLS estimates of population growth on GDP growth; column (2) adds a dummy equal to one for the largest population-growth observation. Columns (3)-(4) report median (quantile, q = 0.5) regressions with and without the same dummy. OLS columns display R 2 and the F-statistic; quantile regressions report a pseudo-R 2 . Robust standard errors in parentheses. * p < 0.1, * * p < 0.05, * * * p < 0.01.
Table 2: OLS and median (quantile) regressions

Economic interpretation and robustness. Across specifications, the estimated slope on GDP lies in a narrow interval between 0.03 and 0.04. Interpreting both variables in percentage changes, this coefficient can be read as an elasticity: a 1% increase in French GDP is associated with an increase in French population growth of roughly 0.03-0.04%, ceteris paribus. Importantly, this magnitude is remarkably stable across (i) OLS on the trimmed sample, (ii) median (quantile) regression, and (iii) the Huber-based robust linear model. Such stability suggests that the positive association between GDP growth and population change is not mechanically driven by the single atypical observation. In other words, the relationship appears to reflect the central tendency of the data rather than the influence of extreme residuals.

For comparison, the RANdom SAmple Consensus (RANSAC) estimator delivers a slope close to 0.04, which is economically and statistically comparable to the other robust estimates. However, unlike M-estimators or quantile regression, RANSAC relies on a hard classification between inliers and outliers determined by a residual threshold parameter. As a result, its estimates can vary non-negligibly when this threshold is modified.

Overall, the convergence of OLS (after trimming), median regression, and Huber RLM reinforces the credibility of the estimated elasticity, while the RANSAC exercise illustrates both the usefulness and the sensitivity of consensus-based robust methods.

So far we applied univariate models or regressions, we will now introducefoot_44 multivariate models such as Vector autoregressions (VARs) that were introduced into empirical economics by Sims (1980).
We suggest the companion website on linear state space dynamic system.

div.head: VAR model
div.p: Model equation 130 links the the French population growth with the GDP growth. This models that immigration and birth rate simultaneously increase when the economy is performing well.

If no cointegration exists between the population and GDP level times seriesfoot_45 and if we agree to discard contemporaneous relationships between variables, we are limited to model that the GDP growth is linked to lagged immigration and lagged birth rate, so. Let:
• p t the population at quarter t
div.formula: • g t the GDP at quarter t • e i,t idd error terms ∼ N (0, σ i ) ∆p t = b 1 + c 11 ∆p t-1 + c 12 ∆g t-1 + e p,t , ∆g t = b 2 + c 21 ∆p t-1 + c 22 ∆g t-1 + e g,t .(141)
div.p: which can be written in matrix form as:
If we write:
we can rewrite equation 142
div.formula: y t = b + Φy t-1 + e t e t ∼ (0, Ω),(143)
div.p: where
div.formula: y t ∈ R 2 , b ∈ R 2 ,
div.p: and Φ ∈ R 2×2 . 32.1.1 Stability, Stationarity, and Estimation of the VAR(1) Stability condition. Iterating forward,
div.formula: y t = k-1 j=0 Φ j b + Φ k y t-k + k-1 j=0
div.p: Φ j e t-j .
The process is stable if
div.formula: Φ k -→ 0 as k → ∞.
div.p: This holds if and only if the spectral radius of Φ satisfies
div.formula: ρ(Φ) = max i |λ i (Φ)| < 1,
div.p: that is, all eigenvalues of Φ lie strictly inside the unit circle.

Equivalently, the roots of the characteristic polynomial det(I -Φz) = 0 must lie outside the unit circle. If one root lies on the unit circle, the process is non-stationary; if cointegrating relations exist, a VECM representation is required.
Wold representation and covariance stationarity.
div.formula: If ρ(Φ) < 1, then ∞ j=0 ∥Φ j ∥ < ∞,
div.p: and the VAR admits the convergent moving-average representation
div.formula: y t = µ + ∞ j=0 Φ j e t-j , µ = (I -Φ) -1 b.
div.p: Since e t is white noise with finite second moments and the coefficient matrices are absolutely summable, y t has:
• a constant mean µ,
• a finite, time-invariant covariance matrix,
• autocovariances depending only on the lag.
Hence stability implies covariance stationarity.
Estimation. Under covariance stationarity and the exogeneity condition
each equation of the reduced-form VAR can be consistently estimated by OLS. In fact, because the regressors are identical across equations, OLS coincides with equation-by-equation GLS.

If, in addition, e t ∼ N (0, Ω), OLS is equivalent to maximum likelihood estimation for the reduced-form VAR. Therefore, the eigenvalue condition ρ(Φ) < 1 is not merely technical: it guarantees existence of a finite second-moment solution, a convergent MA representation, and standard asymptotic properties of OLS estimators.

div.head: From Reduced-Form Innovations to Structural Shocks
div.p: In the reduced-form VAR 142, the innovation vector satisfies
div.formula: e t ∼ N (0, Ω), Ω = E(e t e ′ t ) = σ pp σ pg σ pg σ gg .
div.p: In general, Ω is symmetric positive definite but not necessarily diagonal. If Ω is diagonal, the reduced-form shocks are contemporaneously uncorrelated.
If instead σ pg ̸ = 0, the reduced-form residuals are correlated:

Cov(e p,t , e g,t ) ̸ = 0, which implies that each e t combines multiple underlying economic disturbances. In that case, impulse responses computed directly from the reduced form do not correspond to economically meaningful structural shocks.
To recover orthogonal structural shocks, assume the existence of a structural representation
div.formula: A 0 y t = A 1 y t-1 + u t , u t ∼ N (0, I),
div.p: where u t are structural shocks normalized to have identity covariance matrix. The reduced-form and structural innovations are linked by
Identification therefore requires imposing restrictions on A 0 . Under recursive (triangular) identification, one uses the Cholesky factorization
div.formula: Ω = P P ′ ,
div.p: with P lower triangular, and sets A -1 0 = P . Structural shocks are then obtained as
div.formula: u t = P -1 e t .
div.p: The triangular structure implies that the ordering of variables determines the contemporaneous causal relations: variables ordered earlier are not contemporaneously affected by those ordered later. This highlights that structural identification is not purely statistical but embeds economic assumptions about contemporaneous interactions.

div.head: Get the data
div.p: As we did with the GDP series, we fetch the unemployment series from INSEE website and import the data.

div.head: Preparing the VAR model
div.p: We prepare the VAR model, first we import the corresponding package and then resample the population series in quarterly. Then we concatenate the time series into a DataFrame, take the differences related to the integration order of each variable and drop the rows where there are some missing values (NaN). VAR models are not good to deal with seasonality nor trend, we need to remove those effects before applying the model. Finally we apply the VAR model and print the results summary.

div.head: Select the lag order
div.p: For a VAR model of the type:
div.formula: y t = c + A 1 y t-1 + • • • + A p y t-p + ϵ t
div.p: , the order of the VAR is the value p such that A p ̸ = 0 and A k = 0, ∀k > p.
We suggest two main references (Lütkepohl, 2005) and (Kilian and Lütkepohl, 2017) to select the lag order, keeping in mind as Luetkepohl mentions:

we know that the approximate Mean Square Error matrix of the 1-step ahead predictor will increase with the order p. Thus, choosing p unnecessarily large will reduce the forecast precision of the corresponding estimated VAR(p) model
• Question 39
-As in (Lütkepohl, 2005), we want to experiment fitting a VAR model of higher order than the data generating process:
1. consider the following VAR model:
2. consider ϵ 1 ∼ N (0, 0.1) and ϵ 2 ∼ N (0, 0.9)

3. generate 200 time series for T = 30 and T = 100 4. on the generated series, fit a VAR(1), a VAR(2) and a VAR(3), and for each model compute the average squared forecast errors at horizon h = 1 and h = 3.
We create the VAR model and select the lag, search for up to lag order 15.
If the chosen lag order is unnecessarily large, the forecast precision of the corresponding VAR(p) model will be reduced. The VAR order selected is 1, the statistics for four criterio are given:
1. Akaike's Information Criterion (AIC)
2. Bayesian information criterion (BIC) or Schwarz information criterion 3. Final Prediction Error (FPE) 4. Hannan-Quinn Criterion (HQIC)
Following (Lütkepohl, 2005), there is no systematic rule to chose which coefficient to base your decision on: HQIC and BIC are consistent but

In small samples, AIC and FPE may have better properties (choose the correct order more often) than HQ and SC [BIC]. Also, the former two criteria are designed for minimizing the forecast error variance. Thus, in small as well as large samples, models based on AIC and FPE may produce superior forecasts although they may not estimate the orders correctly. [...] AIC and FPE asymptotically overestimate the true order with positive probability and underestimate the true order with probability zero

In a simulation study based on many other processes, Luetkepohl (1985) obtained similar results. In that study, for low order VAR processes, the most parsimonious SC criterion was found to do quite well in terms of choosing the correct VAR order and providing good forecasting models. Unfortunately, in practice we often don't even know whether the underlying data generation law is of finite order VAR type. Sometimes we may just approximate an infinite order VAR process by a finite order model. In that case, for moderate sample sizes, some less parsimonious criterion like AIC may give superior results in terms of forecast precision. Therefore, it may be a good strategy to compare the order estimates obtained with different criteria and possibly perform analyses with different VAR orders.

div.head: VAR Order
div.p: AIC and BIC are (Information Criteria) statistics that measure the distance between observations and the model. We wish to minimize that distance to fit the data generating process. IC have two additive parts:
• a goodness-of-fit measure (e.g. minus the maximized likelihood);
• a penalty that increases with the model's complexity.
A study of lag order selection for VAR Impulse Response Analysis is proposed by (Ivanov and Lutz, 2005). (Hirano and Wright, 2017) suggest to add bootstrap aggregation (bagging) :

It is common practice to select the model based on pseudo out-of-sample fit from a sequence of recursive or rolling predictions. Parameters are then estimated over the whole sample period. The idea of using an out-of-sample criterion was discussed by Clark (2004) and West (2006), and has a long history, going back to Wilson (1934), Meese and Rogoff (1983), and Ashley, Granger, and Schmalensee (1980). Instead, one might select the model based on in-sample fit, but adjust for potential overfitting by using an information criterion, such as the Akaike information criterion (AIC) (Akaike (1974)), as advocated by Inoue and Kilian (2006).

We consider various methods of model selection and forecasting, including: using in-sample fit with the AIC information criterion; selecting the model based on recursive pseudo out-of-sample forecast accuracy and then using the whole data set for parameter estimation; and splitting the sample into two parts, using one part for model selection and the other for parameter estimation. We call this last method the split-sample approach.

div.head: Apply a VAR model and checking for impact
div.p: We apply a VAR(1) to our data. We perform test on our model (all eigenvalues of Φ have modulus less than 1, autocorrelations and cross-correlations of model residuals, normality tests of residuals, ARCH-LM test on residuals).

The coefficient c 1,2 (from equation 142) seems to be significantly different from 0, so we expect that a shock on the GDP will have an impact on the population dynamics. That seems to be the only non diagonal element different from zero. We can apply a Granger causality test 110 (more detail on this test can be found section 47) and also visually check that with an impulse response function (irf).

div.head: (structural) VAR model
div.p: Let y t = (∆p t , ∆g t ) ′ . A reduced-form VAR(1) writes
div.formula: y t = b + Cy t-1 + u t , E(u t ) = 0, E(u t u ′ t ) = Ω. (144
div.p: This representation is statistically convenient, but the innovations u t generally combine multiple economic disturbances: when Ω is not diagonal, the reduced-form innovations are contemporaneously correlated and cannot be interpreted as independent structural shocks. As a result, impulse responses based on u t do not have a causal interpretation without further identifying assumptions.
To recover economically meaningful shocks, we introduce a structural VAR:
div.formula: Ay t = a + By t-1 + ε t , E(ε t ε ′ t ) = Σ ε ,(145)
div.p: where A captures contemporaneous interactions and ε t are structural shocks (normalized so that Σ ε is diagonal, typically the identity). Premultiplying 161 by A -1 yields the reduced form with
Identification consists in imposing economically motivated restrictions on A (and/or on Σ ε ) so that A can be recovered from the estimated Ω.
Let's take a step back, we would need to identify the following set of simultaneous equations:
If both relationships hold, then the model is not identified. To identify the following equations with n variables, on top of assuming that ϵ 1 and ϵ 2 are uncorrelated we need n(n-1)

= 1 restrictions 111 , we impose β 0 = 0: we believe that people cannot "nowcast" GDP. We believe that the current population does contribute to the current GDP, β 1 ̸ = 0. We will show section 32.4.2 that this is equivalent with a Cholesky decomposition (see section 11.3.5) with a careful ordering of variables. We estimate the following structural 112 VAR:

We assume that ϵ 0 and ϵ 1 are iid, orthogonal and exogenous. If this orthogonality condition is not respected, then changes in ϵ 0 affects ϵ 1 and then changes in g t will be due to changes in p t and in ϵ 1 , it is then not possible to identify (or isolate) the change in g t caused by p t .

We write: A = 1 -β 0 -β 1 1 As long as A -1 existsfoot_47 , the system of equations can be written in reduced form exactly as in equation 142. But now regressors are correlated with e t = e p,t e g,t is the forecast error and both forecast errors are affected by both shocks ϵ p and ϵ g . Indeed, e t = A -1 ϵ t .
If our assumptions are correct that A -1 exists and the innovations follow:
div.formula: ϵ t = ϵ p,t ϵ g,t ∼ N (0, D)
div.p: with D a diagonal matrix.

The main issue we have with the reduced VAR form is that we do not identify the β i , we only have correlation of variables with forecast and cannot properly apply orthogonal shock ϵ i , that is why we implemented the structural VAR, in our example, this is equivalent with a Cholesky decomposition (provided we have the right ordering of variables). Our main reference text book is (Kilian and Lütkepohl, 2017).

div.head: Getting the intuition about the necessity for parameter restrictions
div.p: Let's imagine we want to work with the simple model:
div.formula: ∆p t = a 1 + β 0 ∆g t + ϵ 1 t ∆g t = a 2 + β 1 ∆p t + ϵ 2 t (149) If ∆p t = a 1 + β 0 ∆g t + ϵ 1 t , then ∆g t = -a 1 β 0 + 1 β 0 ∆p t -1 β 0 ϵ 1 t
div.p: , so you do not have the liberty to identify β 0 and β 1 separately.

div.head: Cholesky or LDL decomposition, aka short-run restrictions
div.p: We have made the assumptions:
div.formula: ϵ t = ϵ p,t ϵ g,t ∼ N (0, D)
div.p: with D a diagonal matrix and e t ∼ N (0, Ω)

In order to identify the model, we can make the assumption that the population get knowledge of the GDP growth only with a lag, thus the immigration or birth rate is only "reacting" to the GDP growth rate with a lag and we can assume a lower triangular matrix, from this structure it is as if we assume that:
which enables us to identify A, the inverse of a lower (resp. upper) non-singular triangular matrix is a lower (resp. upper) triangular matrix.
The Cholesky decomposition delivers a unique triangular matrix (with strictly positive diagonal elements), which allows us to recover A -1 and therefore A.
Recall that:
• The inverse of a non-singular triangular matrix is triangular of the same type (lower remains lower, upper remains upper).
• Identification relies entirely on the zero restrictions imposed on the contemporaneous matrix.
• The ordering of variables is crucial: changing the ordering modifies the implied contemporaneous structure and therefore the structural impulse responses.
It is then possible to do a moving average or Wold representation of the VAR to determine the impact of shocks on the variables.

div.head: Impulse response function
div.p: We can also plot the impulse responses: these are the responses to a unit impulse to a variable that our VAR model predicts. In our case, we can believe that it is possible to have an isolated shock on the population that is not contemporaneous with a shock on the GDP, hence it makes economic sense to study such an isolated shock.
As we expected from our Granger causality test, a shock on the GDP has an impact on the population significantly different from 0 (but marginally):
Impulse responses (orthogonalized)

32.4.4 SVAR vs VAR (orthogonalized) IRFs -are they the same?
Setup. Let y t ∈ R n follow a reduced-form VAR(p):
div.formula: y t = c + p i=1 B i y t-i + u t , E[u t ] = 0, E[u t u ′ t ] = Σ u . (151
div.p: Let Ψ h denote the MA coefficients of the reduced-form VAR:
div.formula: y t = µ + ∞ h=0 Ψ h u t-h , Ψ 0 = I n .(152)
div.p: Structural form. A (static) SVAR representation writes
div.formula: A 0 y t = a + p i=1 A i y t-i + ε t , E[ε t ε ′ t ] = I n ,(153)
div.p: so that the mapping between reduced-form and structural shocks is
div.formula: u t = A -1 0 ε t ≡ S ε t , Σ u = E[u t u ′ t ] = SS ′ . (154
div.p: IRFs. Structural (non-orthogonalized) IRF in an SVAR: the response of y t+h to a unit structural shock ε j,t is
div.formula: IRF SVAR h (:, j) = ∂y t+h ∂ε j,t = Ψ h Se j = Ψ h A -1 0 e j ,(155)
div.p: where e j is the j-th canonical basis vector.
Orthogonalized IRF in a reduced-form VAR (Cholesky): choose a factor P such that Σ u = P P ′ , P lower triangular (Cholesky). ( 156)
Define orthogonal innovations η t by u t = P η t with E[η t η ′ t ] = I n . Then
div.formula: IRF VAR,orth h (:, j) = ∂y t+h ∂η j,t = Ψ h P e j . (157
div.p: Equivalence conditions. Comparing 155 and 157:
div.formula: IRF SVAR h (:, j) = IRF VAR,orth h (:, j) ∀h, j ⇐⇒ S = A -1 0 = P. (158)

div.head: "The Demise of Granger Causality Tests in Macroeconomics"
div.p: As (Kilian and Lütkepohl, 2017) put it:

Initially, economists thought that bivariate Granger causality tests established that money leads income, but that result weakened once more variables were included in the VAR model. It also proved highly sensitive to different forms of detrending and to changes in the model specification. Moreover, it was shown that apparent Granger noncausality from money to income may simply reflect the omission of a third variable, whereas a finding of bivariate Granger causality may likewise reflect the omission of a third variable, calling into question even further the usefulness of Granger causality tests [. . . ]. Granger causality tests were replaced by the new idea that we are not interested in the contemporaneous correlation between money and income growth or the lead-lag pattern, but in the question of how income responds to unanticipated changes in money growth (also known as innovations or shocks).
The hope was that these shocks would be exogenous, even if the underlying money growth time series was not.

div.head: Short-run and long-run restrictions
div.p: So far, we only imposed zero short-run restrictions which is simply done by imposing some parameters in A to be null, this is equivalent with a Cholesky decomposition. This can be justified, if we take:
div.formula: y t = M y t-1 + ϵ t
div.p: where y t is the time series vector of GDP growth rate and inflation. Economists will assume that the structural innovations represent supply s S t and demand s D t shocks and can restrict that a demand shock as no contemporaneous effect on the GDP growth rate, which correspond to the Cholesky decomposition. We check 114 ex-post our assumptions:
• a positive supply shock should increase the GDP and decrease prices (violated).

• a positive demand shock should increase the GDP and increase prices We might also want to impose 115 zero long-run restrictions, introduced in (Blanchard and Quah, 1989) where they take GDP growth rate and unemployment rate. They assume that the structural innovations represent supply s S t and demand s D t shocks, but restrict that the demand shock has no long-run effect on the GDP. Economic theory posit that money is neutral: the level of money has no long-run impact on the level of GDP. A VAR model of the macroeconomy would include: GDP, consumption, investment, employment, nominal wages, money, prices and nominal interest rates.
114 SVAR_vansteenberghe.py 115 SVAR_long_run_restrictions_vansteenberghe

div.head: A macroeconomic model
div.p: As a basic macro-economist, assume the GDP growth g t , the nominal interest rate i t and the inflation rate π t follow:
div.formula:      g t = α g + β i,g i t-1 + β π,g π t + u g,t i t = α i + β i,i i t-1 + β π,i π t + u i,t Taylor rule π t = α π + β π,π π t-1 + β g,π g t-1 + u π,t Phillips curve (159)
div.p: where the structural shocks u t are uncorrelated.

div.p: It is not easy to find I(0) data and to model a VAR that makes sense. We use the data in Bourbonnais' Econometrie, Dunod. You can download the file online here. Demand Y 1 and prices Y 2 of some commodity are given.

div.head: Import the data
div.p: We import the data and clean it.

We verify that both given series are stationary. We find indeed that both series are I(0). We define a function that print the result of the test and that allow to chose for a threshold for the p-value.

The impact of Y 2 on Y 1 seems to be significantly different than 0. We again check this with an impulse response function.

div.head: A rolling regression
div.p: If we want to perform a rolling regression of Y 2 on Y 1 with a window of 10 quaters, we can simply use the function or define the rolling regression ourselves. And apply our function. Note that we need to add some zeros at the beginning of our beta list with the size of our window.

div.head: Why there is debate about VAR-based measures of policy shocks
div.p: A common empirical strategy is to treat the reduced-form innovations of a VAR as containing monetary policy shocks, after imposing identifying restrictions. This strategy has been influential, but there is an active debate about whether the resulting "policy shock" series is economically interpretable and stable.
Baseline VAR and the identification problem. Let y t ∈ R n collect macro variables (e.g. output, prices, an interest rate, reserves, etc.). A reduced-form VAR(p) is
div.formula: y t = c + p i=1 B i y t-i + e t , E(e t ) = 0, E(e t e ′ t ) = Ω. (160
div.p: The residuals e t are not structural shocks: the covariance matrix Ω is typically not diagonal, so the components of e t are contemporaneously correlated.
A structural representation writes
div.formula: A 0 y t = a + p i=1 A i y t-i + ε t , E(ε t ε ′ t ) = I n ,(161)
div.p: with the mapping

Thus identifying the structural shocks ε t from e t requires choosing a factorization of Ω (i.e. pinning down A 0 ). Any orthonormal rotation Q with QQ ′ = I n delivers another admissible set of shocks:

So the "monetary policy shock" is not uniquely defined without additional restrictions (recursive ordering, sign restrictions, external instruments, etc.). The debate is about whether common restrictions deliver a credible policy-shock series.

The typical VAR shock interpretation. Suppose the policy instrument is the short-term interest rate (one element of y t ). Under a recursive (Cholesky) scheme, one assumes a contemporaneous causal ordering that implies a lower-triangular A 0 (equivalently, a lower-triangular impact matrix). Then the identified policy shock is the innovation to the interest-rate equation that is orthogonal to earlier-ordered variables.

Mathematically, with e t = P u t where P is the Cholesky factor of Ω (so Ω = P P ′ ) and u t has identity covariance, the structural shocks are set to u t . The policy shock is one component u m,t , and the associated impulse response is computed from the moving-average representation
div.formula: y t = µ + ∞ h=0 Ψ h e t-h = µ + ∞ h=0 Ψ h P u t-h .(164)
div.p: Rudebusch's key point: the policy equation is structural and can be wrong. A central observation in Rudebusch ( 1998) is that the interest-rate equation is unlike a generic VAR equation: since the central bank directly controls the short rate (approximately), the VAR interest-rate equation has a natural structural interpretation as a central-bank reaction function, and its residuals as policy shocks. This invites direct scrutiny of whether the estimated reaction function "makes sense" (functional form, information set, stability), and whether the implied shocks are plausible. Formally, Rudebusch writes a typical VAR funds-rate equation as a reduced-form reaction function
div.formula: i t = p s=1 A ′ s X t-s + u t ,(165)
div.p: and, under a Cholesky-type contemporaneous identification, as a structural reaction function
div.formula: i t = p s=0 B ′ s X pre t-s + p s=1 C ′ s X post t-s + ε i,t ,(166)
div.p: where X pre t are variables ordered contemporaneously before i t (thus allowed to enter at t), and X post t are variables ordered after i t (thus entering only with lags). The residual ε i,t is interpreted as the exogenous (orthogonalized) policy shock.
Why the residual can be contaminated (reaction-function misspecification). Let the true policy rule be
div.formula: i t = ϕ ′ x t + η t ,(167)
div.p: where x t is the (possibly high-dimensional) information set relevant for policy and η t is the true exogenous policy shock. The VAR conditions on a smaller information set, typically I V AR t-1 = σ(y t-1 , . . . , y t-p ) (and, under recursive schemes, possibly some y t ordered before i t ). If x t is not well spanned by I V AR , write
div.formula: x t = Π I V AR t + v t , v t ̸ = 0,(168)
div.p: then the interest-rate residual from the VAR generally satisfies
div.formula: u t = i t -E(i t | I V AR t-1 ) = η t true policy shock + ϕ ′ v t omitted-policy-information
div.p: + specification/measurement error model error .

(169) Orthogonalizing u t with Cholesky does not remove the omitted-information component ϕ ′ v t : it may be systematic endogenous policy responding to information missing from the VAR, yet it is recorded as a "shock."
Empirical diagnostics emphasized by Rudebusch (1998). Rudebusch (1998) documents two broad failures that follow from 169:

1. Implausible and unstable estimated reaction functions. The VAR interest-rate equation often imposes a time-invariant linear rule over long samples, uses a restricted information set, relies on revised (not real-time) data, and features long distributed lags that imply predictability of short rates at horizons inconsistent with term-structure evidence.

2. Shock series that do not match market-based policy surprises. Using federal funds futures to construct unanticipated policy moves, Rudebusch finds that VAR-based policy residuals have low correlation with futures-based surprises and, moreover, policy shocks differ markedly across VAR specifications (low cross-VAR correlation).

Implication for impulse responses. A useful interpretation is that identifying a monetary shock in a VAR amounts to constructing a shock series and then tracing dynamic responses by projecting outcomes on that series. If the measured shock equals the true shock plus measurement error (or includes endogenous omitted-information components), standard errors-in-variables logic implies biased and potentially inconsistent impulse responses. Hence, the debate is not only about how to factorize Ω, but about whether the underlying interest-rate equation is a credible approximation to the central bank's reaction function and information set (Rudebusch, 1998).
32.12 Estimating Dynamic Effects of "Exogenous" Shocks: from VAR IRFs to External Instruments

Motivation. A central object in applied macroeconometrics is the dynamic causal effect of a shock: how an intervention at date t propagates to outcomes at horizons h = 0, 1, 2, . . . . In monetary economics, the question becomes: what is the effect of an exogenous monetary policy shock on output, prices, credit spreads, etc.? This subsection presents (i) the baseline reducedform VAR approach and impulse responses (IRFs) computed from in-sample dynamics, and (ii) how the modern literature seeks quasi-experimental variation through external instruments ("proxy VAR", "SVAR-IV") and local projections-IV.

div.head: Baseline: reduced-form VAR and in-sample impulse responses
div.p: Let Y t ∈ R n be a (demeaned) vector of macro/financial variables (typically stationary transformations). A reduced-form VAR(p) is
div.formula: Y t = A 1 Y t-1 + • • • + A p Y t-p + u t , E(u t ) = 0, E(u t u ′ t ) = Σ u ,(170)
div.p: where u t are innovations (Wold errors): they are linearly unpredictable given (Y t-1 , Y t-2 , . . . ).
Reduced-form IRFs are not causal. Equation 170 implies a moving-average representation
div.formula: Y t = ∞ h=0 Ψ h u t-h , Ψ 0 = I n ,(171)
div.p: so that Ψ h describes the response of Y t+h to a one-unit innovation u t . However, u t is generally a mixture of economically meaningful shocks (policy, demand, supply, information, measurement errors, . . . ). Thus Ψ h is not, by itself, the causal effect of a structural shock.
From reduced form to structural shocks. A standard structural VAR posits
div.formula: u t = B ε t , E(ε t ε ′ t ) = I n ,(172)
div.p: so that the structural impulse responses are
Identification is the problem of recovering B (or equivalently Θ 0 ) from Σ u = BB ′ using restrictions.

Internal identification: ordering and Cholesky. A common choice is recursive identification: take B as the Cholesky factor of Σ u , yielding a lower triangular impact matrix. Economically, this imposes a set of contemporaneous zero restrictions whose content depends entirely on the ordering of variables. This delivers a unique B (with positive diagonal) and hence IRFs, but credibility hinges on the plausibility of these internal restrictions.
2. Why "exogenous shocks" are hard in macro Macroeconomic shocks are rarely observed. Researchers therefore rely on:
• Internal instruments (SVAR restrictions): exclusion restrictions inside the system (recursive ordering, sign restrictions, long-run restrictions, etc.).

• External instruments (proxy shocks): variables constructed from quasi-experiments (high-frequency surprises, narrative measures, institutional discontinuities) that are correlated with the shock of interest but orthogonal to others.

A key conceptual point emphasized in the external-instrument literature is that many "shock series" are best treated as instruments rather than the true shock, because they are noisy or partial measures of the underlying structural disturbance.

div.head: Monetary policy example: high-frequency surprises (Kuttner, 2001)
div.p: A canonical approach to monetary policy shocks exploits the fact that, in short windows around FOMC announcements, movements in futures rates largely reflect unexpected policy news rather than slower-moving macro shocks. (Kuttner, 2001) uses Fed funds futures to decompose target rate changes into expected and unexpected components, showing that market rates react strongly to the surprise component while reacting little to the anticipated component. In particular, Kuttner constructs a daily "unexpected" target-rate change using changes in spot-month futures rates, scaled by remaining days in the month (to undo the contract's averaging feature), and discusses important timing/frictions and end-of-month issues in implementing the measure.

Interpreted through the lens of modern macro identification, these futures-based surprises provide external variation related to monetary policy: they are correlated with the policy shock and (arguably) orthogonal to other contemporaneous structural shocks because of the narrow announcement window.
4. External instruments: LP-IV (direct) and SVAR-IV (two-step)
Structural moving-average and causal IRFs. Let the economy admit a structural MA representation
div.formula: Y t = Θ(L)ε t = ∞ h=0 Θ h ε t-h , E(ε t ε ′ t ) = I,(174)
div.p: where Θ h are the causal impulse responses (dynamic causal effects). Suppose the shock of interest is ε 1,t . Because ε 1,t is unobserved, its scale must be normalised.
A convenient choice is the unit-effect normalisation:
i.e. a unit shock raises its indicator variable Y 1,t by one unit on impact.

div.head: LP-IV (local projections with an external instrument)
div.p: For each horizon h ≥ 0, consider the (direct) projection
div.formula: Y i,t+h = β h,i Y 1,t + γ ′ h,i W t + e i,t+h ,(176)
div.p: where W t are controls (often lags of Y t , and sometimes lags of the instrument). Because Y 1,t is endogenous, OLS is not causal. Let Z t be an external instrument (e.g. a high-frequency monetary surprise).
Instrument conditions (LP-IV). The LP-IV estimand relies on:
div.formula: 1. Relevance: E(Z t ε 1,t ) ̸ = 0.
div.p: 2. Contemporaneous exogeneity: E(Z t ε j,t ) = 0 for j ̸ = 1.
Lead-lag exogeneity: E(Z t ε j,t+ℓ ) = 0 for all j and all ℓ ̸ = 0 (possibly after conditioning on W t ).

The third condition is the distinctive macro difficulty: because Y i,t+h depends on the whole history of shocks, the instrument must not "pick up" past shocks (unless controls remove that dependence). This is the central practical issue when arguing that an empirical shock proxy is "as-if random".

Estimation and inference. Equation 176 is estimated by 2SLS for each h, using Z t as an instrument for Y 1,t , with HAC standard errors because the h-step-ahead regression error is serially correlated. LP-IV is flexible (no parametric restrictions across horizons) but can be noisy at long horizons, and demands careful instrument diagnostics (weak-IV and serial correlation).
4.2. SVAR-IV (proxy VAR): using an instrument to identify a VAR shock

An alternative is to keep the VAR dynamics for efficiency and use the instrument only to identify the impact vector. Let u t be the VAR residuals from 170. Under a structural representation u t = Bε t , an external instrument that is correlated with ε 1,t and orthogonal to the other contemporaneous shocks implies moment restrictions of the form
so that (up to scale) one identifies the first column B •1 , i.e. the contemporaneous effect of shock 1 on all variables. Combining this with a normalisation (e.g. 175) yields B •1 , and then
where Ψ h are the estimated reduced-form MA coefficients from the VAR.

div.head: python: pandas exercises wrap-up, main takeaways
div.p: To wrap upfoot_48 the exercises we just had in pandas, here is an example. Let's create two related time series:
Where here we write ẋ(t) = x(t)-x(t-1) x(t-1) , for each time series, the level will be of the form x(t) = [1 + ẋ(t)] x(t -1).

div.head: ADF test
div.p: Following section 24.7, we perform an Augmented Dickey-Fuller test.

div.head: Cointegration test
div.p: Following section 31.3.1 we test whether the time series are cointegrated (in level).

div.head: Work with a structural VAR
div.p: Structural Vector Autoregression (SVAR) is a type of VAR that considers economic theory by imposing certain restrictions to identify structural shocks in the system, which allows for a causal interpretation of shocks to the variables in the system. Following section 32.4, we create a SVAR where we impose that it is a lower triangular matrix and plot the IRF. In the 'statsmodels' Python package, SVAR models can be utilized and specified by providing particular structural matrices. The SVAR model is represented as follows, writing df (t) = [ ẋ(t), ẏ(t)]:
div.formula: A df t = constant + 2x2 matrix df t-1 + u t(180)
div.p: In a two-variable SVAR (df t has length 2), let's consider imposing restrictions on matrix A. For example, we may specify:
Here, E represents a free parameter to be estimated and can be denoted as a specific string when defining A in Python.

The matrix A is utilized to identify the SVAR model and, together with the data, enables the extraction of structural shocks u t which have a causal interpretation. By restricting A to be lower triangular (for example), we are assuming that while a shock to the first variable ẋ(t) can affect the second variable ẏ(t) contemporaneously, the reverse is not true. This kind of causal ordering can provide interpretable impulse responses and variance decompositions.

div.head: An Inflation Panel for Empirical Macroeconomics
div.p: This section introduces a simple yet instructive panel of monthly inflation data, combining harmonised euro-area inflation series with national inflation data for Ukraine. The objective is to illustrate how economists construct comparable macroeconomic time series from heterogeneous official sources and to clarify the conceptual meaning of inflation measures used in empirical work.

All data construction steps, transformations, and replication codes used in this section are fully documented and publicly available in the accompanying GitHub repository: https://github.com/skimeur/pioneer-detection-method. The repository ensures transparency and reproducibility, and allows readers to replicate the figures and extend the analysis to alternative countries, inflation measures, or sample periods.

div.head: What is inflation?
div.p: Inflation measures the rate at which the general level of consumer prices increases over time, thereby eroding the purchasing power of money. Empirically, inflation is derived from a consumer price index (CPI), which aggregates prices of a representative basket of goods and services consumed by households.
