Finding that the effect of unions (for short) estimated using conditional quantile regressions is smaller at the 90th than at the 10th quantile simply means that unions reduce within-group dispersion, where the 'group' consists of workers who share the same values of the covariates X (other than union status).

The basic idea is that for any given forecast ensemble there is a "best" model, or member, but we do not know what it is, and our uncertainty about the best member is quantified by BMA. Writing f k a bias-corrected forecast and g k (y|f k ) the conditional pdf of y conditional on f k , given that f k is the best forecast in the ensemble. The BMA predictive model is

where w k is the posterior probability of forecast k being the best one and is based on forecast k's performance in the training period. They can be seen as weights summing to unity and can be estimated using maximum likelihood methods with the EM algorithm).

div.head: Vincentization
div.p: Vincentization consists of averaging the α per cent quantiles of the experts' distributions in order to construct the α per cent quantile of the consensus, 0 < α < 1. This is q i is the α per cent quantile of f i , F i (q i ) = α, the consensual distribution F would be defined by setting

, with arbitrary h j (e.g. 1 m ). Nota bene: the quantile of a Gamma distributed random variable is the ratio of the inverse of the lower incomplete gamma function and the rate parameter of the Gamma distribution.

div.head: Ranking methods
div.p: In our set up, n items have to be ranked by m experts and the decision maker D assign weights (q vector of weights) to experts to form his final (subjective) ranking (r is D's ranking vector). In matrix notation, if π i,j is the valuation of expert j on item i, then the decision maker ranking r is:

The counting method assigns scores proportional to the totals of the valuations: q = 1 m . The invariant method can be called the eigenvalue methods, it chooses r so that r is an eigenvector of π, i.e. r = λπr, for some positive λ. It can be called Liebowitz-Palmer (LP) method. The Perron-Frobenius theorem states that if the matrix π has positive elements (∀i, jπ i,j > 0) then such an eigenvector exists and is unique up to a scalar. The hyperlink-induced topic search method (HITS method) is such that r = λππ ′ r, whereπ ′ is the transpose of π. Demange (2014) introduces a handicap-based ranking method which is the only method to be intensity invariant, homogeneous, and uniform. The handicap-based method is highly correlated with the invariant and HITS methods. D assigns weight w to experts and scores h to the periods (handicap). Experts whose statements have a high correlation with the handicaps receive a lower

div.head: R: Panel Data -Basics
div.p: The following is greatly inspired by the work of (Hurlin, 1999).

div.head: Homogeneity tests
div.p: We assume that we have N individual processes i that span over a time horizon T . We can first assume that we have the following unrestricted model:

The reason to work with panel analysis on data, is to assume that the data generation processes for the individuals have some homogeneity. If there are completely heterogeneous, then panel data analysis has no sense. In other words, we will apply some homogeneity tests to our data, in order to check whether there is some homogeneity in α or β.
71.1.1 Homogeneity test 1:
We test with a Fisher statistics: H 1 0 : β i = β and α i = α, ∀i If we accept H 1 0 , then we need to estimate the fully restricted model, the panel is homogeneous:
The test statistics is:
With:
• N : the number of individuals • K: the dimension of β
• SCR 1,c : the sum of squared residual of the equation of the constrained equation 469
• SCR 1 : the sum of squared residual of the equation of the unconstrained equation 468
If we accept the fully restricted model, this is quite powerful in terms of implications. For each process i we have the same sensitivity and effects.
If we reject H 1 0 , then we move on to the next test:
We test with a Fisher statistics: H 2 0 : β i = β, ∀i The test statistics is:
With: We test with a Fisher statistics: H 3 0 : α i = α, ∀i The test statistics is:
If we reject H 3 0 , then we need to estimate the fixed effects model:
The α i capture the effects of each process i which is constant over time.
In the case where we would reject H 2 0 and not reject H 3 0 , then we do not have a model for this panel but N models.

div.head: Between estimator
div.p: Within refers to the variation within one individual (over time), between measures the variation between the individuals. The variance of the dependent variable may be decomposed into the sum of variance within and the variance between. We obtain the Between estimator with an ordinary least square (OLS) on the following:
With the bar operator the average over the time period for each country. We obtain: Variable Estimate Constant -341.55 unemployment 80.95 inflation 59.39

div.head: Random effects
div.p: The random effect estimator can be understood as an efficient combination of within and between dimencions 220 .
In a random effect model, in which the variation across entities is assumed to be random and uncorrelated with the predictor or independent variables included in the model:
With:
• ν it the between-entity error
• ϵ it the within-entity error

div.head: Economic approach
div.p: If individuals i are randomly selected from a pool, we might want to apply random effect. If individuals i are countries, which make each unique and particular, we might want to stick to fixed effect, as we can consider that each country has unobserved characteristics.

div.head: Econometric test approach
div.p: We can perform various econometric tests to help the decision making between several models. Hausman test between the OLS and the fixed-effect model. Hausman test between the fixedeffect and the random-effect model. Hausman test to select or not time-fixed effect.
Based on the tests results, we estimate the fixed-effects model:
72.6 Generalized Least Square

div.head: Betweenness Centrality
div.p: The betweenness centrality is a measure of centrality that can be applied on directed and weighted networks, based on shortest paths between nodes. We define by δ i,j the minimum length of any path connecting i and j, we denote by σ ij the number of shortest paths from i to j and σ ij (k) the number of shortest paths from i to j where k is on the path. The betweenness centrality of a node k is defined as:

Betweenness Centrality measures node's importance as link in the shortest connection between two nodes. Regarding this measure in financial networks, we could assimilate an agent with high score to an agent which may have high probability to amplify shocks (concept of financial hub). We have to note that an agent could have a low betweenness centrality score and still be a threat to a network, one could think of a heavy and isolated node with a unique link to a central node.
We followed (Brandes, 2001) for the implementation of the algorithm. Take the simple example of an exposure matrix:
We can identify the following paths:

In presence of a cycle in the network there is an infinite number of reverberations of the impact of a node to the others and back to itself, which leads to no simple and measurable economic interpretation. DebtRank overcomes this problem by only allowing for walks that do not visit the same edge twice.

The authors develop a measure inspired by the centrality measures. The method allows to measure impact not limited to those cases in which the capital buffer is exhausted, but where partial stress on solvency is spread through the system. The measure is captured by the individual cumulative relative equity loss in time:
and the leverage of bank i toward bank j at time t:
The dynamic is defined as:
where j|s j (t -1) = D, meaning we sum over the distressed nodes:
The distressed induced in the system is then weighed by the relative exposures:

In the updated version of the algorithm (Bardoscia et al., 2015), the author show that when the modulus of the larger eigenvalue of the leverage matrix L is smaller than one, the dynamics converges to the fixed point ∆h = 0. On the contrary, if there exists an eigenvalue of the leverage matrix greater than one, the initial shock will be amplified and at least one bank will default.
In practice, in this paper, we implement three DebtRank algorithm:
1. DebtRank_rec: an algorithm based on a simple matrix multiplication as detailed in equation 495
2. DebtRank_dyn: the algorithm based on the original DebtRank paper of 2012 3. DebtRank_new: the algorithm updated by the 2015 paper

75.12 Google's PageRank (1998) applied to a bank network
For this section we use the code PageRank_illustration_vansteenberghe.py

div.head: See a Page as a bank
div.p: For illustration purpose, think instead of a web page as of a bank. Each bank is lending money to another bank, hence they are linked. A central and first question we want to answer as a financial supervisor is: "who are the 'central' banks of our network?" Each bank points to another bank by lending, each web page points to another web page with hyperlinks.
In this visual example, the thickness of the arrow represent how much a bank lend to another.
Here we can see that some bank do not lend to some others. We would like to be able to know from this set up which bank is the most "important" one to the network.

div.head: Variables definitions
div.p: • N banks (or node)
• p j the total assets of bank j
• p i,j payment due from bank i to bank j
• Π the relative liabilities matrix

76 R: Before and after regressing financial time series -some steps

div.head: Example: exercise on the CAC40 and the Dow Jones
div.p: We suggest an example 225 inspired by the book (Bazen and Sabatier, 2007). We download the 'Close' value for the CAC 40 and the Dow Jones indexes from 1995-01- We also apply the DF-GLS version of the test introduced by (Elliott et al., 1996) which has higher power than the augmented Dickey-Fuller test when the autoregressive root is large but less than one.

div.head: Cointegration test
div.p: We do a cointegration test on the two series and find that the two time series are not cointegrated.

div.head: Are both series I(1)?
div.p: We apply the first difference to the log of both index and we find that both series were I(1).

div.head: A first regression model
div.p: Both time series have means different than 0 hence we need to add an intercept in our regression model. We use the model and find a R 2 of 13%:

In a Durbin-Watson test, the null hypothesis H0 is that the autocorrelation of the disturbances (or error terms of our regression is 0). As the p-value in this case is low, we reject H0 and suspect autocorrelation in the model error terms. Note that if there is a lagged dependent variable, we have to use a Breusch-Godfrey test instead as in section 76.7. our statistics are below the critical values and we fail to reject H0: the time series has a unit root 3. test the statistic LM = nR 2 against the threshold χ 2 0.05 (p), at the order 2, the threshold is 5.99
The regression 502 has a Breusch Godfrey test statistic below 5.99, hence we accept both the hypothesise that there are neither first nor second order autocorrelation.

div.head: Gauss-Markov hypotheses
div.p: Underlying our linear regression, we assume the Gauss-Markov hypotheses for our Ordinary Least Square estimate. We follow (Verbeek, 2017):
Assuming our error terms are normally distributed, we can apply a one-sample t-test that use the following statistics:
we want to test if the null hypothesis H 0 : u t = µ with u t the mean of the residuals. In its two-sided form, the alternative hypothesis is:
76.8.2 Error terms and explanatory variables are independent u t and X t are independent, where X t is the vector of explanatory variables.

div.head: Homoskedasticity
div.p: By homoskedasticity we mean that the conditional variance of the error term is constant over time and not correlated with the explanatory variable: var(u t ) = σ 2 . We apply the Breusch-Pagan test with H 0 : homoskedasticity, which is based on the following regression, testing for the significance of the parameter β:
We check whether error terms are uncorrelated with a Durbin-Watson or Breusch-Godfrey test seen above.

check if those hypotheses are respected with our data set. If those hypotheses are respected, the OLS estimator is then BLUE (best linear unbiased estimator). Is the estimator biased if we have heteroskedasticity?

76.9 Normality test: Shapiro-Wilk test

We perform the Shapiro-Wilk test on both series returns to test for their normality. The null hypothesis H 0 is that the sample of returns came from a normally distributed population. The normality of the returns is rejected.

div.head: Scaling the returns
div.p: We can rescale the returns, for example with:
This would not change our conclusion on the normality of the distribution of return, although sometimes one call this "normalizing" the returns.

div.head: Regression residuals -test the presence of ARCH
div.p: We now follow (Bazen and Sabatier, 2007) and apply an ARCH(1) model on the errors terms of the model described by equation 502. We start witht the ARCH(1) model introduced section 30.13.

div.head: Test for ARCH
div.p: First we use the Robert Engle method to test the presence of an ARCH process. From the regression 502 we did, we use the residuals u t and regress as in equation ??. Then we use the R 2 of that regression and the length of the time series (here 1062 days) and compare it to the threshold 3.94. We find a R 2 = 12% and we have T = 1062 the length of our time series. Hence our test statistic is above the threshold: 12.5 > 3.94, therefore the null hypothesis H 0 : β = 0 is rejected and we are facing an ARCH process.

div.head: ARCH model of the residuals -PACF
div.p: We use a PACF of the squared residual to determine the order m of the ARCH process. Visually we decide to select an ARCH(4): The ARCH model standardized residuals should show less clustering issues than the original time series. This will be further explored in section 84.2. We assume that we have autocorrelated financial returns (after a Ljung-Box test):
where

) and σ t are F t-1 -measurable and (F t ) t is the natural filtration 230 of the process (R t ) t . (Z t ) t is a time series of iid random variable of unknown distribution that we call the innovations process and has zero mean 231 . We now suppose that the process (µ t ) t is an autoregressive model of order p, p ∈ N, noted AR(p) defined as
In many cases in finance we simply use an AR(1):
We model the process (σ 2 t ) t as an GARCH(p, q), after testing for the ARCH effect, defined as:

where 232 α i ≥ 0, β i ≥ 0, ∀i and p i=1 α i + q j=1 β j < 1. It is the stationarity condition of second order, i.e. the non-conditional variance is constant and finite. This is a generalization of the ARCH (Auto Regressive Conditionnal Heteroskedasticity) model introduced by (Engle, 1982a) 233 . In finance, the GARCH(1, 1) model is often used:

code: ARMA_GARCH_vansteenberghe in simple terms, the filtration contains information up to time t that is useful to determine Rt and unit variance if we standardize them in R rugarch package, they defined "ar1" as θ and "omega" as α0 once estimated, a Ljung-Box(see section 25.1 for more details) test can check the residuals independence, to verify that the heteroskedasticity has been correctly modelled. Models can be compared and chosen with AIC, BIC or likelihood ratios.
Thus the returns are modelled by the following equation system known as the AR(1) -GARCH(1, 1):
It is not advised to calibrate first an ARMA on the data and then a GARCH and this first model residuals. Indeed, when first estimating the ARMA model, you would assume conditional homoskedasticity.

We present a method of pseudo maximum liquelihood to estimate the parameters of the AR(1)-GARCH(1, 1) model. We assume that we have a data set of size n + 1; R 0 , ..., R n and notice that the joint density of the random variable R 0 , ..., R n can be written as:
As the marginal density f R 0 in the equation 510 is unknown, we compute the conditional density knowing R 0 and we have:
Furthermore, as σ t depends on σ t-1 , we need an initial value for σ 0 and we compute the conditional density knowing R 0 and σ 0 writing:

Thus the probability density f Rt|R t-1 ,...,R 0 ,σ 0 only depend on the past via the value σ t , that is obtained by a recursive approach from σ 0 , R 0 , ..., R t-1 using that σ 2 t = α 0 + α 1 (R t-1 -µ t-1 ) 2 + βσ 2 t-1 . We consider the conditional likelihood function defined as:

where g is the unknown probability density of the iid random variables (Z t ) t . In practice, it is often supposed that g is the density of a standard normal law or a t-Student with an unknown degree of freedom ν. In finance, the true law for Z t is seldom normal nor t-Student but (Gourieroux et al., 1984) demonstrate that under various regularity conditions, the maximum pseudo-likelihood estimators converge and are asymptotically normal.

div.head: Fit an AR(1)-GARCH(1,1) to the Dow Jones and CAC 40 index returns
div.p: We fit an AR(1)-GARCH (1,1) to the Dow Jones and CAC 40 index returns, and find for both α 0 and θ not significantly different than 0. The standardized innovations seems freed of clusters: We can test whether the standardized residuals (the innovations) are iid.

The above model can be applied to a univariate time series sample, in practice, we are dealing with multiple time series and we need to model the evolution of their correlation over time as empirically observed in section 84.1.2 with a multivariate volatility model. We use the Dynamic conditional correlations model.

div.head: Binary choice models
div.p: Suppose as in (Verbeek, 2017) we want to explain 235 whether a credit card customer default on its loan or not. Let the sole explanatory variable be the client income. We have data on N agents (i = 1, . . . , N ), with observations on their income, x i,2 , and whether or not they default. This latter element is described by the binary variable y i , defined as
Suppose we were to use a regression model to explain y i fromx i2 and an intercept term (x i,1 = 1). This linear model would be given by
It seems reasonable to make the standard assumption that E (ϵ

Thus, the linear model implies that x ′ i β is a probability and should therefore lie between 0 and 1. This is only possible if the x i values are bounded and if certain restrictions on β are satisfied. Usually this is hard to achieve in practice. In addition to this fundamental problem, the error term ϵ i has a highly non-normal distribution and suffers from heteroskedasticity. Because y i has only two possible outcomes (0 or 1), the error term, for a given value of x i , has two possible outcomes as well. In particular, the distribution of ϵ i can be summarized as
This implies that the variance of the error term is not constant but dependent upon the explanatory variables according to V (ϵ
Note that the error variance also depends upon the model parameters β.

In our case, the maximum M is between .1 and .2, hence this indicates that to invalidate the conclusion of a positive effect, we would need to allow for a post-treatment violation of parallel trends 10% larger than the maximal pre-treatment violation. The picture is much different if we focus only on the first post-treatment period (which is the default configuraiton of the HonestDiD package). It is unclear to me why such gap exists between a single period conclusion and the global conclusion.

div.head: Interpretation
div.p: We run the specification replicating the first row of Table 5 in Table 14 with standard error clustered at places of birth:
The DiD estimator is composed of two elements:
1. the effect on adult income of a given childhood malaria burden 2. the magnitude of decline of the malaria burden following the eradication campaigns

The second element can be estimated to be 30% in the United-States, which then allows to approximate the effect on adult income of childhood malaria exposure in units of infection rates. We find an income effect per probability of childhood infection between 46 and 49% for the United States which is close to the 47 and 60% found in the original paper (in the original paper additional controls are used). As in the paper:
Being infected with malaria through childhood leads to a reduction in adult income of approximately 50 percent.

div.head: R: Introduction to GMM
div.p: For this introduction to the Generalized Method of Moments, we follow the work of (Hamilton, 1994), (Verbeek, 2017), (Cochrane, 2005) and the package and examples suggest by (Chausse, 2010) and suggest the codes GMM_Jondeau_test.R and GMM_and_CAPM.R with examples on CAPM and intertemporal CAPM tests.

div.head: Introduction on the moments of a distribution: normality test
div.p: If we consider that we have T observations of a random variable r t , we might want to test if as for a normal law its skewness and excess kurtosis 244 are nil. As in (Jondeau et al., 2007) we define the four moments 245

We are searching for estimate of the vector θ = [µ σ s κ] ′ of dimension N that satisfy the equalities above. Writing the vector-valued function h(θ, r t ) = [e 1 e 2 e 3 e 4 ] ′ and the sample average g(θ, r T ) = 1 T T t=1 h(θ, r t ), we are searching for θ that minimizes the criterion function g ′ W g, where W is a N ×N positive definite symmetric weighing matrix reflecting the importance of matching each moment. If Γ ν is the auto-covariance matrix of the vector-valued function 246 we can define S = ν=+∞ ν=-∞ Γ ν , it can be demonstrated that S is the asymptotic variance of the sample mean S = lim T →∞ T E ([g(θ 0 , r T )][g(θ 0 , r T )] ′ ) and that the optimal weighing matrix is S -1 .

In practice, we start with an arbitrary weighing matrix, the identity I, this leads to a first estimate θa , from which can be estimated Ŝ-1 a , this is then taken as the new weighing matrix to establish a new estimate θb , until convergence. As a numerical illustration 247 , we follow the test suggested in (Richardson and Smith, 1993) following the steps and the data set in (Jondeau et al., 2007) based on GMM and a Wald test, for which the null hypothesis of normality consist of nil skewness and excess kurtosis: H 0 : s = κ -3 = 0, which can be written in matrix form with the constraint matrix Φ:
The test has the Wald statistic that under the null hypothesis is distributed as a χ 2 (2):

We use the data set suggested in (Jondeau et al., 2007), the raw version of the gmm function is not converging and we have to resort to defining lower and upper bound for the moments, as if kurtosis is κ, then excess kurtosis is κ -3 the following are also called orthogonality conditions Γν = E[h(θ0, rt)][h(θ0, rt-ν )] ′ with θ0 the true value of the moments R code: GMM_Jondeau_test

div.head: Working-Leser model
div.p: Working (1943) and Leser (1963) introduced a model for consumption analysis when survey data is available, they assume:
where ϵ i are random disturbance terms and H k are fixed effects (information on the agents like age, family size, etc.).

and from the main assumption from Working-Leser, equation 548 that ∂w i ∂M = β i M . Rearranging, we find an expression income elasticity:
by definition the marginal share is w i η i , which here is w i + β i , which describe how a one unit increase in expenditure decomposes into each good i.
Without detailed survey, we have to work with aggregated data sets.

div.head: Linear Expenditure Systems
div.p: Klein and Rubin (1947) introduced a Linear Expenditure Systems (LES) that was estimated by Stone (1954). Now agents, to maximize their utility U (q 1 , . . . , q n ), consume n j=1 p j γ j for subsistence with a budget constraint of total "income" M = n i=1 p i q i . The following assumption is made on the utility:
with q i > γ i and n i=1 β i = 1, we write the Lagrangian of the consumer maximizing his utility:
The first order conditions, ∂. ∂q i = 0 yield:
summing over i's and using the assumption that n i=1 β i = 1:
This yields the demand function:
where q i > γ i . Using equation 555 to express q i as a function of M and deriving using logarithmic differentiation:

(556) thus we find that in this model, β i w -1 i is the income elasticity and 0 < β i < 1 is the i th marginal share, it represents the fraction of a one-dollar rise in of supernumerary 249 income that will be spent on good i. Commodities with income elasticities less than unity are called necessities, those with elasticities greater than one are called luxuries. The model, to be estimated econometrically, rewrites:

where α i = n j=1 (δ ij -β i ) p j γ j , with δ ij the Kronecker delta 250 , and n i=1 α i = 0. Equation 557 estimation has been discussed (Pollak and Wales (1969), etc.) and remains a challenge, one of the main challenge lies in identifying γ j . It can be demonstrated that for this model, commodities are price inelastic and cross-price elasticities are negative (all pairs are gross complements).

div.head: Introduction on UK data set on alcohol consumption
div.p: We follow 251 Selvanathan (1995), using UK alcohol data We estimate equation 557 and retrieve the income elasticities.

div.head: The Rotterdam model
div.p: Contrary to the LES that start with the consumer's utility function, the Rotterdam model starts with the demand function q i = q i (M, p 1 , . . . , p n ) and the utility function doesn't need to be specified (we do not use the assumptions of equation 550).

supernumerary in the sense that it is total expenditure less total committed expenditure δij = 1 if i = j, 0 otherwise code: vansteenberghe_demand_supply.R and the arithmetic average of the budget share of total alcohol Wgt = 3 i=1 wit . Selvanathan (1995) demonstrates that under some conditions, equation 562 can be rewritten into their equation 4.5:

So now the marginal share is directly estimated: θ i . Selvanathan (1995) found that a one unit increase in income results in a Θ g = 0.078 increase in spending on alcohol. And a one unit increase in expenditure on alcohol decomposes into θ 1 = .35 for beer, θ 2 = .2 for wine and θ 3 = .46 for spirits.
The main criticisms of the Rotterdam model are (cf. Clements and Gao (2015) for a discussion and implications):
• constant marginal shares: the income elasticity is inversely proportional to the corresponding budget share;
• constant Slutsky coefficients;
• degenerate preferences.

div.head: Almost Ideal Demand System
div.p: Deaton and Muellbauer (1980) introduced an Almost Ideal Demand System that satisfies the axioms of choice exactly; aggregates perfectly over consumers without invoking parallel linear Engel curves; has a functional form which is consistent with known household-budget data; is simple to estimate, largely avoiding the need for non-linear estimation; and can be used to test the restrictions of homogeneity and symmetry through linear restrictions on fixed parameters. and recommended their model to testing, extending, and improving conventional demand analysis.
The main hypothesis of this model is that there is an expenditure function c(u, p), with u the utility and p the price:
For a utility-maximizing consumer, using the Shephard's lemma:
with
and n i=1 α i = 1, n i=1 β i = 0 and n i=1 γ ij = 0, ∀j ∈ [1, n]. The income elasticity can be derived exactly as in section 82.3 is

For homogeneity, we test whether n j=1 γ ij = 0, ∀i ∈ [1, n]. For symmetry, we test whether Alley et al. (1992) review the AIDS model to study alcoholic beverage demand. We apply the AIDS model to the UK data set and again find that the spirits are a strong luxury item (elasticity greater than one). Price is correlated with both unobserved product quality and unobserved demand shocks. Hausman et al. (1994) introduced the Hausman instrument: prices in one city to instrument for prices in another. Clements and Gao (2015) show that working with differentials, the AIDS model falls back to the Rotterdam estimation equation 565. When number of products is large, estimations run into dimensionality problems, which is solved using logit demand model.

div.head: Multinomial logit demand model
div.p: We follow McFadden (1974) for a conditional logit analysis of qualitative choice behavior. An individual i choose from a set of (discrete) options J with probability s ij and receives utility U ij for choosing j:
we assume that ties occur with zero probability and that the utility can be separated into observed V and unobserved ϵ components, U ij = V ij + ϵ ij then the probability of i choosing j rewrites:
McFadden (1974) demonstrates in his Lemma 1, that if ϵ are independent and identically distributed according to a Weibull distribution (p.d.f. of e -e -ϵ ), then the probability can be written:
Three axioms are satisfied:
• Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives: the ratio of choice probabilities for j and k do not depend on an alternative l;
• Positivity: s ij > 0;
• Irrelevance of Alternative Set Effect, which is equation 570.
To identify the IIA logit, some normalization choices have to be made, usually, the utility of an outside option 0 (e.g. not buying an insurance contract at all) is set to 0:

Now if we assume that the price enters utility: V ij = β p p j + x j β + β z z ij , with x j observed attributes of good j and z ij are also observed variables and β z is a vector of random terms with zero mean. Then:
and to evaluate the price elasticity:
Where we find the usual critics that the price elasticity is increasing in it own price and that the larger the share s ij → 1, the least elastic to price agent i is.

div.head: Nested logit
div.p: IIA logit (or multinomial logit) presented section 82.7.1 has i.i.d. unobserved components ϵ. This model is poor at explaining some observed substitution between goods. The Nested Logit relax part of this assumption by grouping consumers into categories g, ϵ ij = η ig + εij . Demonstrations and derivations of this model is left out of this lecture.

In both presented logit demand model, we cite Berry and Haile (2021) for criticisms that lead to allow β and β p to vary for each consumer i, as these restrictions imply that any two goods with the same (or similar) market shares-no matter how they differ in other respects-will have the same (or similar) own-price elasticities, equilibrium markups, and cross-price elasticities with respect to any third good. These are not only strong restrictions, but properties that are contrary to economic models of differentiated products, where, for example, goods that are more similar tend to have larger cross-price elasticities. Random-coefficients logit model were introduced by Boyd and Mellman (1980) and Cardell and Dunbar (1980). We refer to pyblp documentation for the model presentation. Such model have a wide range of applications, even in insurance (Koijen and Yogo, 2016) and (Koijen and Yogo, 2022). Estimations methods were developed, e.g. Berry et al. (1995) (BLP) provide the following advantages:
• estimation is possible with only price and quantity data;
• endogeneity of prices is taken into account;
• elasticities are closer to economic analysis.

In this model, taste heterogeneity in the population is allowed and the parameters governing the distribution of this heterogeneity are estimated. To follow the last sections and compare, Miller et al. (2021) estimate a random coefficient nested logit on the U.S. beer market. Nevo (2000) provide a practitioner's guide to BLP model and estimation methods are detailed in Conlon and Gortmaker (2020). The key estimation challenge is to find instruments as prices are endogenous and no close formula is possible.
In such a model, the utility of an agent i now has random coefficients β i , α i to purchase product j out of N products:

with ξ j good j unobserved (to the econometrician) characteristics 253 which can be correlated with p. In this model, only x j are assumed exogenous. If ϵ ij are i.i.d. draw from a standard type-1 extreme value distribution, then this yield mixed 254 multinomial logit model. We further assume that there is an outside option 0 (not purchasing any good) and the indirect utility is normalized to 0, U i0 = 0.
We add some structure to the random coefficients:

with D i a d × 1 vector of demographic variables following a nonparametric distribution and ν i a parametric distribution (taken from a standard normal law). Following this assumptions, equation 572 rewrites:
with the mean utility common to all consumers: δ j = x j β + αp j + ξ j and deviation from the mean utility capturing the effects of the random coefficients:

We follow the notations in Conlon and Gortmaker (2020): with θ 1 the endogenous and exogneous linear parameter (for X 1 characteristics, typically including the price) and θ 2 nonlinear parameter (typically for a subset of X 1 characteristics) that interact with the demographic characteristics. ν being the unobserved population heterogeneity that are drawn from standard normal distribution. The market share of good j is:
where θ2 is a subset of θ 2 that governs heterogeneity and f (µ i | θ2 ) denotes the mixing distribution over the heterogeneous types i.

On the supply side, the marginal cost for the firm is assumed to follow: X 3 γ+ω with X 3 observed supply-side characteristics and ω unobserved supply-side characteristics. The firm maximizes its profit:

253 this can also be considered more generally as latent taste variation and latent product characteristics 254 mixed in the sense that the coefficient αi and βi are consumer heterogeneous which yields:
where
where ⊙ is the Hadamard product and H jk = 1 if firm k produces j, 0 otherwise. Now the price elasticities expression are not trivial and the reader can refer to Nevo (2000) page 525.
where the probability of individual i purchasing good j writes:
With this, we need to estimate the equilibrium prices that satisfy equation 577. For this, we approximate:
where we take ns draws for ν i and D i . We use the same method to approximate ∂s j ∂p k with the Morrow and Skerlos (2011) approach suggested in Conlon and Gortmaker (2020).

div.p: The minimization program that comes to mind for this approach to estimate θ = [β, Σ, Π, γ] is:
with s ij the shares predicted by the theoretical model and S ij the observed market shares. But non-linearities and number of parameters render the estimation complicated.
A Generalized Method of Moments (GMM) estimator is constructed to estimate θ,
with W a weight matrice and g(θ) a vector of moments.
θ is an instrument variable estimator if
with Z D and Z S demand and supply-side instruments. Berry et al. (1995) uses three sets of instruments:

1. observed product characteristics 2. sum of product characteristics for all models marketed by a single firm in a given market, excluding the considered model for the instrument 3. sum of product characteristics for all models in a given market, excluding the firm considered for the instrument 82.8 Discrete choice model estimations 82.8.1 Automobile prices in market equilibrium Berry et al. (1995) specify the utility of agent i for good j in a market t, in logarithm u ij = log U ij :

with y i the income of agent i. x j are observed product characteristics. ξ reflects the difficult to quantify aspects of style, prestige, reputation, and pas experience as well as quantifiable characteristics of the car no available in the data set.

div.head: python and R: copula application to risk measures
div.p: The following 256 is inspired by (Cherubini et al., 2004). The copula functions help to cope with the problems of specification of marginal univariate distribution. The copula functions model fat tails and tail dependence, separately.

div.head: Bivariate copula definition
div.p: Definition 83.1. A two-dimension copula C is a real function defined on I × I (I = [0, 1]): Sklar, 1959) wrote the theorem:
Theorem 2. Given any joint distribution F (x 1 , x 2 ) with continuous margins F 1 (x 1 ) and F 2 (x 2 ), there exists a unique copula function C : I × I → I such that:
Conversely 257 , if F 1 (x 1 ) and F 2 (x 2 ) are continuous distribution functions, then 585 defines a bivariate distribution function with marginal distributions F 1 (x 1 ) and F 2 (x 2 ).
Since copulas are joint distribution functions of standard uniforms 258 , a copula computed at F 1 (x 1 ), F 2 (x 2 ) gives a joint distribution function at (x 1 , x 2 ):
The copulas are bounded (Frechet bounds) by the minimum 259 and the maximum 260 copulas:
An using the (Sklar, 1959) theorem, on can write the Frechet-Hoeffding inequality:
Corollary 3.1. Two continuous random variables X 1 and X 2 with distribution functions F 1 and F 2 are independent r.v.s iff they have the product 261 copula:

some elements can also be found here this is important because then "much of the study of joint distribution can be reduced to the study of copulas" (Schweizer, 1991), copulas separate the marginal behaviors Fi from their dependence. from the probability integral transform, ui = P r(Xi < xi) ∈ [0, 1], ui has a uniform distribution C -(u1, u2) = max(u1 + u2 -1, 0), for perfect negative dependence C + (u1, u2) = min(u1, u2), for perfect positive dependence, also known as the comonotonic copula C ⊥ (u1, u2) = u1u2

div.head: Copulas density
div.p: When it exists, the density of a copula is defined as:

div.head: Tail dependence
div.p: Copulas are agile tools to model extreme events dependence between random variables, writing the lower tail dependence coefficient:
and the upper tail dependence coefficient:
which represents the conditional probability that variable 1 takes a value in its lower (resp. upper) tail, given that 2 takes a value in its lower (resp. upper) tail.

div.head: Examples of copulas
div.p: Some popular copulas are the Gaussian, Student t, Normal Mixture, Archimedean (such as Clayton or Gumbel), detailed table 15.

div.p: We observe realizations of random variables x 1 and x 2 , we want to estimate the copula C (F 1 (x 1 ), F 2 (x 2 )), let's take the Frank copula as an example, we want thus to find the optimal parameter θ and we think of applying maximum likelihood techniques, but we do not know the distributions F 1 nor F 2 (the marginals). If we assume that the marginals and the copula are continuous and can be described by parameters, then we can differentiate C (F 1 (x 1 ), F 2 (x 2 )) and the density is:

div.p: It can be demonstrated, (Marshall and Olkin, 1983), that the Gaussian copula (when the correlation matrix does not contain any 1 except in its diagonal) belongs to the domain of attraction of the independent copula.

We assume that the distribution function H of µ is continuous. Then √ n∥H n -H∥ ∞ converges in law to the Kolmogorov-Smirnov law µ KS that can be characterized by the distribution function

div.head: VaR uppder bound
div.p: It can be demonstrated 265 , that following a peak-over-threshold methodology then the VaR has an upper bound such that:
with

In practice, we create a grid of possible values for u i ∈ [0, 1] ∀i and select the combinations that equals α with a given tolerance 266 . We are then in a position to compute the VaR upper bound and compare it to the VaR measured with the product copula which is the one assuming independence between the random variables. We expect that V aR independent ≤ V aR upper .
265 some elements here 266 the more dense the grid and the lower the tolerance, the more precise our VaR measure will be 84 Python and R: risk measures, correlation, clusters, an introduction

We introduced the risk measure VaR section 59.1 and the Q-Q plot section 22.7. We are observing realizations x t from a random variable X, we drop the time consideration and reorder the observations so that x 1 ≤ x 2 ≤ . . . x n , we reconcile both notations writing:
where [nq] represents the integer part of nq. Intuitively, the more observations, the more precise our estimate of Q.

VaR is defined in the tail of the distribution, as for some data set the number of extreme events might be limited, extreme value theory is a tool to apply parametric methods to these data sets. As most parametric models assume that the rate of loss of assets are independently and identically normally distributed, the rate of loss time series have to be filtered before modelling the risk. We present in this section that the financial returns are not independent nor identically distributed, thus first some dynamic models have to be applied to extract independent standardized innovations 267 . Second the occurrences of extreme losses have to be model and third their dependence structure modelled before computing the risk measure VaR and performing a backtest to validate the approach.

div.head: Independently and identically normally distributed returns tests
div.p: To test for idd, we introduced the Ljung-Box test section 25.1, the BDS test section 50.1. We further test for normality, 'heavy-tailness' and correlation breaks in the following sections.

div.head: Richarson and Smith test (normality)
div.p: We test the normality of the returns, following section 81.1 based on (Richardson and Smith, 1993) following the steps in (Jondeau et al., 2007) based on GMM and a Wald test 268 . For those asset where the GMM converged 269 , we find the most asset returns satistics are above the 5.99 threshold and fail the normality test. This test focuses on the skewness (symmetry on each side of the x=0 axis) and kurtosis (fat tails).

div.head: Correlation Breakdown
div.p: We have time series of asset returns from 2006-12-01 until 2017-12-29 and we can split the data set in two periods of equal length (split at 2012-06-19). In the first half we have the direct aftermaths of the 2007-2009 financial crisis and in the second half occurs a recovery of the CAC 40 index. The average difference of the correlation between each asset returns is 8.6%, the average correlation being greater in the first half of the sample, during "turbulent time". As discussed in (H. Boyer et al., 1997), we are aware of the selection bias and our correlation should be corrected before testing.

We perform 270 a test on the correlation matrices as in (Jennrich, 1970) with the null hypothesis H 0 : both correlation matrices are equal. We strongly reject H 0 . some illustration can be found here R code: Richardson_Smith_test convergence code = 0 R code: assetmanager_correlation_matrices α 1,i and γ 1,i are both positive and significant, meaning that we tend to observe higher volatility when losses are higher and be able to predict this phenomenon. We verify that for each model the weighted Ljung-Box 273 Test on Standardized Residuals do not reject H 0 of independent distribution. We plot an example of the filtering: We follow section 77.1.1 and apply a DCC-GARCH model to our returns. As this is demanding in terms of computing power, we do an example on the most weighed assets in the portfolio: FR0000120271 (Total), FR0000120578 (Sanofi) and FR0000121014 (LVMH). We have the following unconditional correlation for the sample: FR0000120271 FR0000120578 FR0000121014 FR0000120271 1.00 0.56 0.62 FR0000120578 0.56 1.00 0.51 FR0000121014 0.62 0.51 1.00 We plot the asset prices and returns evolution over time: We will be in a position to measure the unconditional VaR of the innovations and extract the volatility in equation 84.2.1. We plot 274 the conditional correlations of the asset returns over time: The conditional correlation is rather unstable and there is no clear pattern during the 2007-2009 financial crisis. We can extract the standardized residuals from the DCC-model, the innovations, that we plot below the original returns to show how the model "filtered out" the volatility clusters: Engle and Ng, 1993) present diagnostic tests which emphasize the asymmetry of the volatility response to news (good news and bad news have different predictability for future volatility) and praise the GJR-GARCH model from (Glosten et al., 1993), defining it in its simplest form:

with the information set F t-1 , the expected return m t = E(y t |F t-1 ), the conditional variance h t = V ar(y t |F t-1 ) and the news ϵ t = y t -m t . S - t = 1 if ϵ t < 0, S - t = 0 otherwise. We can illustrate the asymmetric 275 effect, respecting the conditions 0 ≤ β < 1, 0 ≤ α < 1 and α+γ < 1 and γ > 0: Seting alpha = 0.1 and gamma = 0.5

div.head: GJR news impact curve
div.p: In the above GJR model, γ > 0 and negative news will generate more future volatility then positive news, all else being equal. (Engle and Ng, 1993) We estimate F (x) for x = u+y with u some suitably chosen high threshold and y > 0. Following assumption 1 we can write: Thus for all x = u + y with y > 0:
The equality 609 allows to obtain, when F (u) is known, some high order quantile of the underlying distribution that we interpret as the V aR. Indeed, for some q > F (u), the V aR is defined as:

To estimate the V aR, we replace ξ and β by their M L estimates and F (u) is replaced by its historical estimate n -1 N u . We chose u such that there are enough data points over u and we have for all x > u:
The confidence intervals can be estimated using the Delta method described in Appendix.
Once the threshold u has been chosen and the V aR estimated, we have to backtest the results to validate the approach or else update the chosen threshold u.

div.head: VaR Backtesting
div.p: Backtesting is a process allowing us to accept or reject a risk model, we compare the modelled V aR ex-ante with the historical loss rate observed ex-post with a statistical approach to test the validity of our measure. We write V aR t q (R t+1 ) the estimate of the V aR for the period t + 1,conditional on the information F t available at date t and for a coverage rate p = 1 -q. By definition of the conditional V aR we have:

We say that this is violation (or hit) when the observed loss rate ex-post, R t+1 , is higher than the ex-ante expected value V aR t q (R t+1 ). We write I t+1 (p) the random variable associated with a violation of the V aR t q (R t+1 ) at date t + 1: (Christoffersen, 1998) states that a V aR model is valid if the violation series (I t (p)) t=1,...,T satisfy the two properties: 85.2.2 Regression based backtest: Engle and Manganelli (2004) We consider a linear regression model linking current violations to past violations and/or past information to test the conditionaly coverage hypothesis. We write the demeaned violation process Hit t (p) = I t (p) -p and we have:

. Thus current violations are independent of past violations as long as a k = b k = 0 for all k = 1, ..., K. If a 0 = 0, the unconditional coverage hypothesis is satisfied as E Hit t+1 (p)|F t = 0 as long as a 0 = a k = b k = 0 for all k = 1, ..., K. This implies that P(I t (p) = 1) = E(I t (p)) = p. Which is the same as the unconditional coverage test in (Kupiec, 1995). Writing Φ = (a 0 , a k , ..., a K , b 1 , ..., b K ) ′ the 2K + 1 vector of the model parameters and Z the matrix of explanatory variables of the model, the Wald statistics, written DQ cc , associated with the jointn ullity of all coefficients verifies:
The p-valule of the test is given by p -value = 1 -F χ 2 (2K+1) (DQ cc ).

The optimal thresholds used oscillate in the vicinity of 1.
